blocks:
Ayahs

طه (١)
Ta Ha! (1)
مَآ أَنزَلْنَا عَلَيْكَ ٱلْقُرْءَانَ لِتَشْقَىٰٓ (٢)
We did not send down to you the Quran that you should be miserable, (2)
إِلَّا تَذْكِرَةً لِّمَن يَخْشَىٰ (٣)
but only as an admonition to him who fears [his Lord]. (3)
تَنزِيلًا مِّمَّنْ خَلَقَ ٱلْأَرْضَ وَٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ ٱلْعُلَى (٤)
A sending down [of the Revelation] from Him who created the earth and the lofty heavens (4)
ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ عَلَى ٱلْعَرْشِ ٱسْتَوَىٰ (٥)
—the All-beneficent, settled on the Throne. (5)
لَهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا وَمَا تَحْتَ ٱلثَّرَىٰ (٦)
To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth, and whatever is between them, and whatever is under the ground. (6)
وَإِن تَجْهَرْ بِٱلْقَوْلِ فَإِنَّهُۥ يَعْلَمُ ٱلسِّرَّ وَأَخْفَى (٧)
Whether you speak loudly [or in secret tones,] He indeed knows the secret and what is still more hidden. (7)
ٱللَّهُ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۖ لَهُ ٱلْأَسْمَآءُ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ (٨)
Allah—there is no god except Him—to Him belong the Best Names. (8)

Virtue of Surah Ta-Ha

Numerous narrations regarding the greatness and importance of this Surah have been recorded in Islamic sources.

We read from the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله): "Allah recited Surah Ta-Ha and Ya-Sin to the angels two thousand years before the creation of Adam. When the angels heard this part of the Quran, they said:

طوبى لامة ينزل هذا عليها، و طوبى لاجواف تحمل هذا، و طوبى لالسن تكلم بهذا:

'Blessed is the nation upon whom these Surahs are revealed, blessed are the hearts that embrace these verses, and blessed are the tongues upon which these verses flow.'" [^1] In another Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), we read:

لا تدعوا قراءة سورة طه، فان اللَّه يحبها و يحب من قرأها، و من ادمن قراءتها اعطاه اللَّه يوم القيامة كتابه بيمينه، و لم يحاسبه بما عمل فى الاسلام، و اعطى فى الآخرة من الاجر حتى يرضى:

"Do not abandon the recitation of Surah Ta-Ha, for indeed Allah loves it and loves those who recite it. Whoever continues to recite it regularly, Allah will place his record of deeds in his right hand on the Day of Resurrection, He will not hold him to account for what he did in Islam, and He will grant him so much reward in the Hereafter that he becomes well-pleased." [^2] In another Hadith from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), we read:

من قرأها اعطى يوم القيامة ثواب المهاجرين و الانصار:

"Whoever recites it will be granted the reward of the Muhajirun and the Ansar on the Day of Resurrection." [^1] Once again, we find it necessary to reiterate the truth that all these immense rewards reported from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and the Imams (علیهم السلام) for reciting the Surahs of the Quran never mean that such results are achieved by mere recitation alone. Rather, the intent is a recitation that serves as a prelude to reflection—reflection whose effects manifest in all of a person's actions and speech. If we consider the general content of this Surah, we will see that the aforementioned narrations are in perfect harmony with its themes.

Content of the Surah

"Surah Ta-Ha," according to all commentators, was revealed in Makkah. Its content is similar to other Meccan Surahs, which mostly speak of the "Origin" (Mabda') and the "Resurrection" (Ma'ad), and enumerate the results of Monotheism (Tawhid) and the miseries of Polytheism (Shirk).

In the first part: This Surah makes a brief reference to the greatness of the Quran and some of the attributes of the Majesty and Beauty of the Lord.

The second part: Which comprises more than eighty verses, speaks of the story of Musa (عليه السلام); from the time he was commissioned with prophethood, then his struggle against the tyrannical Pharaoh, and after numerous conflicts with the Pharaonic establishment and the struggle against the sorcerers and their subsequent faith, Allah miraculously drowned Pharaoh and his folk in the sea and rescued Musa and the believers.

Then it describes the incident of the calf-worship by the Children of Israel and the confrontation of Harun and Musa with them.

In the third part: There are sections regarding the Resurrection and some characteristics of the Day of Judgment.

In the fourth part: The discourse is about the Quran and its greatness.

And in the fifth part: It describes the story of Adam and Eve in Paradise, then the incident of Iblis's temptation, and finally their descent to the earth.

Ultimately, in the final section, it provides awakening advice and exhortations for all believers, much of which is addressed to the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله).

Occasion of Revelation

Numerous narrations have been recorded regarding the occasion of revelation for the first verses above. From the sum of these, it is understood that after the revelation of the Wahi (revelation) and the Quran, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) engaged in excessive worship, particularly standing in prayer to such an extent that his feet became swollen. Sometimes, to be able to continue his worship, he would shift his weight onto one foot and then the other, or stand on his heels and then on his toes. [^1] The above verses were revealed, commanding the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) not to impose such hardship and distress upon himself.

Commentary

Do Not Toil Yourself So Much

Once again, at the beginning of this Surah, we encounter the "Disconnected Letters" (Muqatta'at), which pique human curiosity (Ta-Ha).

Of course, we have sufficiently discussed the interpretation of the Disconnected Letters of the Quran at the beginning of three Surahs [^2], but here we find it necessary to add this point: it is possible that all or at least some of these Disconnected Letters possess specific meanings and concepts, just like a word that contains content.

Coincidentally, in many narrations and words of the commentators at the beginning of this Surah and Surah Ya-Sin, we come across the point that "Ta-Ha" means "O Man" (Ya Rajul). In some Arabic poetry, we also find the word "Ta-Ha" with a meaning similar to "O Man" or close to it, some of which may date back to the beginning of Islam or before it. [^1] As one knowledgeable person related to us, some Western scholars who study Islamic issues have generalized this point to all the Disconnected Letters of the Quran, believing that the Disconnected Letters at the beginning of each Surah are words with specific meanings, some of which became obsolete over time while others reached us. Otherwise, it seems unlikely that the Arab polytheists would hear the Disconnected Letters, understand nothing of them, and yet not resort to mockery and derision, whereas it is nowhere seen in history that these petty-minded excuse-seekers used the Disconnected Letters as a pretext for such a reaction.

Of course, it is difficult to accept this view generally for all the Disconnected Letters of the Quran, but it is acceptable for some, and it has been discussed in Islamic sources.

It is also noteworthy that in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), we read: "Ta-Ha is one of the names of the Prophet, and its meaning is: O seeker of the Truth, the guide to it (Ya Talib al-Haqq, al-Hadi ilayh)."

From this Hadith, it appears that "Ta-Ha" is composed of two symbolic letters: "Ta" refers to "Seeker of the Truth" (Talib al-Haqq) and "Ha" refers to "Guide to it" (Hadi ilayh). We know that the use of symbolic letters and abbreviations was common in the past and present, and is especially utilized in our era.

The final word in this regard is that the word "Ta-Ha," like "Ya-Sin," has gradually become a "proper name" for the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) over time, to the extent that the family of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) is also called "Al-e Ta-Ha" (The Family of Ta-Ha), and Imam al-Mahdi (عليه السلام) is addressed in the Nudba Supplication as "O son of Ta-Ha."

Then He says: "We have not sent down the Quran to you that you should be distressed" (ما أَنْزَلْنا عَلَيْكَ الْقُرْآنَ لِتَشْقى‌). It is true that worship and seeking proximity to the Lord through supplication are among the best of deeds, but every action has its measure; worship too has its measure. You should not impose so much upon yourself that your feet swell and your strength for preaching and Jihad diminishes.

It should be noted that "Tashqa" is from the root "Shaqawah" (misery), which is the opposite of "Sa'adah" (happiness). However, as Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: sometimes this root is used to mean "toil and hardship," and in the above verse, this is the intended meaning, as the occasions of revelation also indicate.

In the next verse, He explains the purpose of the Quran's revelation as follows: "We did not reveal the Quran except as a reminder for those who fear [God]" (إِلَّا تَذْكِرَةً لِمَنْ يَخْشى‌). The expression "Tadhkirah" (Reminder) on one hand, and "man yakhsha" (he who fears) on the other, points to an undeniable reality: "Reminder" shows that the essence of all divine teachings exists within the human soul and his nature (Fitrah), and the teachings of the Prophets make it flourish, as if they are reminding him of something.

We do not say that man previously knew all sciences and knowledge and then forgot them, and that the role of education in this world is the role of recollection (as is quoted from Plato); rather, we say that their primary essence is embedded in human nature (pay close attention).

The expression "man yakhsha" shows that until a type of sense of responsibility—which the Quran calls "Khashyah" (fear/awe)—exists in a person, he will not accept the truths. This is because the receptivity of the recipient is a condition for the flourishing of any seed or grain. In truth, this expression is similar to what we read at the beginning of Surah al-Baqarah: "A guidance for the God-fearing" (هُدىً لِلْمُتَّقِينَ).

Then He proceeds to introduce the God who is the "Sender of the Quran," so that through knowing Him, the greatness of the Quran becomes more apparent. He says: "This Quran is a revelation from Him who created the earth and the high heavens" (تَنْزِيلًا مِمَّنْ خَلَقَ الْأَرْضَ وَ السَّماواتِ الْعُلى‌). [^1] In fact, this description points to the beginning and the end of the Quran's descent: its end is the earth and its beginning is the heavens in the broad sense of the word. If the phrase "and what is between them"—as in some other verses of the Quran—is not added here, it is perhaps because the goal was to state the beginning and the end. In any case, the God whose power, management, and wisdom have encompassed the expanse of the heavens and the earth—it is evident that if He reveals a book, how rich in content and weight it must be.

He continues to introduce the Lord who revealed the Quran, saying: "He is the All-Beneficent (al-Rahman) whose grace and mercy have encompassed everything, and He is established upon the Throne" (الرَّحْمنُ عَلَى الْعَرْشِ اسْتَوى‌).

As we have previously stated in the commentary of the verse "Then He established Himself upon the Throne" (Al-A'raf: 54), "Arsh" (Throne) literally refers to something that has a roof, and sometimes it is applied to the roof itself or high-based thrones like those of monarchs.

In the story of Sulayman, we read: "Which of you can bring me her throne (Arsh)?" (An-Naml: 38).

It is obvious that Allah has neither a physical throne nor a government like that of human rulers; rather, the "Throne of Allah" refers to the entire universe, which is considered the seat of His sovereignty.

Therefore, "Established upon the Throne" is a metaphor for the Lord's dominance and His complete encompassment of the universe, and the influence of His command, decree, and management throughout the world.

Fundamentally, the word "Arsh" in Arabic and "Takht" in Persian are often metaphors for power. For example, we say "so-and-so was pulled down from the throne," meaning his power and government were ended. Or in Arabic, we say: "Thulla Arshuhu" (his throne collapsed). [^1] In any case, it is very childish if some people want to imagine a physical form for God from this expression.

Following God's "Sovereignty" over the universe, He speaks of His "Ownership":

"To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth and whatever is between them and whatever is beneath the soil" (لَهُ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَ ما فِي الْأَرْضِ وَ ما بَيْنَهُما وَ ما تَحْتَ الثَّرى‌).

"Thara" originally means moist soil. Since only the top layer of the earth dries out due to sunlight and wind, while the lower layer is usually moist, this layer is called "Thara." Thus, "what is beneath the soil" means the depths of the earth and its interior, all of which are owned by the King of Kings and the Creator of the universe.

Up to this point, three pillars of the Lord's attributes have been stated: the first pillar is "Creatorship," the second is "Sovereignty," and the third is His "Ownership."

In the next verse, He points to the fourth pillar, namely His "Omniscience," saying: "He has such scientific encompassment that if you speak aloud, He knows it, and if you hide it, He also knows it, and He is even aware of what is more hidden than the hidden" (وَ إِنْ تَجْهَرْ بِالْقَوْلِ فَإِنَّهُ يَعْلَمُ السِّرَّ وَ أَخْفى‌).

Regarding what is meant by "Akhfa" (more hidden than the secret) here, there is much discussion among commentators.

  • Some have said: "Sirr" (secret) is what a person tells another privately, and "Akhfa" is what he keeps in his heart and tells no one.

  • Some have said: "Sirr" is what a person has in his heart, and "Akhfa" is what has not yet occurred to anyone's mind, but God is aware of it.

  • Others have said: "Sirr" is an action performed secretly, and "Akhfa" is the intention held in the heart.

  • Some have said: "Sirr" refers to the secrets of people, and "Akhfa" refers to the secrets within the Pure Essence of God.

  • In a Hadith from Imam al-Baqir (عليه السلام) and al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), we read: "Sirr is what you have hidden in your heart, and Akhfa is what occurred to your mind but you have forgotten." [^1] This Hadith may point to the fact that what a person learns is entrusted to the storehouse of memory; however, sometimes a person's connection with a corner of this storehouse is severed and a state of forgetfulness occurs. Thus, if he is reminded by some means, he sees it as a completely familiar matter. Therefore, what a person has forgotten is his most hidden secret, which has been concealed in the corners of memory and whose connection has been severed temporarily or forever.

In any case, there is no obstacle to all the interpretations mentioned above being included in the broad concept of the words "Sirr" and "Akhfa."

In this way, a clear depiction of the Lord's infinite knowledge is presented, and from the sum of the above verses, a general understanding of the Sender of the Quran is obtained in the four dimensions of "Creation," "Sovereignty," "Ownership," and "Knowledge."

And perhaps it is for this reason that in the next verse He says: "He is Allah; there is no deity except Him. To Him belong the Best Names" (اللَّهُ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ لَهُ الْأَسْماءُ الْحُسْنى‌). As we said in the commentary of (Al-A'raf: 180), the expression "Al-Asma al-Husna" has appeared repeatedly both in the verses of the Quran and in books of Hadith. This expression originally means "the good names." It is obvious that all the names of the Lord are good, but since among the names and attributes of God some possess greater importance, they have been called "the Best Names."

In many narrations that have reached us from the Prophet and the Imams (علیهم السلام), we read that God has 99 names; whoever calls upon Him by these names, his prayer will be answered, and whoever enumerates (ihsa) them is of the people of Paradise. This theme is also seen in the famous Hadith sources of the Sunnis.

It seems that the meaning of "enumerating" and counting these names is "adopting the character" (takhalluq) of these attributes, not merely mentioning their words. Without doubt, if someone adopts the character of the attribute of being All-Knowing, All-Powerful, or All-Merciful and All-Forgiving and the like, and a ray of these great divine attributes shines within his being, he is both a person of Paradise and his prayer is answered (for further explanation, refer to Volume 7 of Tafsir Nemouneh, pages 25-28).

Ayahs

وَهَلْ أَتَىٰكَ حَدِيثُ مُوسَىٰٓ (٩)
Did the story of Moses come to you, (9)
إِذْ رَءَا نَارًا فَقَالَ لِأَهْلِهِ ٱمْكُثُوٓا۟ إِنِّىٓ ءَانَسْتُ نَارًا لَّعَلِّىٓ ءَاتِيكُم مِّنْهَا بِقَبَسٍ أَوْ أَجِدُ عَلَى ٱلنَّارِ هُدًى (١٠)
when he sighted a fire, and said to his family, ‘Wait! Indeed, I descry a fire! Maybe I will bring you a brand from it, or find some guidance at the fire.’ (10)
فَلَمَّآ أَتَىٰهَا نُودِىَ يَـٰمُوسَىٰٓ (١١)
So when he came to it, he was called, ‘O Moses! (11)
إِنِّىٓ أَنَا۠ رَبُّكَ فَٱخْلَعْ نَعْلَيْكَ ۖ إِنَّكَ بِٱلْوَادِ ٱلْمُقَدَّسِ طُوًى (١٢)
Indeed I am your Lord! So take off your sandals. You are indeed in the sacred valley of Tuwa. (12)
وَأَنَا ٱخْتَرْتُكَ فَٱسْتَمِعْ لِمَا يُوحَىٰٓ (١٣)
I have chosen you; so listen to what is revealed. (13)
إِنَّنِىٓ أَنَا ٱللَّهُ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّآ أَنَا۠ فَٱعْبُدْنِى وَأَقِمِ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ لِذِكْرِىٓ (١٤)
Indeed I am Allah—there is no god except Me. So worship Me, and maintain the prayer for My remembrance. (14)
إِنَّ ٱلسَّاعَةَ ءَاتِيَةٌ أَكَادُ أُخْفِيهَا لِتُجْزَىٰ كُلُّ نَفْسٍۭ بِمَا تَسْعَىٰ (١٥)
Indeed the Hour is bound to come: I will have it hidden, so that every soul may be rewarded for its endeavour. (15)
فَلَا يَصُدَّنَّكَ عَنْهَا مَن لَّا يُؤْمِنُ بِهَا وَٱتَّبَعَ هَوَىٰهُ فَتَرْدَىٰ (١٦)
So do not let yourself be distracted from it by those who do not believe in it and who follow their desires, or you will perish.’ (16)

Commentary

A Fire on the Other Side of the Desert!

From here begins the story of Moses, the great Prophet of Allah, and in more than eighty verses, important parts of his eventful life are explained. This serves as a source of comfort and solace for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and the believers who were under severe pressure from enemies in Makkah during those days.

It is so they may know that these satanic powers cannot withstand the power of Allah, and all these plots are but writing on water.

Furthermore, from this content-rich history and its instructive lessons, they may find their path in the matter of Monotheism (Tawhid), the worship of God, and the struggle against the Pharaohs and sorcerers of every age and time, as well as the struggle against internal deviations and misguided tendencies—lessons that can serve as a guide and a path-opener throughout the era of the Islamic revolution.

The collection of verses in this Surah that speak of Moses, the Children of Israel, and the people of Pharaoh can be divided into four parts:

The first part: Speaks of the beginning of the prophethood and mission of Moses and the first sparks of revelation. In other words, it discusses a short-term yet profound "school" that Moses attended in that "Sacred Valley" in that dark and lonely desert.

The second part: Speaks of the invitation of Moses and his brother Aaron to Pharaoh and his people toward the religion of Monotheism, and subsequently their conflicts with the enemies.

The third part: Discusses the departure of Moses and the Children of Israel from Egypt, how they were saved from the clutches of Pharaoh and his people, and the drowning and destruction of the latter.

The fourth part: Speaks about the sharp deviant tendency of the Children of Israel from the religion of Monotheism toward polytheism, their acceptance of Samiri's temptations, and Moses' decisive and severe struggle against this deviation.

Now we return to the verses under discussion, which pertain to the first part:

These verses state with a delicate and attractive expression: "Has the story of Moses reached you?" (وَ هَلْ أَتاكَ حَدِيثُ مُوسى‌).

Clearly, this inquiry is not to obtain information, for He is aware of all secrets; rather, it is what is known as a "rhetorical question" (Istifham Taqrizi), or in other words, an inquiry that serves as a prelude to stating an important piece of news. Just as in everyday language, when starting an important report, we say: "Have you heard the news that...?"

Then it says: "When he saw a fire [from afar] and said to his family, 'Stay here; indeed, I have perceived a fire. Perhaps I can bring you a torch from it or find guidance by the fire.'" (إِذْ رَأى‌ ناراً فَقالَ لِأَهْلِهِ امْكُثُوا إِنِّي آنَسْتُ ناراً لَعَلِّي آتِيكُمْ مِنْها بِقَبَسٍ أَوْ أَجِدُ عَلَى النَّارِ هُدىً‌).

Considering that qabas refers to a small portion of fire taken from a larger mass, and given that seeing a fire in the deserts usually indicates a group gathered around it or a flame lit on a height so travelers do not lose their way at night, and also noting that umkuthu (from the root makth) means a short pause—from the sum of these expressions, it is understood that Moses was passing through the desert with his wife and child on a dark night. It was a cold and dark night in which he had lost his way. A flame of fire from afar caught his attention. As soon as he saw this flame, he told his family to pause briefly: "I have seen a fire; let me go and bring a bit of it for you, or find the way by means of the fire or the people who are there."

In historical accounts, we read that when Moses (عليه السلام) finished his contract period with Shu'ayb in Midian, he took his wife, child, and sheep and set out from Midian toward Egypt. He lost his way; it was a dark and gloomy night. His sheep scattered in the desert. He wanted to light a fire so that he and his children could warm themselves on that cold night, but the flint would not strike a spark. In the meantime, his pregnant wife went into labor.

A storm of difficult events surrounded him. It was at this moment that a flame from afar caught his eye, but it was not fire; rather, it was Divine Light. Moses, thinking it was fire, moved toward it to find the way or take a flame. [1]

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse being discussed.

Now we hear the rest of the event from the tongue of the Quran:

"When he came to it, he was called, 'O Moses!'" (فَلَمَّا أَتاها نُودِيَ يا مُوسى‌).

"Indeed, I am your Lord, so take off your sandals. Indeed, you are in the sacred valley of Tuwa." (إِنِّي أَنَا رَبُّكَ فَاخْلَعْ نَعْلَيْكَ إِنَّكَ بِالْوادِ الْمُقَدَّسِ طُوىً‌).

From verse 30 of Surah Al-Qasas, it is understood that Moses heard this call from a tree that was there: (نُودِيَ مِنْ شاطِئِ الْوادِ الْأَيْمَنِ فِي الْبُقْعَةِ الْمُبارَكَةِ مِنَ الشَّجَرَةِ أَنْ يا مُوسى‌ إِنِّي أَنَا اللَّهُ رَبُّ الْعالَمِينَ‌). From the combination of these two expressions, it is understood that when Moses approached, he saw the fire inside the tree (commentators say it was a jujube tree). This was a clear sign that this was no ordinary fire, but rather Divine Light that not only did not burn the tree but was harmonious and familiar with it—it was the light of life!

Upon hearing this soul-nourishing call, "I am your Lord," Moses became overwhelmed with excitement, and an indescribable pleasure enveloped his entire being. Who is this speaking to me? This is my Lord, who has honored me with the word Rabbuka (your Lord), to show me that I have been nurtured in the embrace of His mercy from early childhood until now and have become ready for a great mission.

He was commanded to take off his sandals because he had stepped into a sacred land—a land where Divine Light was manifest, where he heard God's message and accepted the responsibility of prophethood. He had to step upon this land with the utmost humility and modesty; this is the reason for removing the sandals.

Therefore, the detailed discussion some commentators have engaged in regarding the removal of sandals, and the opinions they have quoted, seem redundant (of course, narrations regarding the esoteric interpretation of this verse have been cited, which we will discuss when mentioning the key points).

The expression Tuwa is either because it was the name of that land, as most commentators have said, or because Tuwa, which originally means "to fold," is a metaphor here for the fact that spiritual blessings surrounded this land from every side. For this reason, in verse 30 of Surah Al-Qasas, it is referred to as "the Blessed Spot" (الْبُقْعَةِ الْمُبارَكَةِ).

Then he heard this from the same Speaker: "And I have chosen you, so listen to what is revealed [to you]." (وَ أَنَا اخْتَرْتُكَ فَاسْتَمِعْ لِما يُوحى‌).

Following that, Moses received the first sentence of revelation in this form: "Indeed, I am Allah. There is no deity except Me." (إِنَّنِي أَنَا اللَّهُ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا أَنَا). "So, since it is so, worship Me alone—a worship pure from any kind of polytheism." (فَاعْبُدْنِي‌).

"And establish prayer for My remembrance." (وَ أَقِمِ الصَّلاةَ لِذِكْرِي‌).

In this verse, after stating the most important principle of the prophets' call, which is the issue of Monotheism, the subject of worshipping the One God is mentioned as a fruit of the tree of faith and Monotheism. Following that, the command for prayer is given—the greatest act of worship, the most important bond between the creation and the Creator, and the most effective way to not forget His Pure Essence.

These three commands, along with the decree of prophethood in the previous verse and the issue of the Hereafter in the next verse, recount a complete and condensed set of the principles and branches of religion, which is perfected in every respect by the command for steadfastness that will come in the final verses under discussion.

And since after mentioning "Monotheism" and its branches, the second fundamental principle is the issue of the "Hereafter," He adds in the next verse: "Indeed, the Hour is coming—I almost conceal it—so that every soul may be recompensed for that which it strives." (إِنَّ السَّاعَةَ آتِيَةٌ أَكادُ أُخْفِيها لِتُجْزى‌ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ بِما تَسْعى‌).

In this sentence, there are two points to be noted: First, the phrase akadu ukhfiha means "I am close to" hiding the date of the Resurrection. The implication of this expression is that I have not hidden it, whereas we know according to the explicit text of many Quranic verses that no one knows the date of the Resurrection, as we read in verse 187 of Surah Al-A'raf: "They ask you about the Hour, when is its arrival? Say, 'Its knowledge is only with my Lord.'"

Commentators have discussed the answer to this question. Many believe the above expression is a type of hyperbole, meaning that the date of the start of the Resurrection is so hidden and concealed that I am even close to hiding it from Myself. A narration has also been transmitted in this regard, and likely this group of commentators adapted their point from that narration.

Another interpretation is that the derivatives of kada do not always mean "to be close to," but sometimes come for emphasis without carrying the meaning of proximity. Therefore, some commentators have interpreted akadu as uridu (I want/intend), and this meaning is explicitly found in some lexicographical texts. [1]

Another point is that the reason for keeping the date of the Resurrection hidden, according to the above verse, is that "Allah wants to reward everyone for their efforts and struggles." In other words: by its being hidden, a kind of freedom of action is created for everyone. On the other hand, since its time is not exactly known and is possible at any moment, the result is a state of permanent readiness or the rapid acceptance of educational programs. Just as it has been said regarding the philosophy of hiding "Laylat al-Qadr" (the Night of Power), the purpose is for people to honor all nights of the year, or all nights of the holy month of Ramadan, and turn toward God.

In the last verse under discussion, He refers to a fundamental principle that guarantees the implementation of all the above ideological and educational programs, saying: "So let not one who does not believe in it and follows his desire avert you from it, lest you perish!" (فَلا يَصُدَّنَّكَ عَنْها مَنْ لا يُؤْمِنُ بِها وَ اتَّبَعَ هَواهُ فَتَرْدى‌).

Stand firm against faithless individuals and their temptations and obstructions; neither be terrified by their numbers, nor let fear of their conspiracies enter your heart, and never have any doubt or hesitation regarding the truth of your call and the authenticity of your school because of these outcries.

Interestingly, here the phrase "does not believe" (la yu'minu) is in the present/future tense, while the phrase "and followed his desire" (wattaba'a hawahu) is in the past tense. In reality, this points to the fact that the lack of faith in those who deny the Resurrection stems from following their whims. It is as if they want to be free to do whatever their hearts desire, so they find it better to deny the Resurrection so that no blemish falls upon the freedom of their passions!

Key Points

1- What is meant by "Take off your sandals"?

As we said, the literal meaning of the verse is that Moses was commanded to remove his sandals out of respect for that sacred land and to step into that valley with humility and modesty, to hear the word of Truth and receive the command of prophethood. However, some commentators, following certain narrations, say: this was because the leather of those sandals was from the skin of a dead animal! This statement, besides being unlikely in itself—as there was no reason for Moses (عليه السلام) to use such skin and contaminated sandals—has been rejected by other narrations. Specifically, a narration from the sacred presence of Imam al-Mahdi (may our souls be sacrificed for him) strongly negates this interpretation. [1] In the current Torah, Exodus Chapter 3, the same expression found in the Quran is also seen.

Other narrations that point to the esoteric interpretation (ta'wil) and inner meanings of the verse say: "Take off your sandals" means your two fears: your fear for the loss of your family and your fear of Pharaoh! [2]

[1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 373. [2]: Ibid, p. 374.

In another hadith from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), an interesting point is narrated regarding this phase of Moses' life, where he says: "Be more hopeful for what you do not expect than for what you do expect; for Moses son of Imran went out to fetch a flame of fire for his family, but returned to them as a Messenger Prophet!" [1] This indicates that often a person hopes for something but does not reach it, yet more important things for which he had no hope are provided for him by the grace of the Lord! This same meaning has also been narrated from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (عليه السلام). [2]

2- Answering an Objection

Some commentators have raised a question here: How and from where did Moses know that this voice he was hearing was from God? And how did he gain certainty that the Lord was giving him a mission?

This question, which can also be raised regarding other prophets, can be answered in two ways: First, in that state, a type of internal intuition and inner perception occurs that brings the person to complete certainty and removes any doubt or ambiguity.

Second, the beginning of revelation may be accompanied by extraordinary phenomena that are impossible except by the power of the Lord, just as Moses (عليه السلام) saw the fire within the green tree and understood from it that the matter was a Divine and miraculous one.

It is also necessary to remind that hearing the word of God, and that too without any intermediary, does not mean that God has a larynx or a voice; rather, by His perfect power, He creates sound waves in the atmosphere, and through these waves, He speaks to His prophets. Since the beginning of Moses' (عليه السلام) prophethood occurred in this manner, he was given the title Kalim-Allah (The one who spoke with Allah).

3- Prayer: The Best Means of Remembering God

In the above verses, one of the important philosophies of prayer is mentioned: that man, in the life of this world, given the "distracting factors," needs reminders and awakening—a means that, at various intervals of time, reminds him of God, the Resurrection, the call of the prophets, and the purpose of creation, and protects him from drowning in the whirlpool of negligence and ignorance. Prayer carries this important responsibility.

Man rises from sleep in the morning—a sleep that has made him a stranger to everything in this world. He wants to start his life's program; before anything else, he goes to prayer. He purifies his heart and soul with the remembrance of God, seeks strength and help from Him, and becomes ready for effort and struggle coupled with purity and honesty.

Again, when he becomes immersed in daily tasks and a few hours pass, and perhaps a gap occurs between him and the remembrance of God, suddenly it is noon and he hears the caller to prayer: Allahu Akbar! ... Hayya 'ala-s-Salah! "God is greater than everything, greater than can be described... Hasten to prayer!" He goes to prayer, stands before his Deity in intimate supplication, and if any dust of negligence has settled on his heart, he washes it away. It is here that God, in the first instructions at the beginning of revelation to Moses, says: "Establish prayer for My remembrance."

Interestingly, this verse says: "Establish prayer for My remembrance," but in verse 28 of Surah Ar-Ra'd, it says: "Verily, in the remembrance of Allah do hearts find rest" (أَلا بِذِكْرِ اللَّهِ تَطْمَئِنُّ الْقُلُوبُ‌). And in verses 27 to 30 of Surah Al-Fajr, He says: "O reassured soul, return to your Lord, well-pleased and pleasing [to Him], and enter among My [righteous] servants, and enter My Paradise!" By placing these three verses side by side, we understand well that prayer keeps man in remembrance of God, the remembrance of God gives him a reassured soul, and the reassured soul brings him to the station of special servants and eternal Paradise.

[1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 374. [2]: Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 1, p. 513.

Ayahs

‘Moses, what is that in your right hand?’ (17)
قَالَ هِىَ عَصَاىَ أَتَوَكَّؤُا۟ عَلَيْهَا وَأَهُشُّ بِهَا عَلَىٰ غَنَمِى وَلِىَ فِيهَا مَـَٔارِبُ أُخْرَىٰ (١٨)
He said, ‘It is my staff. I lean on it, and with it I beat down leaves for my sheep; and I have other uses for it.’ (18)

He said, ‘Moses, throw it down.’ (19)
فَأَلْقَىٰهَا فَإِذَا هِىَ حَيَّةٌ تَسْعَىٰ (٢٠)
So he threw it down, and behold, it was a snake, moving swiftly. (20)
قَالَ خُذْهَا وَلَا تَخَفْ ۖ سَنُعِيدُهَا سِيرَتَهَا ٱلْأُولَىٰ (٢١)
He said, ‘Take hold of it, and do not fear. We will restore it to its former state. (21)
وَٱضْمُمْ يَدَكَ إِلَىٰ جَنَاحِكَ تَخْرُجْ بَيْضَآءَ مِنْ غَيْرِ سُوٓءٍ ءَايَةً أُخْرَىٰ (٢٢)
Now clasp your hand to your armpit: it will emerge white, without any harm—[this is yet] another sign, (22)
لِنُرِيَكَ مِنْ ءَايَـٰتِنَا ٱلْكُبْرَى (٢٣)
that We may show you some of Our great signs. (23)

Commentary

The Staff of Moses and the White Hand

Undoubtedly, prophets require miracles to prove their connection with Allah; otherwise, anyone could claim prophethood. Therefore, distinguishing true prophets from false ones is not possible except through miracles. This miracle can reside within the content of the prophet’s (صلى الله عليه وآله) call and the Heavenly Book, and it can also manifest in other forms, such as sensory and physical miracles. Furthermore, a miracle has an effect on the soul of the prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) himself, granting him strength of heart, power of faith, and steadfastness.

In any case, after receiving the command of prophethood, Moses (عليه السلام) had to receive its credentials as well. Thus, on that same eventful night, Moses (عليه السلام) received two great miracles from Allah.

The Quran describes this event as follows:

Allah asked Moses: "And what is that in your right hand, O Moses?" (وَ ما تِلْكَ بِيَمِينِكَ يا مُوسى‌).

This simple question, accompanied by kindness and affection, served not only to calm Moses—who was naturally overwhelmed by spiritual storms at that moment—but also as a prelude to disclosing a great reality.

In response, Moses "said, 'It is my staff'" (قالَ هِيَ عَصايَ‌).

And since he wished to continue his conversation with his Beloved, who had opened the door to him for the first time, and perhaps because he thought merely saying "this is my staff" was insufficient—rather, the purpose was to explain its uses and benefits—he added: "I lean upon it" (أَتَوَكَّؤُا عَلَيْها).

"And I beat down leaves with it for my sheep" (وَ أَهُشُّ بِها عَلى‌ غَنَمِي‌). [^1] "And I have other uses for it as well" (وَ لِيَ فِيها مَآرِبُ أُخْرى‌). [^2] It is clear what benefits a staff holds for its owners; sometimes it is used as a means of defense against harmful animals and enemies, sometimes it is used to create a shelter in the desert, and sometimes a vessel is tied to it to draw water from a deep river.

In any case, Moses was immersed in deep wonder: what is this question in this Great Presence, and what answer am I giving? What were those previous commands? And what is the purpose of this inquiry?

Suddenly, "he was commanded: 'Throw it down, O Moses!'" (قالَ أَلْقِها يا مُوسى‌).

"Moses threw it down immediately and without delay; suddenly it became a massive snake and began to move" (فَأَلْقاها فَإِذا هِيَ حَيَّةٌ تَسْعى‌).

"Tas’a" (تَسْعى‌) is derived from the root "sa’y" (سعى), meaning to walk rapidly, though not reaching the stage of running.

At this point, "Moses was commanded: 'Seize it and do not fear; We shall return it to its former state!'" (قالَ خُذْها وَ لا تَخَفْ سَنُعِيدُها سِيرَتَهَا الْأُولى‌). [^1] In verse 31 of Surah Al-Qasas, we read: "He turned back retreating and did not look back. [Allah said], 'O Moses, come forward and do not fear.'" Upon witnessing that massive snake, Moses became afraid and fled; Allah said once more: "O Moses, return and do not fear."

Although the issue of Moses' fear here has raised questions for some commentators—how this state is compatible with the courage we know Moses possessed, which he practically proved throughout his life when confronting the Pharaohs, and which is a general condition for prophets—the answer becomes clear by considering one point. It is natural for any human—no matter how brave and fearless—that if they suddenly see a piece of wood turn into a massive snake and move rapidly, they would be momentarily terrified and pull away, unless the scene is repeated before them many times. This natural reaction is no flaw upon Moses and does not contradict verse 39 of Al-Ahzab, which says: "الَّذِينَ يُبَلِّغُونَ رِسالاتِ اللَّهِ وَ يَخْشَوْنَهُ وَ لا يَخْشَوْنَ أَحَداً إِلَّا اللَّهَ‌" ("Those who deliver the messages of Allah and fear Him, and do not fear anyone except Him"). This was a fleeting and temporary natural dread in the face of a completely unprecedented and extraordinary event.

Then, He refers to the second important miracle of Moses and commands him: "And draw your hand to your side; it will come out white and shining, without any disease—another miracle" (وَ اضْمُمْ يَدَكَ إِلى‌ جَناحِكَ تَخْرُجْ بَيْضاءَ مِنْ غَيْرِ سُوءٍ آيَةً أُخْرى‌). [^1] Although commentators have various interpretations for the phrase "وَ اضْمُمْ يَدَكَ إِلى‌ جَناحِكَ", by looking at verse 32 of Surah Al-Qasas which says: "اسْلُكْ يَدَكَ فِي جَيْبِكَ" and verse 12 of Surah Al-Naml which says: "وَ أَدْخِلْ يَدَكَ فِي جَيْبِكَ", it is clearly understood that Moses was commanded to put his hand into his collar and reach it down to his armpit or side (since "janah" originally means the wing of birds and here can be a metaphor for the armpit).

"Bayda" (بيضاء) means white, and the phrase "مِنْ غَيْرِ سُوءٍ" (without any ill) indicates that the whiteness of your hand will not be due to leprosy or similar diseases, because it possesses a special radiance; it appears in one moment and disappears in another.

However, it is understood from some narrations that Moses' hand, in that state, acquired an extraordinary luminosity. If this was the case, we must accept that the phrase "مِنْ غَيْرِ سُوءٍ" has a meaning other than what was mentioned above—meaning it has a luminosity without defect, neither hurting the eye nor containing any dark spots or otherwise.

In the final verse under discussion, as a conclusion to what was stated in the previous verses, He says: "We have provided these to you so that We may show you some of Our greatest signs" (لِنُرِيَكَ مِنْ آياتِنَا الْكُبْرى‌).

It is evident that "the greatest signs" refers to the two important miracles mentioned above. The possibility suggested by some commentators that it refers to other miracles Allah later placed at Moses' disposal seems very unlikely.

Key Points

1- Two Great Miracles:

Undoubtedly, what was mentioned regarding the transformation of Moses' staff into a massive snake—which is even referred to as "thuban" (dragon/serpent) in verse 107 of Surah Al-A'raf—and also the specific radiance of the hand for a brief moment and then returning to its original state, is not an ordinary or rare occurrence. Rather, both are considered extraordinary (miraculous) events that are impossible without reliance on a supra-human power, namely the power of the Great Allah. Those who believe in Allah and consider His knowledge and power to be infinite can never deny these matters or attribute them to superstition as materialists do.

What is important in a miracle is that it must not be intellectually impossible, and in this case, this holds true. No rational proof exists to deny the possibility of a staff turning into a massive snake.

Have not both the staff and the massive snake been derived from the earth in the distant past? Of course, it perhaps took millions or hundreds of millions of years for such beings to emerge from the earth (and there is no difference in this matter whether we believe in the evolution of species or the fixity of species, for in any case, both the wood of trees and animals are created from the earth). However, the work of the miracle in this case was that it completed those stages, which should have been traversed over long years, in a single moment and a very short duration. Does such a thing seem impossible?

I might write a thick book by hand in one year; now, if someone appears who, relying on a miracle, performs it so quickly that it is written in an hour or less, this is not an intellectual impossibility; it is an extraordinary event (reflect on this). In any case, hasty judgment regarding miracles and—God forbid—attributing them to superstitions is far from logic and reason. The only thing that sometimes creates such thoughts is that we have become accustomed to ordinary causes and effects to the point that we perceive them as a necessity, and we consider anything contrary to them as contrary to necessity. In reality, the form of these natural and ordinary causes and effects never possesses the aspect of necessity, and there is no obstacle to a supra-natural agent creating changes within them. [^1]

2- The Extraordinary Potential of Objects:

Certainly, on the day Moses chose that shepherd's staff for himself, he did not believe that this simple object could perform such a great task by Allah's command, such that it would shatter the power of the Pharaohs. But Allah showed him that from this very simple means, such an extraordinary force could be generated.

This is, in fact, a lesson for all humans not to consider anything in this world as simple. There may be objects or individuals whom we look upon with contempt, yet they hold great powers hidden within their hearts of which we are unaware.

3- What the Torah Says on This Matter:

In the verses above, we read that when Moses (عليه السلام) brought his hand out from his collar, it was white and bright without having any defect. This phrase might be to negate the description found in the corrupted Torah, for in this Torah it is stated: "And the Lord said furthermore unto him, Put now thine hand into thy bosom. And he put his hand into his bosom: and when he took it out, behold, his hand was leprous as snow." [^1] The word "leprous" (مبروص) comes from the root "baras" (برص), meaning leprosy, which is a type of disease, and certainly, using this description in this context is wrong and inappropriate.

[^1]: "Ahushshu" (أَهُشُّ) is from the root "hashsh" (هش), meaning to strike the leaves of trees to make them fall. [^2]: "Ma'arib" (مآرب) is the plural of "ma'rabah" (مأربة), meaning need, necessity, or purpose. [^1]: "Sirah" (سيرة), as Raghib says in Al-Mufradat, means an internal state, whether instinctive or acquired. Some have also interpreted it here to mean form and appearance. [^1]: "Ayatan" (آيَةً) is in the accusative case (mansub) because it is a noun functioning as a circumstantial qualifier (hal) for the pronoun hidden in "takhruj" (تخرج). [^1]: We have also discussed this in Volume 6, page 284. [^1]: Torah, Book of Exodus, Chapter 4, Verse 6.

He said, ‘Moses, your request has been granted! (36)

Commentary

Moses’ Calculated Requests

Up to this point, Moses (peace be upon him) has reached the station of prophethood and received significant miracles. However, from here on, the command of the messengership is issued in his name—a truly grand and heavy mission. It is a mission that begins with delivering the Divine command to the most powerful and dangerous person in that environment. He says: "Go to Pharaoh, for he has transgressed!" (اذْهَبْ إِلى‌ فِرْعَوْنَ إِنَّهُ طَغى‌).

Indeed, to reform a corrupt environment and initiate a comprehensive revolution, one must start with the leaders of corruption and the "Imams of disbelief"—those who play a role in all pillars of society. They, or their ideas and supporters, are present everywhere; they have seized all propaganda, economic, and political organizations. If they are reformed, or if not reformed, uprooted, then one can hope for the salvation of society; otherwise, any reform will be superficial, temporary, and fleeting.

It is interesting that the reason for the necessity of starting with Pharaoh is stated in one short phrase: "إِنَّهُ طَغى‌" (He has transgressed). In this word "transgression" (Tughyan), everything is gathered: transgression and exceeding the limits in all dimensions of life. For this reason, such individuals are called "Taghut," which is derived from the same root.

Moses (peace be upon him) not only did not fear such a heavy mission, nor did he ask for the slightest reduction from Allah, but he welcomed it with open arms. However, he asked Allah for the means of victory in this mission. Since the first means of victory is a great spirit, high thought, and a powerful intellect—or in other words, an expansion of the chest—he "said: My Lord! Expand for me my chest" (قالَ رَبِّ اشْرَحْ لِي صَدْرِي‌).

Indeed, the first asset for a revolutionary leader is an expanded chest (Sharh al-Sadr), immense patience, steadfastness, courage, and the ability to bear the weight of difficulties. For this reason, we read in a Hadith from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (peace be upon him):

"The tool of leadership is the expansion of the chest" [1] (We have also discussed the concept of Sharh al-Sadr in Volume 5 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 436, under verse 125 of Surah Al-An'am).

And since this path has many difficulties that cannot be opened except by the grace of Allah, in the second stage, he requested Allah to make matters easy for him and remove obstacles from his path. He said: "And ease for me my task" (وَ يَسِّرْ لِي أَمْرِي‌).

Then Moses requested the power of expression to the highest degree and said: "And untie the knot from my tongue" (وَ احْلُلْ عُقْدَةً مِنْ لِسانِي‌). It is true that possessing the asset of Sharh al-Sadr is the most important asset, but this asset is effective only if there is the power to present and express it perfectly. Therefore, after requesting Sharh al-Sadr and the removal of obstacles, Moses requested that Allah remove the knot from his tongue.

He specifically stated the reason for this as: "That they may understand my speech" (يَفْقَهُوا قَوْلِي‌). This sentence, in fact, explains the previous verse. From it, it becomes clear that the purpose of "untying the knot of the tongue" was not that Moses' tongue had a physical impediment due to a burn in childhood (as some commentators have narrated from Ibn Abbas), but rather it refers to the knots in speech that prevent the listener's understanding. That is: "Let me speak so eloquently, effectively, and clearly that every listener understands my purpose well."

Another witness to this interpretation is verse 34 of Surah Al-Qasas: "And my brother Aaron, he is more eloquent than me in speech" (وَ أَخِي هارُونُ هُوَ أَفْصَحُ مِنِّي لِساناً). It is interesting that Afsah (more eloquent) is from the root Fasih, which originally means something being pure from additions, and is then applied to speech that is clear, expressive, and free from redundancy.

In any case, a successful and victorious leader is one who, in addition to breadth of thought and spiritual power, possesses an expressive speech free from any ambiguity or inadequacy.

And since carrying this heavy burden to its destination (the burden of the Lord's message, leading humanity, and fighting against tyrants and oppressors) requires a helper and cannot be done alone, the fourth request of Moses (peace be upon him) from his Lord was: "O Allah! Appoint for me a minister (helper) from my family" (وَ اجْعَلْ لِي وَزِيراً مِنْ أَهْلِي‌). "Wazir" (minister) is from the root Wizr, which originally means a heavy burden. Since ministers carry many heavy burdens in governance, they are given this name. The word Wazir is also applied to an assistant or helper.

As for why Moses (peace be upon him) requested that this minister be from his family, the reason is clear: he would have more knowledge of him and more compassion. How good it is for a person to cooperate with someone to whom they are bound by both spiritual and physical ties.

Then he specifically pointed to his brother and said: "Give this responsibility to my brother Aaron" (هارُونَ أَخِي‌). According to some commentators, Aaron was Moses' older brother, three years his senior. He had a tall stature, an eloquent tongue, and excellent understanding. He passed away three years before Moses [1]. He was among the sent Messengers, as we read in verse 45 of Surah Al-Mu'minun:

"Then We sent Moses and his brother Aaron with Our signs and a clear authority." He also possessed inner light and the means to distinguish truth from falsehood, as we read in verse 48 of Surah Al-Anbiya:

"And We gave Moses and Aaron the Criterion (al-Furqan) and a light." Finally, he was a prophet whom Allah granted to Moses out of His mercy: "And We granted him out of Our mercy his brother Aaron as a prophet" (Maryam: 53).

He worked side-by-side with his brother Moses (peace be upon him) in fulfilling this heavy mission. It is true that on that dark night in that sacred valley, when Moses made this request to Allah upon receiving the command of messengership, he had been away from his homeland for more than ten years. However, his connection with his brother had presumably not been completely severed during this time, as he speaks of him with such clarity and requests his participation in this great program from Allah's threshold.

Then Moses states his goal for appointing Aaron as a minister and assistant: "O Allah! Strengthen my back through him" (اشْدُدْ بِهِ أَزْرِي‌). "Azr" is originally derived from Izar, meaning a garment, specifically a garment tied at the waist. For this reason, the word is sometimes applied to the waist, or to strength and power.

To complete this objective, he requests: "And make him a partner in my task" (وَ أَشْرِكْهُ فِي أَمْرِي‌). Let him be a partner in the station of messengership and participate in implementing this great program. However, in any case, he was a follower of Moses in all programs, and Moses was his Imam and leader.

Finally, he states the result of his requests as: "That we may glorify You much" (كَيْ نُسَبِّحَكَ كَثِيراً).

"And remember You much" (وَ نَذْكُرَكَ كَثِيراً).

"For indeed You have always been observant of us" (إِنَّكَ كُنْتَ بِنا بَصِيراً). You know our needs well and are more aware of the difficulties of this path than anyone. We ask You to give us the power to obey Your command and to make us successful and victorious in fulfilling our duties, commitments, and responsibilities.

Since Moses, in these sincere requests, had no intention other than greater and more perfect service, Allah granted his request at that very moment: "He said: You have been granted your request, O Moses!" (قالَ قَدْ أُوتِيتَ سُؤْلَكَ يا مُوسى‌). In fact, in these sensitive and decisive moments when Moses first stepped onto the banquet of the Great Lord, he requested everything he needed at once. He, in turn, honored His guest to the utmost and granted all his requests in one short sentence with a life-giving call, without any conditions or "ifs and buts." He completed it by repeating Moses' name, which removes any ambiguity from the heart. How thrilling and pride-inducing it is for a servant's name to be repeated in the speech of the Master.

Key Points

1- Conditions for Revolutionary Leadership:

Undoubtedly, a fundamental transformation in the system of human societies and changing material and polytheistic values into spiritual and human values—especially where the path goes through the territory of Pharaohs and autocrats—is no simple task. It requires spiritual and physical readiness, power of thought and expression, clarity of the path, Divine assistance, and having a reliable and powerful helper.

These are the very things Moses asked of his Lord in his initial requests at the very beginning of his great mission. These themselves indicate that Moses (peace be upon him) had an awakened and ready spirit even before his prophethood. They also clarify the fact that he was well aware of the dimensions of his responsibility and knew what tools he must bring to the field under those specific conditions to be able to fight against Pharaonic systems. This is a model for all Divine leaders in every age and for all wayfarers on this path.

2- Fighting Against Transgressors:

Undoubtedly, Pharaoh had many points of deviation: he was a disbeliever, an idolater, an oppressor, and a tyrant... but the Quran highlights the issue of his "transgression" (إِنَّهُ طَغى‌) among all these deviations. This is because the spirit of transgression and rebellion against the command of Truth is the essence of all these deviations and encompasses them all.

Furthermore, it becomes clear that the primary goal of the Prophets is to fight against the Taghuts and the arrogant. This is exactly contrary to the analysis that Marxists have regarding religion, viewing it as being in the service of transgressors and colonialists. Their words might be true regarding fabricated, sedative religions, but the history of the true Prophets, with total clarity, 100% negates their hollow imaginings regarding heavenly religions. Specifically, the uprising of Moses son of Amram is a vivid witness in this regard.

3- Programs and Means are Necessary for Every Task:

Another lesson this episode of Moses' life teaches us is that even the Prophets, despite having all those miracles, utilized ordinary means for the progress of their work—such as eloquent and effective expression and the intellectual and physical strength of assistants. We are not meant to always wait for miracles in life; we must prepare the program and the means of work and advance through natural ways. Where things become knotted, one should then await Divine grace!

4- Glorification and Remembrance:

As we read in the above verses, Moses sets the ultimate goal of his requests as: "That we may glorify You much and remember You much." It is clear that "glorification" (Tasbih) means purifying Allah from the accusations of "shirk" (polytheism) and "contingent deficiencies." It is also clear that Moses' intention was not merely to repeat the phrase "Subhan Allah" (Glory be to Allah) repeatedly, but rather the goal was to implement its reality in the corrupt society of that time: to remove idols, destroy idol temples, wash minds of polytheistic thoughts, and remove material and spiritual deficiencies.

Following that, he sought to revive the remembrance of Allah and His attributes in hearts and to let Divine attributes shine across society. The emphasis on the word "much" (Kathira) shows that he wanted to make it universal and public, moving it beyond a limited circle.

5- The Prophet of Islam Repeats the Same Requests as Moses:

From the narrations found in the books of both Sunni and Shi'a scholars, it is understood that the Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family) also desired the same things Moses had asked of Allah to advance his goals, with the difference that instead of the name "Aaron," he placed the name "Ali" (peace be upon him), saying:

"O Allah! I ask You for what my brother Moses asked You: that You expand for me my chest, ease for me my task, untie the knot from my tongue that they may understand my speech, and appoint for me a minister from my family, Ali my brother. O Allah! Strengthen my back through him and make him a partner in my task, that we may glorify You much and remember You much, for indeed You are observant of us."

This Hadith has been narrated by al-Suyuti in the Tafsir al-Durr al-Manthur, the late Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan, and many other great Sunni and Shi'a scholars with slight variations.

Similar to this is the Hadith al-Manzila, where the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) said to Ali (peace be upon him):

"Are you not pleased to be to me as Aaron was to Moses, except that there is no prophet after me?"

This Hadith, which appears in the primary books of the Sunnis and, according to Muhaddith al-Bahrani (as cited in Tafsir al-Mizan) in the book Ghayat al-Maram, has been narrated through one hundred Sunni chains and seventy Shi'a chains, is so authentic that it cannot be denied.

We have discussed the Hadith al-Manzila sufficiently in Volume 6 of Tafsir Nemouneh under verse 142 of Surah Al-A'raf (page 339). However, what is necessary to mention here is that some commentators (like al-Alusi in Ruh al-Ma'ani), while accepting the origin of the narration, have objected to its implication, saying that the phrase "And make him a partner in my task" (وَ أَشْرِكْهُ فِي أَمْرِي‌) proves nothing except partnership in the matter of guidance and inviting people to the Truth.

But it is obvious that the matter of partnership in guidance—or in other words, enjoining the good and forbidding the evil and spreading the call of Truth—is the duty of every single Muslim. This was not something the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) would specifically ask for Ali (peace be upon him); this is an "explanation of the obvious" that the Prophet's prayer can never be interpreted as such.

On the other hand, we know that partnership in the matter of prophethood was not intended either. Therefore, we conclude that it was a specific station other than prophethood and other than the general duty of guidance. Is this anything other than the matter of "Specific Wilayah" (Guardianship)? Is this not the same "Khalifate" (in the specific sense held by the Shi'a)? The word "Wazir" (minister/helper) also confirms and strengthens this.

In other words, there are duties that are not the work of all individuals: preserving the Prophet's religion from any distortion or deviation, interpreting any ambiguity that might occur for some regarding the content of the religion, leading the Ummah during the Prophet's absence and after him, and providing highly effective assistance in advancing his goals. These are the very things the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) asked of Allah for Ali (peace be upon him) by saying "make him a partner in my task."

From here it becomes clear that Aaron's death before Moses (peace be upon him) creates no problem in this discussion, because succession and deputyship are sometimes during the leader's absence—as Aaron had during Moses' absence—and sometimes after his death—as Ali (peace be upon him) had after the Prophet's death. Both share a common denominator and a single comprehensive concept, even if the instances differed. (Reflect on this).

[1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Short Sayings, Wisdom 176. [1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse.

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ مَنَنَّا عَلَيْكَ مَرَّةً أُخْرَىٰٓ (٣٧)
Certainly, We have done you a favour another time, (37)
إِذْ أَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَىٰٓ أُمِّكَ مَا يُوحَىٰٓ (٣٨)
when We revealed to your mother whatever was to be revealed: (38)
أَنِ ٱقْذِفِيهِ فِى ٱلتَّابُوتِ فَٱقْذِفِيهِ فِى ٱلْيَمِّ فَلْيُلْقِهِ ٱلْيَمُّ بِٱلسَّاحِلِ يَأْخُذْهُ عَدُوٌّ لِّى وَعَدُوٌّ لَّهُۥ ۚ وَأَلْقَيْتُ عَلَيْكَ مَحَبَّةً مِّنِّى وَلِتُصْنَعَ عَلَىٰ عَيْنِىٓ (٣٩)
“Put him in the casket, and cast it into the river. Then the river will cast it on the bank, and he shall be picked up by an enemy of Mine and an enemy of his.” And I made you endearing, and that you might be reared under My [watchful] eyes. (39)
إِذْ تَمْشِىٓ أُخْتُكَ فَتَقُولُ هَلْ أَدُلُّكُمْ عَلَىٰ مَن يَكْفُلُهُۥ ۖ فَرَجَعْنَـٰكَ إِلَىٰٓ أُمِّكَ كَىْ تَقَرَّ عَيْنُهَا وَلَا تَحْزَنَ ۚ وَقَتَلْتَ نَفْسًا فَنَجَّيْنَـٰكَ مِنَ ٱلْغَمِّ وَفَتَنَّـٰكَ فُتُونًا ۚ فَلَبِثْتَ سِنِينَ فِىٓ أَهْلِ مَدْيَنَ ثُمَّ جِئْتَ عَلَىٰ قَدَرٍ يَـٰمُوسَىٰ (٤٠)
When your sister walked up [to Pharaoh’s palace] saying, “Shall I show you someone who will take care of him?” Then We restored you to your mother, that she might not grieve and be comforted. Then you slew a soul, whereupon We delivered you from anguish, and We tried you with various ordeals. Then you stayed for several years among the people of Midian. Then you turned up as ordained, O Moses! (40)
وَٱصْطَنَعْتُكَ لِنَفْسِى (٤١)
And I chose you for Myself.’ (41)

Commentary

What a Merciful God!

In these verses, Allah refers to another chapter of the life of Musa (عليه السلام), which pertains to his childhood and his miraculous rescue from the clutches of the Pharaohs' wrath. Although this chapter chronologically precedes the chapter of his mission and prophethood, it is mentioned as a secondary matter to the subject of his messengership, serving as evidence of the blessings Allah bestowed upon Musa (عليه السلام) from the very beginning of his life.

First, He says: "O Musa! We have bestowed Our favor upon you once before, and made you the recipient of Our blessings" (وَ لَقَدْ مَنَنَّا عَلَيْكَ مَرَّةً أُخْرى‌). [1]

After mentioning this in brief, He proceeds to elaborate on it, saying: "At that

[^1]: As we have mentioned previously: the word "minnah" (favor/grace) is originally derived from "mann," meaning the large stones used for weighing. For this reason, the granting of any heavy and precious blessing is called minnah, and in the above verse, this is the intended meaning. This concept is its beautiful and valuable sense. However, if someone magnifies their own small deed through speech and throws it in the face of the recipient, it is an ugly act and constitutes the censured form of minnah (reminding of favors).

time when We revealed to your mother that which was to be revealed" (إِذْ أَوْحَيْنا إِلى‌ أُمِّكَ ما يُوحى‌).

This refers to the fact that: All the paths leading to the rescue of Musa (عليه السلام) from the clutches of the Pharaohs on that day—We taught them all to your mother.

For, as understood from other verses of the Quran, Pharaoh had severely oppressed the Children of Israel, specifically to prevent their power and strength and their potential rebellion. Or, according to a group of historians and commentators, to prevent the birth of a child who it was predicted would rise from the Children of Israel and demolish Pharaoh’s establishment, he had ordered their sons to be killed and their daughters to be kept alive for concubinage and service.

Naturally, Pharaoh's spies kept a close watch on the neighborhoods and houses of the Children of Israel, reporting the birth of male children to his administration, which would then act swiftly to destroy them.

Some commentators have said: Pharaoh, on one hand, wanted to break the power of the Children of Israel, and on the other hand, did not wish for their race to become entirely extinct, as they were considered ready slaves and servants for him. Therefore, he ordered that one year their infants be left alive and one year the boys be put to the sword. Coincidentally, Musa was born in the very year of the massacre of male children!

In any case, the mother felt that her infant's life was in danger and that keeping him hidden temporarily would not solve the problem. At this moment, the God who had nominated this child for a great uprising inspired the heart of this mother to entrust him to Us from now on and see how We shall preserve him and return him to you.

He inspired the heart of Musa's mother thus: "Cast him into the chest, and cast the chest into the river!" (أَنِ اقْذِفِيهِ فِي التَّابُوتِ فَاقْذِفِيهِ فِي الْيَمِ‌).

"Yam" here refers to the great River Nile, which, due to its vastness and abundant water, is sometimes referred to as a sea.

The expression "اقْذِفِيهِ فِي التَّابُوتِ" (cast him into the chest) perhaps suggests that she should detach her heart from him without any fear or apprehension, bravely place him in his chest, and indifferently cast him into the Nile stream, allowing no fear or terror into herself.

The word "tabut" means a wooden chest, and contrary to what some imagine, it does not always mean a coffin for the dead; rather, it has a broad meaning that is sometimes applied to other boxes, as we read in the story of Talut and Jalut in Surah Al-Baqarah under verse 248. [1] Then He adds: "The river is commanded to cast it onto the shore, so that an enemy of Mine and an enemy of his may take him" (and nurture him in his own lap!) (فَلْيُلْقِهِ الْيَمُّ بِالسَّاحِلِ يَأْخُذْهُ عَدُوٌّ لِي وَ عَدُوٌّ لَهُ‌).

It is interesting that the word "aduww" (enemy) is repeated here, which is in fact an emphasis on Pharaoh's enmity toward both Allah and toward Musa and the Children of Israel. It points out that the one who persisted to such an extent in enmity and hostility eventually took upon himself the service and nurturing of Musa, so that mortal man may know he is not only unable to fight Allah's command, but that Allah will have his enemy nurtured by his own hand and in his own lap.

And when He wills the destruction of the rebellious oppressors, He destroys them by their own hands and burns them with the fire they themselves kindled. What an extraordinary power He has! And since Musa (عليه السلام) had to be placed within a protective shield on this path of ups and downs ahead of him, Allah cast a ray of His love upon him such that anyone who saw him would fall in love with him; not only would they not consent to killing him, but they would not even allow a hair to be lost from his head! As the Quran says in the continuation of these verses: "And I cast love from Myself upon you" (وَ أَلْقَيْتُ عَلَيْكَ مَحَبَّةً مِنِّي‌).

What an amazing shield—completely invisible, yet stronger than steel and iron.

It is said: Musa's midwife was from the Pharaohs and intended to report his birth to Pharaoh's tyrannical administration, but the first time her eyes met the infant's eyes, it was as if a spark leaped from his eyes that illuminated the depths of the midwife's heart, cast the cord of his love around her neck, and drove any evil thought from her mind!

In this regard, we read in a Hadith from Imam al-Baqir (عليه السلام): "When Musa (عليه السلام) was born and the mother saw her infant was a boy, the color drained from her face. The midwife asked, 'Why has your color turned yellow like this?' She said, 'I fear they will cut off my son's head.' But the midwife said, 'Never harbor such fear, for Musa was such that no one saw him but that they loved him!' (و كان موسى لا يراه احد الا احبه)." [1] And it was this shield of love that completely protected him even in Pharaoh's court.

At the end of this verse, He says: "The goal was that you might be brought up under My care and before My eyes (My knowledge)" (وَ لِتُصْنَعَ عَلى‌ عَيْنِي‌).

Undoubtedly, not an atom in the heavens or the earth is hidden from Allah's knowledge, and all are present before Him. However, this expression here refers to the special grace Allah had toward Musa and his upbringing.

Although some commentators have limited the phrase "وَ لِتُصْنَعَ عَلى‌ عَيْنِي‌" to the issue of Musa's nursing period and the like, it is clear that this phrase has a broad meaning, encompassing every kind of nurturing, training, and preparation of Musa (عليه السلام) to carry the banner of the messengership under the special grace of the Lord.

From the clues present in these verses and similar verses in the Holy Quran, and what has come down in narrations and histories, it is well understood that Musa's mother (عليه السلام) finally, with terror and anxiety, cast the chest containing Musa into the Nile. The waves of the Nile carried it on their shoulders, and the mother, seeing the scene, fell into a feverish state, but Allah inspired her heart not to let grief or sorrow in, for We shall eventually return him safely to you.

Pharaoh's palace was built on a corner of the Nile stream, and likely a branch of this great stream passed through his palace. The waves of the water pulled the chest of Musa's rescue into that branch while Pharaoh and his wife were busy watching the waves by the water. Suddenly, this mysterious chest caught their attention. He ordered the guards to take the chest from the water. When the chest was opened, to their utter amazement, they saw a beautiful infant inside—something they perhaps had not even considered a possibility.

Pharaoh realized that this infant must be from the Children of Israel who had met such a fate out of fear of the guards, and he issued the order to kill him. But his wife, who was "barren," became deeply attached to the child, and the mysterious ray that leaped from the infant's eyes penetrated the corners of that woman's heart, making her captivated and enchanted by him.

She pleaded with Pharaoh, and while referring to this child as "the delight of the eyes" (qurratu 'ayn), she requested that his killing be waived. Even more, she requested that they nurture him in their lap as their own son and source of future hope, and finally, with persistence, she succeeded in establishing her word.

But on the other hand, the child has become hungry and wants milk; he cries and sheds tears—cries and tears that made the heart of Pharaoh's wife tremble. There was no choice but for the guards to search for a wet nurse as soon as possible. But every wet nurse they brought, the infant would not take her breast, for Allah had decreed that he should only return to his mother. The guards set out again to search, going door to door looking for a new wet nurse.

Now we read the rest of the story from the above verses:

Yes, O Musa, We had decreed that you be brought up before Our eyes (Our knowledge) "when your sister walked (near Pharaoh's palace)" and, by her mother's order, was monitoring the situation and your fate (إِذْ تَمْشِي أُخْتُكَ‌).

She said to Pharaoh's guards, "Shall I direct you to one who will take charge of him?" (فَتَقُولُ هَلْ أَدُلُّكُمْ عَلى‌ مَنْ يَكْفُلُهُ‌).

And perhaps she added: This woman has pure milk that I am sure the infant will accept.

The guards were delighted and, hoping that their missing piece might be found this way, they moved along with her. Musa's sister, showing herself as an unknown and stranger, informed the mother of the matter. The mother, without losing her composure, while a storm of love and hope surrounded her entire heart, came to Pharaoh's court. They placed the child in her lap; the child smelled the mother's scent—a familiar scent. Suddenly, he took her breast like his own sweet soul and, with great love and interest, began drinking the milk. A shout of joy rose from those present, and signs of satisfaction and longing appeared in the eyes of Pharaoh's wife.

Some say: Pharaoh was surprised by this event and said, "Who are you that this infant accepted your milk while he rejected all others?" The mother said: "I am a woman of pure scent and pure milk, and no child rejects my milk!"

In any case, Pharaoh entrusted the child to her, and his wife emphasized heavily his protection and care, ordering that the child be brought to her at short intervals.

It is here that the Quran says: "So We returned you to your mother that her eye might be cooled and she might not grieve" (فَرَجَعْناكَ إِلى‌ أُمِّكَ كَيْ تَقَرَّ عَيْنُها وَ لا تَحْزَنَ‌).

And so she could proceed with nurturing the child with peace of mind and assurance of the absence of danger for him from the Pharaohs.

From the above sentence, it can be inferred that Pharaoh entrusted the child to the mother to bring him to her own home, but it is natural that an infant who is Pharaoh's adopted son! and the object of his wife's intense affection must be brought to them at short intervals.

Years passed, and Musa (عليه السلام) was nurtured amidst a halo of Allah's grace and love and a safe and secure environment. Gradually, he became a young man.

One day, while passing through a path, he saw two people engaged in a fight and dispute before him, one from the Children of Israel and the other from the Copts (Egyptians and supporters of Pharaoh). Since the Children of Israel were always under the pressure and harassment of the oppressive Copts, Musa rushed to the aid of the oppressed one who was from the Children of Israel and, to defend him, struck a heavy blow with his fist upon the Copt's body. But this defense of the oppressed reached a critical point, and that single punch finished the Copt.

Musa became distressed by this event because Pharaoh's guards eventually realized by whose hand this killing had occurred, and they set out intensely to pursue him.

But Musa, according to the advice of some of his friends, left Egypt secretly and hurried toward Madyan, where he found a safe and secure environment alongside the Prophet Shu'ayb, the details of which will come, Allah willing, in the commentary of Surah Al-Qasas.

It is here that the Quran says: "And you killed a man and were filled with grief, but We delivered you from that grief" (وَ قَتَلْتَ نَفْساً فَنَجَّيْناكَ مِنَ الْغَمِ‌).

And after that, "We tried you with many trials (in the furnaces of events one after another)" (وَ فَتَنَّاكَ فُتُوناً).

"Then you stayed for years among the people of Madyan" (فَلَبِثْتَ سِنِينَ فِي أَهْلِ مَدْيَنَ‌) and after traversing this long path and gaining spiritual and physical readiness and emerging from the furnace of events with pride and victory, "then you came here at the decreed time" (ثُمَّ جِئْتَ عَلى‌ قَدَرٍ يا مُوسى‌).

The word "qadar," according to many commentators, means the time that had been decreed for Musa to be chosen for the messengership. However, others have taken it to mean "measure" or "stature," as it has appeared with this meaning in some verses of the Quran (Surah Al-Hijr, verse 21). According to this interpretation, the meaning of the sentence would be: O Musa, after these ups and downs and various trials and an extended life being nurtured alongside a great Prophet like Shu'ayb, you finally attained a stature, status, and personality that became ready to receive revelation.

Then He adds: "And I have prepared you for Myself" (وَ اصْطَنَعْتُكَ لِنَفْسِي‌) for the heavy duty of receiving revelation, for accepting the messengership, for the guidance and leadership of My servants; I nurtured you and tested you in the heat of events and gave you strength and power, and now that this great mission is placed upon your shoulders, you are prepared in every respect!

"Istina'" is from the root "sun'" meaning "insistence and emphatic action for the reform of something" (as Raghib has said in Al-Mufradat). That is: I reformed you in every respect as if I want you for Myself, and this is the most affectionate statement that Allah spoke regarding this great Prophet. As some have said, it is similar to the saying of the sages: "Indeed, when Allah Almighty loves a servant, He looks after him just as a friend looks after his friend."

Ayahs

ٱذْهَبْ أَنتَ وَأَخُوكَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِى وَلَا تَنِيَا فِى ذِكْرِى (٤٢)
‘Go ahead, you and your brother, with My signs and do not flag in My remembrance. (42)
ٱذْهَبَآ إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ إِنَّهُۥ طَغَىٰ (٤٣)
Both of you go to Pharaoh, for he has indeed rebelled. (43)
فَقُولَا لَهُۥ قَوْلًا لَّيِّنًا لَّعَلَّهُۥ يَتَذَكَّرُ أَوْ يَخْشَىٰ (٤٤)
Speak to him in a soft manner; maybe he will take admonition or fear.’ (44)
قَالَا رَبَّنَآ إِنَّنَا نَخَافُ أَن يَفْرُطَ عَلَيْنَآ أَوْ أَن يَطْغَىٰ (٤٥)
They said, ‘Our Lord! We are indeed afraid that he will forestall us or will exceed all bounds.’ (45)
قَالَ لَا تَخَافَآ ۖ إِنَّنِى مَعَكُمَآ أَسْمَعُ وَأَرَىٰ (٤٦)
He said, ‘Do not be afraid, for I will be with the two of you, hearing and seeing [whatever happens]. (46)
فَأْتِيَاهُ فَقُولَآ إِنَّا رَسُولَا رَبِّكَ فَأَرْسِلْ مَعَنَا بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ وَلَا تُعَذِّبْهُمْ ۖ قَدْ جِئْنَـٰكَ بِـَٔايَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّكَ ۖ وَٱلسَّلَـٰمُ عَلَىٰ مَنِ ٱتَّبَعَ ٱلْهُدَىٰٓ (٤٧)
So approach him and say, ‘‘We are the apostles of your Lord. Let the Children of Israel go with us, and do not torture them! We certainly bring you a sign from your Lord, and may peace be upon him who follows guidance! (47)
إِنَّا قَدْ أُوحِىَ إِلَيْنَآ أَنَّ ٱلْعَذَابَ عَلَىٰ مَن كَذَّبَ وَتَوَلَّىٰ (٤٨)
Indeed it has been revealed to us that punishment shall befall those who impugn us and turn their backs [on us].’’ ’ (48)

Commentary

The First Encounter with the Tyrant Pharaoh

Now that everything is prepared and all necessary means have been placed at Musa’s disposal, Allah addresses both him and his brother Harun together, saying: "Go, you and your brother, with My signs which I have entrusted to you" (اذْهَبْ أَنْتَ وَ أَخُوكَ بِآياتِي‌).

These "signs" include both the two great miracles of Musa and the other divine tokens, teachings, and programs which themselves testify to the truthfulness of his call—especially since these profound teachings are expressed by a man who apparently spent the greater part of his life as a shepherd.

To strengthen their morale and emphasize the need for utmost effort and striving, He adds: "And do not slacken in My remembrance and in carrying out My commands" (وَ لا تَنِيا فِي ذِكْرِي‌).

For slackness and a lack of decisiveness will blow away all your labors; stand firm, fear no incident, and do not weaken before any power.

After that, He specifies the primary objective of this movement and the point toward which they must aim, saying: "Go both of you to Pharaoh, for he has indeed transgressed" (اذْهَبا إِلى‌ فِرْعَوْنَ إِنَّهُ طَغى‌). He is the source of all the miseries of this vast land, and until he is reformed, nothing can be achieved. This is because the factor behind the progress or decline, the happiness or misery of a nation, is, above all else, its leaders and heads; therefore, your targeting must be directed toward them first.

It is true that Harun was not present in that desert at that moment, and as commentators have stated, Allah informed him of this event and he came out of Egypt to welcome his brother Musa for this mission.
