However, there is no obstacle to addressing two people and assigning them a mission to perform a task while only one is present. We have many such examples in daily Persian expressions as well, such as saying: "You and your brother, who returns from his journey tomorrow, come to me."

Then, He describes the effective way to encounter Pharaoh at the beginning of the task so that they might influence and affect him: "Speak to him with mild speech; perhaps he may take heed or fear God" (فَقُولا لَهُ قَوْلًا لَيِّناً لَعَلَّهُ يَتَذَكَّرُ أَوْ يَخْشى‌). The difference between "taking heed" (يتذكر) and "fearing" (يخشى) here is that: if you face him with soft and gentle speech while expressing the matters with clarity and decisiveness, one possibility is that he will accept your logical proofs from his heart and believe. Another possibility is that, at the very least, out of fear of Divine punishment in this world or the Hereafter and the loss of his power, he will submit and not oppose you. Of course, a third possibility exists: that he neither takes heed nor fears Allah, but rather chooses the path of opposition and struggle, which is hinted at by the expression "perhaps" (لعل). In that case, the argument has been completed against him, and in no circumstance is the implementation of this program useless.

Undoubtedly, Allah knew what the end of his affair would be, but the above expressions are a lesson for Musa, Harun, and all leaders on the Divine path. [1]

Nevertheless, Musa and Harun were concerned that this arrogant and powerful tyrant, whose reputation for violence and stubbornness was widespread, might strike first and eliminate them before Musa (peace be upon him) and Harun (peace be upon him) could deliver the call. Therefore, "They said: 'Our Lord! Indeed, we fear that he may hasten against us or that he may transgress'" (قالا رَبَّنا إِنَّنا نَخافُ أَنْ يَفْرُطَ عَلَيْنا أَوْ أَنْ يَطْغى‌).

"Yafruta" (يفرط) is derived from "fart" (فرط), meaning to get ahead or precede. For this reason, someone who reaches a watering place before everyone else is called "farit." In the words of Ali (peace be upon him) spoken before the graves of the dead outside the gate of Kufa, we read: "You were our preceding vanguard (fart) and you hastened to the abode of the Hereafter before us." [2] In any case, Musa and his brother feared two things: first, that Pharaoh might take severe action before hearing their words, or that he might take such action immediately after hearing them without reflection, and in either case, their mission would be endangered and left incomplete.

But Allah said to them decisively: "Fear not; indeed, I am with you both; I hear and I see" (قالَ لا تَخافا إِنَّنِي مَعَكُما أَسْمَعُ وَ أَرى‌).

[1]: Regarding the meaning of "la'alla" (perhaps) and its usage in the Quran, we have discussed it in detail in Volume 4, page 35, under verse 84 of Surah An-Nisa. [2]: Nahj al-Balagha, Short Sayings, No. 130.

Therefore, in the presence of the Almighty God who is with you everywhere—and for this reason hears everything and all words, and sees everything, and is your Supporter and Protector—fear and terror have no meaning.

Then, He precisely describes how to carry out their call in the presence of Pharaoh in five short, decisive, and content-rich sentences: one regarding the essence of the mission, the second stating the content of the mission, the third providing the proof and document, the fourth encouraging those who accept, and finally, threatening the opponents. First, He says: "Go to him and say: 'Indeed, we are messengers of your Lord'" (فَأْتِياهُ فَقُولا إِنَّا رَسُولا رَبِّكَ‌).

It is interesting that instead of "our Lord," He says "your Lord," to draw Pharaoh's emotions to the point that he has a Lord and these are the representatives of his Lord. Simultaneously, this implicitly makes him understand that the claim of Lordship is not correct for anyone and belongs exclusively to Allah.

Secondly: "So send the Children of Israel with us and do not torment them" (فأَرْسِلْ مَعَنا بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ وَ لا تُعَذِّبْهُمْ‌).

It is true that Musa's call was not solely for the salvation of the Children of Israel from the clutches of the Pharaohs, but rather—as evidenced by other Quranic verses—it was also for the salvation of Pharaoh himself and his people from the clutches of polytheism and idolatry. However, the importance of this issue and its logical connection to Musa caused him to specifically point to this matter, as the exploitation and enslavement of the Children of Israel with all that torture and harassment was not something that could be justified.

Then, referring to his proof and evidence, he says: tell him, "We have come to you with a sign from your Lord" (قَدْ جِئْناكَ بِآيَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّكَ‌).

We do not speak in vain, nor do we say anything without proof. Therefore, by the decree of reason, it is necessary for you to at least reflect on our words, and if they are correct, accept them.

Then, as an encouragement to the believers, they add: "And peace be upon him who follows the guidance" (وَ السَّلامُ عَلى‌ مَنِ اتَّبَعَ الْهُدى‌).

This sentence may also refer to another meaning: that safety in this world and the next from distress, suffering, painful Divine punishment, and the problems of individual and social life belongs to those who follow Divine guidance; and this is, in fact, the ultimate result of Musa's call.

Finally, make him understand the grim consequence of turning away from this call and say: "Indeed, it has been revealed to us that the punishment will be upon him who denies and turns away"! (إِنَّا قَدْ أُوحِيَ إِلَيْنا أَنَّ الْعَذابَ عَلى‌ مَنْ كَذَّبَ وَ تولَّى‌).

It might be imagined that mentioning this sentence is inconsistent with the mild speech they were commanded to use, but this is a mistake. What prevents a compassionate physician from saying in a gentle tone to his patient: "Whoever uses this medicine will be saved, and whoever does not, death will overtake him"?

This statement is the result of an inappropriate encounter with a reality; it contains no specific threat, personal confrontation, or severity of action. In other words, it is a reality that had to be told to Pharaoh plainly.

Key Points

1- God's Extraordinary Display of Power

Throughout history, we have often seen tyrannical and powerful individuals rise to fight against the power of Truth. But interestingly, in no case does Allah mobilize the armies of the heavens and the earth to crush them; rather, He defeats them so simply and easily that no one could even imagine it.

Particularly, it often happens that He sends them after the very means of their own death and entrusts the mission of their execution to themselves!

In this very story of Pharaoh, we see that He nurtures his main enemy—Musa—in his own lap and brings him into his own sphere of protection! More interestingly, according to historical accounts, Musa's midwife was from the Copts; the carpenter who built the ark for his salvation was also a Copt; those who took the ark from the water were Pharaoh's officers; and the one who opened the ark was his own wife. Through Pharaoh's own establishment, Musa's mother was invited as a wet nurse, and the pursuit of Musa by the Pharaohs after the incident of killing the Copt man became the cause of his migration to Madyan and his undergoing a period of complete education and training in the school of the Prophet Shu'ayb.

Indeed, when Allah wills to show His power, He acts in this way so that all the arrogant may know they are too small to stand against His will and decree.

2- Gentle and Affectionate Encounter with Enemies

To influence people's hearts (even if they are misguided and very corrupt), the first command of the Quran is a gentle encounter coupled with kindness and human emotions. Resorting to severity is related to later stages when friendly encounters prove ineffective.

The goal is for people to be attracted, to take heed and find the way, or to fear the grim consequences of their evil deeds (لَعَلَّهُ يَتَذَكَّرُ أَوْ يَخْشى‌).

Every school of thought must have attraction and should not repel individuals without reason. The lives of the Prophets and the Imams of the religion (peace be upon them) clearly show that they never deviated from this program throughout their entire lives.

Yes, it is possible that no affectionate program will affect the black heart of some, and the only way is to resort to severity; that is correct in its place, but not as a general principle or for the beginning of the work. The primary program is kindness and gentleness, and this is the very lesson that the above verses clearly teach us.

Interestingly, we read in some narrations: even Musa was commanded to call Pharaoh by his best name, so that it might affect his dark heart.

3- Is Revelation (Wahy) Given to Other Than Prophets?

Undoubtedly, "Wahy" (revelation) has various meanings in the Holy Quran.

Sometimes it means a low voice or saying something quietly (this is its original meaning in the Arabic language).

Sometimes it means a symbolic gesture toward something, such as: "So he signaled (awha) to them to glorify [Allah] morning and evening" — (Maryam: 11), referring to Zakariyya whose tongue had ceased to function at that hour.

And sometimes it means instinctive inspiration, such as: "And your Lord inspired (awha) the bee" (An-Nahl: 68).

Sometimes it means a creative command (takwini), a command given through the language of creation, such as: "That Day it will report its news because your Lord has inspired (awha) it" (Az-Zalzal: 5).

And sometimes it means inspiration (ilham) that He sends to the hearts of believers, even if they are not a Prophet or an Imam, such as: "When We inspired (awhayna) to your mother what was to be inspired" (Taha: 38).

However, one of its most important usages in the Holy Quran is for Divine messages specific to the Prophets, such as: "Indeed, We have revealed (awhayna) to you as We revealed to Nuh and the prophets after him" (An-Nisa: 163).

Therefore, the word "Wahy" has a broad and comprehensive concept that includes all these cases, and thus we will not be surprised if, in the above verses, the word "Wahy" is used regarding the mother of Musa.

4- Answer to a Question [Why Musa felt apprehension despite Divine promises]

Upon studying the above verses, some might ask: why does Musa (peace be upon him), despite those Divine promises, still experience concern, hesitation, and apprehension?

To the extent that Allah explicitly tells him: "Go, I am with you everywhere, I hear all words, and I see everything, and there is no cause for concern."

The answer to this question becomes clear from the fact that this mission was truly a very heavy one. Musa, who is apparently a shepherd, intends to go alone with his brother to fight a tyrannical, powerful, and rebellious man who had the greatest powers of that time at his disposal. Furthermore, he is commanded to start the first call with Pharaoh himself, not to go after others first to gather an army and helpers; rather, he must strike the first spark upon the heart of Pharaoh. This was truly a very complex and exceptionally difficult mission. Additionally, we know that knowledge and awareness have levels; often a person knows something with certainty, yet desires to reach the stage of "certainty of sight" (ilm al-yaqin) and objective reassurance, just as Ibrahim, despite his firm faith in the Resurrection, asked Allah to see a scene of the dead being brought to life in this world with his own eyes so that his heart might find greater reassurance.

Ayahs

قَالَ فَمَن رَّبُّكُمَا يَـٰمُوسَىٰ (٤٩)
He said, ‘Who is your Lord, Moses?’ (49)
قَالَ رَبُّنَا ٱلَّذِىٓ أَعْطَىٰ كُلَّ شَىْءٍ خَلْقَهُۥ ثُمَّ هَدَىٰ (٥٠)
He said, ‘Our Lord is He who gave everything its creation and then guided it.’ (50)
قَالَ فَمَا بَالُ ٱلْقُرُونِ ٱلْأُولَىٰ (٥١)
He said, ‘What about the former generations?’ (51)
قَالَ عِلْمُهَا عِندَ رَبِّى فِى كِتَـٰبٍ ۖ لَّا يَضِلُّ رَبِّى وَلَا يَنسَى (٥٢)
He said, ‘Their knowledge is with my Lord, in a Book. My Lord neither makes any error nor forgets.’ (52)
ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَ لَكُمُ ٱلْأَرْضَ مَهْدًا وَسَلَكَ لَكُمْ فِيهَا سُبُلًا وَأَنزَلَ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءً فَأَخْرَجْنَا بِهِۦٓ أَزْوَٰجًا مِّن نَّبَاتٍ شَتَّىٰ (٥٣)
He, who made the earth for you a cradle, and in it threaded for you ways, and sent down water from the sky, and with it We brought forth various kinds of vegetation. (53)
كُلُوا۟ وَٱرْعَوْا۟ أَنْعَـٰمَكُمْ ۗ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّأُو۟لِى ٱلنُّهَىٰ (٥٤)
‘Eat and pasture your cattle.’ There are indeed signs in that for those who have sense. (54)
۞ مِنْهَا خَلَقْنَـٰكُمْ وَفِيهَا نُعِيدُكُمْ وَمِنْهَا نُخْرِجُكُمْ تَارَةً أُخْرَىٰ (٥٥)
From it did We create you, into it shall We return you, and from it shall We bring you forth another time. (55)

Commentary

Who is Your Lord?!

Here, the Holy Quran, as is its style, omits the details that can be understood from this story through future discussions and goes directly to the dialogue between Moses and Aaron with Pharaoh.

In fact, the matter is as follows:

After receiving the command of prophethood and a complete, comprehensive, and multifaceted instruction on how to deal with Pharaoh, Moses departs from that holy land. According to historians, he joins his brother Aaron near Egypt, and both approach Pharaoh. With great difficulty, they manage to enter Pharaoh's legendary palace, to which few people had access.

When Moses stood before Pharaoh, he recounted the calculated and effective sentences that Allah had taught him at the time of the command of prophethood:

We are the messengers of your Lord.

Send the Children of Israel with us and do not torture or harass them.

We have brought a clear proof and miracle to you from your Lord.

Peace be upon him who follows the guidance.

And know this also, that it has been revealed to us that punishment awaits those who deny and turn away from the command of God! When Pharaoh heard these words, his first reaction was to say: "Tell me, who is your Lord, O Moses?" (قَالَ فَمَنْ رَبُّكُمَا يَا مُوسَىٰ).

It is strange that the arrogant and self-satisfied Pharaoh was not even willing to say, "Who is my Lord whom you claim?" Rather, he said: "Who is your Lord?"

Moses immediately provided a very comprehensive yet brief introduction of the Lord: "He said: Our Lord is He who gave to each thing its form of creation, and then guided it" (قَالَ رَبُّنَا الَّذِي أَعْطَىٰ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلْقَهُ ثُمَّ هَدَىٰ). In this short statement, Moses refers to two fundamental principles of creation and existence, each of which is an independent and clear proof for identifying the Lord:

First, that Allah has bestowed upon every being what it needed. This is a subject about which volumes could be written; indeed, volumes have been written.

If we look closely at the plants and living creatures that inhabit every region—including birds, sea creatures, insects, and reptiles—we will see that each is in perfect harmony with its environment and has at its disposal whatever it requires.

The structure of birds is such that their shape, weight, and various senses prepare them for flight, and the structure of creatures in the depths of the seas is the same.

Certainly, a discussion of them cannot fit into this brief space.

The second issue: the issue of the guidance and leadership of beings, which the Quran places after the provision of needs using the word "then" (thumma).

A person or thing might have vital tools at its disposal but not know how to use them. What is important is to be familiar with how to apply them, and this is exactly what we see clearly in various beings: how each of them employs its powers precisely in the path of continuing its life—how they build nests, reproduce, raise their offspring, hide from enemies, or rise to fight the enemy.

Humans also possess this ontological guidance (hidayat-e takwini), but since the human is a being endowed with intellect and consciousness, Allah has coupled His ontological guidance with legislative guidance (hidayat-e tashri'i) through the prophets, so that if he does not deviate from that path, he will certainly reach the destination.

In other words, because humans possess intellect and free will, they have duties, responsibilities, and consequently, evolutionary programs that animals do not have. For this reason, in addition to ontological guidance, they also need legislative guidance.

In short: Moses (peace be upon him) wants to make Pharaoh understand that this universe is neither limited to you nor limited to the land of Egypt; it is neither exclusive to today nor the past. This vast world has a past and a future in which neither I nor you existed, and two fundamental issues are striking in this world: the provision of needs and then the employment of powers and possibilities in the path of the progress of beings. These can well introduce you to our Lord, and the more you reflect on this matter, the more proofs of His greatness and power you will find.

Upon hearing this comprehensive and interesting answer, Pharaoh raised another question: "He said: If it is so, then what is the state of the former generations?" (قَالَ فَمَا بَالُ الْقُرُونِ الْأُولَىٰ). Regarding what Pharaoh meant by this sentence, commentators have expressed various views:

  1. Some have said: Since Moses, in his last sentence, considered the opponents of monotheism subject to divine punishment, Pharaoh asked: "Then why were all those polytheistic nations of the past not afflicted with such punishment?!"
  2. Some have said: Since Moses introduced the God of the universe as the Lord and Deity of all, Pharaoh asked: "Then why were our ancestors and all these past nations polytheists? This shows that polytheism and idol-worship are not incorrect!"
  3. Others have said: Since the implication of Moses' words was that eventually everyone reaches the result of their deeds and those who disobeyed the divine command will be punished, Pharaoh asked: "Then what will be the fate of those who perished and have no return to this life?"

In any case, Moses replied: "The knowledge of them is with my Lord in a Record; my Lord neither errs nor forgets" (قَالَ عِلْمُهَا عِنْدَ رَبِّي فِي كِتَابٍ لَا يَضِلُّ رَبِّي وَلَا يَنْسَىٰ) [^1]. Therefore, their account and record are preserved, and they will eventually receive the reward and punishment for their deeds. The keeper of this account is a God in whose work there is neither error nor forgetfulness. Given what Moses (peace be upon him) stated regarding the principle of monotheism and the introduction of God, it is perfectly clear that keeping this account will not be a difficult task for Him who has given every being exactly its needs and then guides it.

Regarding the difference in meaning between the phrase "neither errs" (la yadillu) and "nor forgets" (la yansa), commentators have various explanations. However, it appears that "neither errs" refers to the negation of any kind of mistake by the Lord, and "nor forgets" refers to the negation of forgetfulness. That is, He neither makes a mistake in the beginning when accounting for individuals, nor does He suffer from forgetfulness in maintaining their records. In this way, Moses implicitly points out the Lord's scientific encompassment of everything, so that Pharaoh realizes that not a shred of his deeds will be hidden from the perspective of God's knowledge or go without reward or punishment.

In truth, this divine scientific encompassment is the result of the statement Moses (peace be upon him) made earlier: that the God who has given every being its needs and guides it is aware of the state of everyone and everything.

And since part of Moses' (peace be upon him) speech was about the issue of monotheism and the recognition of God, the Quran here presents another chapter in the same field: "He who has made the earth for you as a cradle, and has threaded for you paths therein, and has sent down water from the sky" (الَّذِي جَعَلَ لَكُمُ الْأَرْضَ مَهْدًا وَسَلَكَ لَكُمْ فِيهَا سُبُلًا وَأَنْزَلَ مِنَ السَّمَاءِ مَاءً).

"And by means of this water, We brought forth various pairs of different plants from the dark soil" (فَأَخْرَجْنَا بِهِ أَزْوَاجًا مِنْ نَبَاتٍ شَتَّىٰ).

Altogether, this verse refers to four categories of God's great blessings:

  1. The earth, which is a cradle of peace and comfort for man. By the blessing of the law of gravity and also the vast layer of air surrounding it, man can live on it with ease, safety, and security.
  2. The paths and roads that God has created on the earth, which link all its regions together. As we have often seen, among sky-high mountain ranges, there are usually valleys and paths through which man can pass to reach his destination.
  3. Water, which is the essence of life and the source of all blessings, sent down from the sky.
  4. Various and diverse plants and vegetation that grow from the earth by means of this water, some of which constitute human food, some medicinal materials, some used by man for making clothes, and others for life's utilities (such as doors and even houses made of wood, ships, and many other means of transport).

Indeed, it can be said that these four great blessings, in the same order they appear in the above verse, constitute the priorities of human life: before everything else, a place of residence and peace is necessary, followed by communication routes, then water, and then agricultural products.

Finally, referring to the fifth and last blessing in this series of divine favors, He says: "Eat and pasture your cattle" (كُلُوا وَارْعَوْا أَنْعَامَكُمْ). This indicates that your animal products—which constitute an important part of your food, clothing, and other life utilities—are also by the blessing of that same earth and that same water which descends from the sky.

And at the end, while referring to all these blessings, He says: "Indeed, in that are clear signs for those of intellect" (إِنَّ فِي ذَٰلِكَ لَآيَاتٍ لِأُولِي النُّهَىٰ).

It is noteworthy that "Nuha" is the plural of "Nuhyah," originally derived from the root "Nahy" (the opposite of command) and means the intellect and knowledge that forbids (yanha) man from ugly deeds. This indicates that just any kind of thought is not sufficient to realize the importance of these signs; rather, responsible intellects and thoughts can grasp this reality.

And fittingly, since the creation of the earth and its blessings were used in the monotheistic explanation of these verses, the "Resurrection" is also mentioned in the last verse under discussion with reference to this same earth: "From it We created you, and into it We will return you, and from it We will bring you forth once again" (مِنْهَا خَلَقْنَاكُمْ وَفِيهَا نُعِيدُكُمْ وَمِنْهَا نُخْرِجُكُمْ تَارَةً أُخْرَىٰ).

What an expressive and concise description of the past, present, and future of humans! We have all come from the soil, we all return to the soil, and we will all be raised from the soil once again.

The return of all of us to the soil or being resurrected from the soil is perfectly clear. But as for how the beginning of all of us is from the soil, there are two interpretations: first, that we are all from Adam and Adam was from soil; and second, that even we ourselves are taken from the soil, because all the food substances that formed our bodies and those of our fathers and mothers were taken from this very soil.

Furthermore, this expression is a warning to all the arrogant and Pharaoh-like people not to forget where they came from and where they will go. Why all this pride, vanity, arrogance, and rebellion for a being who was soil yesterday and will become soil again tomorrow?

Key Points

  1. The word "Mahd" and "Mihad" both mean a place prepared for sitting, sleeping, and resting. Originally, the word "Mahd" refers to the place where a child is laid to sleep (a cradle or the like). It is as if man is a child entrusted to the cradle of the earth, and in this cradle, all his means of life and nutrition are provided.
  2. The word "Azwajan," derived from the root zawj, can refer to the categories and types of plants, as well as a subtle reference to the issue of pairing (being male and female) in the plant kingdom, which, God willing, we will discuss under more appropriate verses.
  3. In the commentary on "Ulul-Nuha," we read in a Hadith narrated in Usul al-Kafi from the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family): "The best of you are the Ulul-Nuha." It was asked, "O Messenger of Allah, who are the Ulul-Nuha?" He said: "They are those who possess good character and weighty intellects, who maintain family ties, are kind to mothers and fathers, look after the poor, neighbors, and orphans, feed the hungry, spread peace in the world, and pray while people are asleep and heedless." [^1]

And in another Hadith from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (peace be upon him), it is narrated that a person asked him about the meaning of the two prostrations in each unit (rak'ah) of prayer. The Imam said: "The meaning of the first prostration when you put your head on the ground is: 'O Lord, I was originally from this soil.' When you raise your head, its meaning is: 'You brought me out from this soil.' The meaning of the second prostration is: 'You will return me to this soil.' And when you raise your head from the second prostration, its meaning is: 'You will resurrect me from the soil once again.'" [^2]

[^1]: Usul al-Kafi, Volume 2, Chapter: The Believer, His Signs and Attributes, Hadith 32. [^2]: Bihar al-Anwar, New Edition, Vol. 85, p. 132.

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ أَرَيْنَـٰهُ ءَايَـٰتِنَا كُلَّهَا فَكَذَّبَ وَأَبَىٰ (٥٦)
Certainly We showed him all Our signs. But he denied [them] and refused [to believe them]. (56)
قَالَ أَجِئْتَنَا لِتُخْرِجَنَا مِنْ أَرْضِنَا بِسِحْرِكَ يَـٰمُوسَىٰ (٥٧)
He said, ‘Moses, have you come to us to expel us from our land with your magic? (57)
فَلَنَأْتِيَنَّكَ بِسِحْرٍ مِّثْلِهِۦ فَٱجْعَلْ بَيْنَنَا وَبَيْنَكَ مَوْعِدًا لَّا نُخْلِفُهُۥ نَحْنُ وَلَآ أَنتَ مَكَانًا سُوًى (٥٨)
Yet we [too] will bring you a magic like it! So fix a tryst between us and you, which neither we shall fail nor you, at a middle place.’ (58)
قَالَ مَوْعِدُكُمْ يَوْمُ ٱلزِّينَةِ وَأَن يُحْشَرَ ٱلنَّاسُ ضُحًى (٥٩)
He said, ‘Your tryst shall be the Day of Adornment, and let the people be assembled in early forenoon.’ (59)
فَتَوَلَّىٰ فِرْعَوْنُ فَجَمَعَ كَيْدَهُۥ ثُمَّ أَتَىٰ (٦٠)
Then Pharaoh withdrew [to consult privately], summoned up his guile, and then arrived [at the scene of the contest]. (60)
قَالَ لَهُم مُّوسَىٰ وَيْلَكُمْ لَا تَفْتَرُوا۟ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ كَذِبًا فَيُسْحِتَكُم بِعَذَابٍ ۖ وَقَدْ خَابَ مَنِ ٱفْتَرَىٰ (٦١)
Moses said to them, ‘Woe to you! Do not fabricate lies against Allah, or He will obliterate you with a punishment. Whoever fabricates lies certainly fails.’ (61)
فَتَنَـٰزَعُوٓا۟ أَمْرَهُم بَيْنَهُمْ وَأَسَرُّوا۟ ٱلنَّجْوَىٰ (٦٢)
So they disputed their matter among themselves, and kept their confidential talks secret. (62)
قَالُوٓا۟ إِنْ هَـٰذَٰنِ لَسَـٰحِرَٰنِ يُرِيدَانِ أَن يُخْرِجَاكُم مِّنْ أَرْضِكُم بِسِحْرِهِمَا وَيَذْهَبَا بِطَرِيقَتِكُمُ ٱلْمُثْلَىٰ (٦٣)
They said, ‘These two are indeed magicians who intend to expel you from your land with their magic, and to abolish your excellent tradition! (63)
فَأَجْمِعُوا۟ كَيْدَكُمْ ثُمَّ ٱئْتُوا۟ صَفًّا ۚ وَقَدْ أَفْلَحَ ٱلْيَوْمَ مَنِ ٱسْتَعْلَىٰ (٦٤)
So summon up your ingenuity, then come in ranks. Today he who has the upper hand will be felicitous!’ (64)

Commentary

Pharaoh Prepares Himself for the Final Struggle

In this section of the verses, another stage of the conflict between Moses (عليه السلام) and Pharaoh is reflected. The Holy Quran begins this part with this sentence: "We showed Pharaoh all Our signs, but he did not allow any of them to affect his darkened heart; he denied them all and refused to accept them" (وَلَقَدْ أَرَيْنَاهُ آيَاتِنَا كُلَّهَا فَكَذَّبَ وَأَبَى).

Certainly, "all Our signs" here does not refer to every single miracle that appeared through Moses throughout his life in Egypt. Rather, it refers to the miracles he presented to Pharaoh at the beginning of his mission: the "Miracle of the Staff," the "White Hand," and the "comprehensive content of his heavenly message," which was itself a living proof of his truthfulness.

Consequently, after this event, we encounter the issue of the magicians' struggle against Moses (عليه السلام) and his new miracles.

Now let us see what the rebellious, arrogant, and stubborn Pharaoh said in response to Moses and his miracles, and how he accused him—as is the custom of all tyrannical rulers. "He said: 'O Moses! Have you come to drive us out of our land and homeland with your magic?'" (قَالَ أَجِئْتَنَا لِتُخْرِجَنَا مِنْ أَرْضِنَا بِسِحْرِكَ يَا مُوسَى).

This implies: "We know that the issue of prophethood, the call to monotheism, and the presentation of these miracles are all a conspiracy to overcome the government and expel us and the Copts from the land of our forefathers. Your intention is neither the call to monotheism nor the salvation of the Children of Israel; your intention is governance, dominion over this land, and the expulsion of your opponents!"

This accusation is the exact same weapon that all tyrants and colonizers throughout history have wielded. Whenever they felt themselves in danger, they would raise the issue of a threat to the country to incite the people in their own favor. To them, "the country" means the rule of these tyrants, and its "existence" means their own existence! Some commentators believe that bringing the Children of Israel to Egypt and keeping them in that land was not solely to exploit their labor as slaves; rather, they also wanted to ensure that this powerful nation did not gain strength, lest they become a center of danger. The command to kill their sons was not only due to the fear of Moses' birth, but also to prevent their power and strength—a tactic employed by all bullies. Therefore, the departure of the Children of Israel according to Moses' demand meant the empowerment of this nation, in which case the throne of the Pharaohs would be endangered.

Another point is that in this short phrase, Pharaoh accused Moses of magic—the same accusation leveled against all prophets in the face of their clear miracles.

As we read in Surah Adh-Dhariyat, verses 52 and 53: كَذَلِكَ مَا أَتَى الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ مِنْ رَسُولٍ إِلَّا قَالُوا سَاحِرٌ أَوْ مَجْنُونٌ أَتَوَاصَوْا بِهِ بَلْ هُمْ قَوْمٌ طَاغُونَ: "Similarly, no messenger came to those before them but they said: 'A magician or a madman!' Have they handed down this (slander) to one another? Nay, they are a rebellious people."

It is also noteworthy that fanning the flames of "patriotism" in such situations is a calculated move, as most people love their land and homeland as they love their own lives. Thus, in some verses of the Quran, these two are placed side by side: وَلَوْ أَنَّا كَتَبْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ أَنِ اقْتُلُوا أَنْفُسَكُمْ أَوِ اخْرُجُوا مِنْ دِيَارِكُمْ مَا فَعَلُوهُ إِلَّا قَلِيلٌ مِنْهُمْ: "If We had decreed for them, 'Kill yourselves' or 'Leave your homes,' they would not have done it, except for a few of them" [1].

Pharaoh then added: "Do not think we are unable to produce magic similar to yours; 'know for certain that we will soon bring you magic like it'" (فَلَنَأْتِيَنَّكَ بِسِحْرٍ مِثْلِهِ).

To show even greater decisiveness, he said: "Set a date right now; there must be an appointment between us and you which neither we nor you shall fail to keep, and that in a place that is equal for all" (فَاجْعَلْ بَيْنَنَا وَبَيْنَكَ مَوْعِدًا لَا نُخْلِفُهُ نَحْنُ وَلَا أَنْتَ مَكَانًا سُوًى).

In the interpretation of "مَكَانًا سُوًى" (a place equal/level), some have said it means a place equidistant from both parties. Others have said it means a place equidistant for the people of the city—that is, a location exactly in the center of the city. Some have said it refers to a flat land where everyone has a clear view, and the high and low are equal therein. All these meanings can be combined.

It is also necessary to note that tyrannical rulers, in order to drive their opponent from the field and to give strength, power, and morale to their entourage—who are sometimes influenced (and in the story of Moses and his miracles, they certainly were)—act with apparent decisiveness regarding such matters and create a great deal of noise!

But Moses, without losing his composure or fearing Pharaoh's clamor, said with clarity and decisiveness: "I too am ready; I shall set the day and hour right now. Our appointment with you shall be the Day of Adornment (the festival day), provided that all the people are gathered together when the sun has risen high" (قَالَ مَوْعِدُكُمْ يَوْمُ الزِّينَةِ وَأَنْ يُحْشَرَ النَّاسُ ضُحًى) [1].

[1]: An-Nisa, 66. [1]: "Duha" (ضُحًى) literally means the spreading of sunlight or the rising of the sun. The "waw" in the phrase "وَأَنْ يُحْشَرَ النَّاسُ ضُحًى" indicates accompaniment (ma'iyyah).

The expression "يَوْمُ الزِّينَةِ" (Day of Adornment) certainly refers to a festival day which we cannot precisely identify, but the important point is that people would cease their work and business on that day and were naturally prepared to attend such an event.

In any case, after witnessing Moses' wondrous miracles and seeing the psychological impact of these miracles on his entourage, Pharaoh decided to rise against him with the help of magicians. Therefore, he made the necessary arrangement with Moses: "Pharaoh withdrew, gathered his plot, and then came (on the appointed day)" (فَتَوَلَّى فِرْعَوْنُ فَجَمَعَ كَيْدَهُ ثُمَّ أَتَى).

In this short sentence, the detailed accounts found extensively in Surah Al-A'raf and Ash-Shu'ara are summarized. After leaving that assembly and separating from Moses and Aaron, Pharaoh held various meetings with his special advisors and arrogant entourage. Then, he summoned magicians from all over Egypt to the capital and invited them to this fateful struggle with many incentives, along with other matters not discussed here. However, the Quran has gathered all of this in these three phrases: (Pharaoh withdrew, gathered his plot, and then came) [1].

Finally, the appointed day arrived. Moses (عليه السلام) stood before a massive crowd—a crowd that included a group of magicians, whose number according to some commentators was 72, while others say it reached 400, and some have mentioned even larger numbers.

Another group consisted of Pharaoh and his entourage, and finally, the third group, which formed the majority, was the masses of spectating people.

Here, Moses turned to the magicians, or to the Pharaoh's people and the magicians, and said to them: "Woe to you! Do not forge a lie against Allah, lest He destroy and root you out with a punishment" (قَالَ لَهُمْ مُوسَى وَيْلَكُمْ لَا تَفْتَرُوا عَلَى اللَّهِ كَذِبًا فَيُسْحِتَكُمْ بِعَذَابٍ).

"And failure, despair, and loss belong to those who forge lies against Allah and attribute falsehood to Him" (وَقَدْ خَابَ مَنِ افْتَرَى).

It is clear that by "forging a lie against Allah," Moses meant associating someone or something as a partner to Him, attributing the miracles of Allah's messenger to magic, and considering Pharaoh as their deity and god. Certainly, whoever forges such lies against Allah and strives with all their might to extinguish the light of truth, Allah will not leave such people without punishment.

This decisive speech of Moses, which bore no resemblance to the speech of magicians but rather had the tone of the call of all true prophets and arose from Moses' pure heart, affected some hearts. Dispute broke out among the crowd; some favored harsh action, while others fell into doubt and considered the possibility that Moses might be a great prophet of God and that his threats might be effective. Especially since his simple clothing and that of his brother Aaron was the same simple clothing of a shepherd, and their determined faces—which showed no weakness or frailty despite being alone—were considered another proof of the authenticity of their words and programs. Therefore, the Quran says: "So they disputed their affair among themselves and kept their talk private" (فَتَنَازَعُوا أَمْرَهُمْ بَيْنَهُمْ وَأَسَرُّوا النَّجْوَى).

It is possible that this private talk and whispering was in the presence of Moses; it is also possible it was in the presence of Pharaoh; and another possibility is that the organizers of this scene engaged in such whispering and dispute away from the masses of the people.

In any case, the proponents of continuing the struggle and taking harsh action prevailed. They took control of the discourse and began to incite those struggling against Moses through various means.

First: "They said: 'These two are certainly magicians!'" (قَالُوا إِنْ هَذَانِ لَسَاحِرَانِ) [1]. Therefore, one should not harbor any fear of fighting them, for you are the masters and leaders of magic in this vast country, and your power and strength are greater than theirs!

Second: "They want to drive you out of your land with their magic"—a land that is as dear to you as your own lives, to which you belong and which belongs to you (يُرِيدَانِ أَنْ يُخْرِجَاكُمْ مِنْ أَرْضِكُمْ بِسِحْرِهِمَا).

Furthermore, they are not satisfied with just expelling you from your homeland; they want to make a mockery of your sanctities "and do away with your superior way of life and true religion!" (وَيَذْهَبَا بِطَرِيقَتِكُمُ الْمُثْلَى) [1].

Now that this is the case, do not allow any doubt into yourselves, and "gather your plot, plan, skill, and power" (فَأَجْمِعُوا كَيْدَكُمْ).

"Then come forward united in a single rank to the field of struggle" (ثُمَّ ائْتُوا صَفًّا).

For unity and union are the secrets of your victory in this fateful struggle.

And finally, "success and prosperity today belong to the one who can prove his superiority over his opponent" (وَقَدْ أَفْلَحَ الْيَوْمَ مَنِ اسْتَعْلَى).

[1]: Although the word "tawalla" (تولى) is interpreted here as separating from Moses or from that assembly, it may also reflect Pharaoh's state of protest and annoyance and his hostile stance against Moses, given the concept of this word. [1]: Grammatically, "in" (إِنْ) here is the lightened form of "inna" (أَنَّ), which is why it has not acted upon what follows it. Furthermore, the nominative case (raf') of the noun of "inna" is not unknown or rare in the Arabic language. [1]: "Tariqah" (طريقة) means method, and here it refers to religion. "Muthla" (مثلى) is from the root "mathal" and here means superior and best (i.e., that which most resembles virtue).

Ayahs

قَالُوا۟ يَـٰمُوسَىٰٓ إِمَّآ أَن تُلْقِىَ وَإِمَّآ أَن نَّكُونَ أَوَّلَ مَنْ أَلْقَىٰ (٦٥)
They said, ‘O Moses! Will you throw first, or shall we?’ (65)
قَالَ بَلْ أَلْقُوا۟ ۖ فَإِذَا حِبَالُهُمْ وَعِصِيُّهُمْ يُخَيَّلُ إِلَيْهِ مِن سِحْرِهِمْ أَنَّهَا تَسْعَىٰ (٦٦)
He said, ‘No, you throw first.’ Thereupon, behold, their ropes and staffs appeared to him by their magic to wriggle swiftly. (66)
فَأَوْجَسَ فِى نَفْسِهِۦ خِيفَةً مُّوسَىٰ (٦٧)
Then Moses felt a fear within his heart. (67)
قُلْنَا لَا تَخَفْ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ ٱلْأَعْلَىٰ (٦٨)
We said, ‘Do not be afraid. Indeed, you will have the upper hand. (68)
وَأَلْقِ مَا فِى يَمِينِكَ تَلْقَفْ مَا صَنَعُوٓا۟ ۖ إِنَّمَا صَنَعُوا۟ كَيْدُ سَـٰحِرٍ ۖ وَلَا يُفْلِحُ ٱلسَّاحِرُ حَيْثُ أَتَىٰ (٦٩)
Throw down what is in your right hand, and it will swallow what they have conjured. What they have conjured is only a magician’s trick, and the magician does not fare well wherever he may show up.’ (69)

Commentary

Musa (ع) Also Enters the Arena

The magicians apparently united and solidified their resolve to rise in combat against Musa. When they stepped into the arena, "They said: 'O Musa! Will you throw [your staff] first, or shall we be the first to throw?'" (قَالُوا يَا مُوسَىٰ إِمَّا أَن تُلْقِيَ وَإِمَّا أَن نَّكُونَ أَوَّلَ مَنْ أَلْقَىٰ).

Some commentators have suggested that this proposal by the magicians—offering Musa the choice to go first—was a form of respect toward him, and perhaps it was this very act that paved the way for their eventual success in attaining faith after the event.

However, this seems quite unlikely, as they were striving with all their might to crush Musa and his miracle. Therefore, the aforementioned expression was perhaps intended to demonstrate their self-confidence before the masses of people.

But Musa, showing no haste—since he was completely certain of his ultimate victory, and even regardless of that, in such contests the winner is usually the one who does not initiate—said to them: "Nay, you throw [first]!" (قَالَ بَلْ أَلْقُوا). Undoubtedly, this invitation by Musa (ع) for them to begin the combat was, in reality, a prelude to the manifestation of the Truth. From Musa's (ع) perspective, this was not only not a reprehensible act, but was considered a necessary preliminary.

The magicians accepted and cast down all the staffs and ropes they had brought for their sorcery into the middle of the arena. If we accept the narration stating they numbered in the thousands, it means that in a single moment, thousands of staffs and ropes—containing specific substances stored within them—were thrown into the center of the field.

"Then suddenly their ropes and their staffs, by their magic, appeared to him as though they were moving!" (فَإِذا حِبالُهُمْ وَ عِصِيُّهُمْ يُخَيَّلُ إِلَيْهِ مِنْ سِحْرِهِمْ أَنَّها تَسْعى‌).

Indeed, they began to writhe and move like snakes, small and large, multicolored and in various forms. We read in other verses of the Quran regarding this: "They bewitched the eyes of the people and struck terror into them, and they produced a mighty magic" (سَحَرُوا أَعْيُنَ النَّاسِ وَ اسْتَرْهَبُوهُمْ وَ جاؤُ بِسِحْرٍ عَظِيمٍ - Al-A'raf: 116).

And as expressed in verse 44 of Surah Ash-Shu'ara: "The magicians cried out: 'By the might of Pharaoh, it is we who shall be the victors!'" (وَ قالُوا بِعِزَّةِ فِرْعَوْنَ إِنَّا لَنَحْنُ الْغالِبُونَ‌).

Many commentators have written that they had placed substances such as "mercury" inside these ropes and staffs which, upon being heated by the sun's rays, caused this highly volatile substance to produce various rapid movements. These movements were certainly not actual walking, but through the suggestions the magicians had made to the people and the specific atmosphere created there, it was visualized in the eyes of the people as if these objects had come to life and were moving! (The expression "سَحَرُوا أَعْيُنَ النَّاسِ‌", meaning "they bewitched the eyes of the people," refers to this very meaning, as does the expression "يُخَيَّلُ إِلَيْهِ‌", meaning "it appeared to Musa"—it may also point to this same concept).

In any case, it was a very strange scene. The magicians, who were both numerous and highly skilled in this craft—knowing well how to utilize the mysterious physical and chemical properties of objects and the like—were able to influence the minds of those present to such an extent that they believed all these inanimate objects had come to life.

A shout of joy rose from the Pharaoh's followers; some cried out in fear and terror and pulled themselves back!

"At this, Musa felt a slight fear within his heart" (فَأَوْجَسَ فِي نَفْسِهِ خِيفَةً مُوسى‌). "Oujasa" is derived from "Ijas," which originally comes from "Wajs" (rhyming with Habs), meaning a hidden sound. Therefore, "Ijas" means a hidden and internal feeling. This expression shows that Musa's internal fear was superficial and slight. Furthermore, it was not because he granted any importance to the terrifying scene created by the magicians' sorcery, but rather he feared that the people might be so influenced by this scene that bringing them back to the truth would not be easy.

Or perhaps he feared that before he had the chance to show his miracle, a group might leave the scene or be driven away, and the Truth would not be made manifest.

As we read in Sermon 6 of Nahj al-Balagha: "Musa (ع) never felt fear within himself for his own sake, but rather he feared the dominance of the ignorant and the triumph of the states of misguidance" (لم يوجس موسى (ع) خيفة على نفسه بل اشفق من غلبة الجهال و دول الضلال). [1]

[1] Ali (ع) spoke these words at a time when he was concerned about the deviation of the people, pointing to the fact that: "My concern is not because I have any doubt in the Truth—for I have not doubted the Truth for a moment since I first saw it—but rather I am concerned about the deviation of the people!"

With what has been said, we see no necessity for the other explanations mentioned regarding the fear of Musa (ع).

In any case, at this moment, Divine help and victory came to Musa, and the command of Revelation defined his duty, as the Quran says: "We said: 'Fear not! Surely you are the superior one!'" (قُلْنا لا تَخَفْ إِنَّكَ أَنْتَ الْأَعْلى‌). This sentence, with absolute decisiveness, reassures Musa of his victory (the word "Inna" and the repetition of the "pronoun" are each independent emphases of this meaning, as is the nominal structure of the sentence). In this way, Musa regained his strength of heart, which had wavered for a few brief moments.

He was addressed again: "And throw that which is in your right hand; it will swallow up all that they have crafted!" (وَ أَلْقِ ما فِي يَمِينِكَ تَلْقَفْ ما صَنَعُوا). "For what they have crafted is but a magician's trick" (إِنَّما صَنَعُوا كَيْدُ ساحِرٍ).

"And the magician shall not succeed, wherever he may go" (وَ لا يُفْلِحُ السَّاحِرُ حَيْثُ أَتى‌).

"Talqaf" is derived from "Laqf" (rhyming with Waqf), meaning to swallow. However, Raghib in Al-Mufradat says: This word originally means to take something with skill, whether by mouth or by hand, and some linguists have defined it as "taking up quickly," which in Persian is translated as "snatching" (robudan).

It is interesting that He does not say "throw your staff," but rather "throw that which is in your right hand." This expression is perhaps intended to show indifference toward the staff itself, indicating that the staff is not the important matter; what is important is the Will and Command of Allah. If He wills it, not only a staff but even something smaller and more insignificant can demonstrate such power!

It is also worth noting that "magician" (sahir) in the above verse appears first in the indefinite form and later in the definite form with the definite article "Al" denoting the genus. This difference is perhaps because the goal in the first instance is to show indifference toward the work of these specific magicians—the meaning being that what they did is nothing more than a magician's trick. But in the second instance, it intends to convey the reality that not only these magicians, but any magician who appears at any time or place, will never be successful or prosperous.

Key Points

1- What is the Reality of Magic?

1- Although we have discussed this in detail previously, it is appropriate to mention a few sentences here as a brief explanation: "Magic" (Sihr) originally refers to any act or thing whose source is hidden and concealed. However, in everyday language, it refers to extraordinary acts performed using various means.

Sometimes it is purely a matter of trickery, deception, sleight of hand, and prestidigitation. Sometimes suggestive factors are utilized. Sometimes it uses unknown physical and chemical properties of certain objects and substances. And sometimes it is through seeking help from devils. All of these are included in the comprehensive linguistic concept.

Throughout history, we encounter many stories regarding magic and magicians, and even now in our era, those who engage in such acts are not few. However, because many properties of entities that were hidden from the masses in the past have become manifest in our time—and books have even been written about the amazing effects of various beings—a large portion of the magicians' magic has been taken out of their hands.

For example, in modern chemistry, we know many substances that are lighter than air; if placed inside an object, that object might move, and no one would be surprised. Even many modern children's toys might have seemed like a type of magic in the past! Today, in circuses, they perform shows similar to the magic of ancient magicians; using the properties of light, mirrors, and physical and chemical properties of objects, they create strange and wonderful scenes that sometimes leave spectators agape with wonder.

Of course, the extraordinary acts of ascetics (mortazān) are another story entirely, which is very astonishing. In any case, magic is not something whose existence can be denied or attributed to mere superstition, whether in the past or today.

A point worthy of note: Magic in Islam is forbidden and is among the major sins, because in many cases, it leads to misleading people, distorting truths, and shaking the foundations of the beliefs of simple-minded individuals. Of course, this Islamic ruling, like many others, has exceptions—including learning magic to nullify the claims of false claimants to prophethood, or to remove its effects from those who have been harmed by it. We have also discussed this in detail in Volume 1 under verses 102 and 103 of Surah Al-Baqarah.

2- Does a Magician Never Succeed?

Many ask: if magicians can perform extraordinary acts similar to miracles, how can one distinguish between their works and a miracle? And how can "magic not compete with a miracle"? The answer to this question becomes clear by noting one point: the work of a magician relies on limited human power, whereas a miracle stems from the infinite and eternal Power of Allah.

Therefore, every magician can only perform limited acts, and if something beyond that is requested, he remains helpless. He can only perform acts he has previously practiced, mastered, and whose intricacies he knows; otherwise, he will be completely incapable. In contrast, because the Prophets sought help from the eternal Power of God, they were capable of performing any kind of extraordinary act, in the earth or the heavens, of any type or sort.

A magician can never perform an extraordinary act according to the specific proposal of the people, unless it happens to coincide with his work (though sometimes they instruct unknown friends to rise from the crowd and propose pre-determined suggestions as if they were spontaneous).

But the Prophets, time and again, performed significant miracles that truth-seeking people requested as proof of prophethood, just as we will observe in this very story of Musa.

Furthermore, because magic is a deviant act and a type of deception and trickery, it naturally requires a corresponding psychological state. Magicians, without exception, are deceptive and fraudulent individuals who can be recognized very quickly by studying their psychology and actions. In contrast, the sincerity, purity, and integrity of the Prophets is a testament that is intertwined with their miracles and doubles their effect. (Reflect on this). Perhaps it is for these reasons that the above verses say: "And the magician shall not succeed, wherever he may go" (وَ لا يُفْلِحُ السَّاحِرُ حَيْثُ أَتى‌). A magician, wherever he may be and in whatever circumstances or time, will not be prosperous, and as the saying goes, his secret will soon be exposed, because his power is limited and his thoughts and attributes are deviant.

This subject is not exclusive to the magicians who rose to fight the Prophets, but is true of magicians in general—they are soon recognized and do not achieve victory.

Ayahs

فَأُلْقِىَ ٱلسَّحَرَةُ سُجَّدًا قَالُوٓا۟ ءَامَنَّا بِرَبِّ هَـٰرُونَ وَمُوسَىٰ (٧٠)
Thereat the magicians fell down prostrating. They said, ‘We have believed in the Lord of Aaron and Moses!’ (70)
