Commentary

The Great Victory of Moses (عليه السلام)

In the preceding verses, we reached the point where Moses was commanded to cast down his staff to destroy the apparatus of the magicians' sorcery.

In the verses under discussion, this matter is pursued, though sentences that were clear have been omitted (Moses cast his staff, the staff turned into a great serpent and swallowed all the tools and instruments of the magicians' sorcery; a clamor and tumult fell upon the entire crowd; Pharaoh became severely terrified, and his entourage stood with their mouths open in astonishment.

The magicians, who until that moment had never encountered such a scene and well knew how to distinguish sorcery from non-sorcery, became certain that this matter was nothing but a divine miracle, and that this man was a messenger of God calling them toward their Lord. A storm arose in their hearts and a great revolution appeared in their souls).

Now we hear the continuation of the account from the language of the verses:

"The magicians fell down in prostration and said: 'We believe in the Lord of Aaron and Moses'" (فَأُلْقِيَ السَّحَرَةُ سُجَّداً قالُوا آمَنَّا بِرَبِّ هارُونَ وَ مُوسى‌).

The expression "Ulqiya" (using the passive verb, meaning "were cast down") seemingly points to the fact that they were so captivated by Moses and influenced by his miracle that it was as if they fell into prostration involuntarily.

This point is also noteworthy: they did not suffice merely with believing, but saw it as their duty to declare this faith clearly and with phrases in which there was no ambiguity—that is, with emphasis on the Lord of Moses and Aaron—so that if anyone had been led astray by their work, they might return, and thus no responsibility would remain upon their shoulders!

It is obvious that this action of the magicians dealt a heavy blow to the body of Pharaoh and his tyrannical, autocratic, and oppressive government, shaking its very foundations.

For a long time, propaganda regarding this issue had been spread throughout Egypt, magicians had been gathered from every corner, and every kind of reward and privilege had been promised to them in the event of victory.

But now he observes that those who were in the front line of the struggle suddenly surrendered to the enemy—not just surrendered, but became his staunch defenders. This was a matter that Pharaoh could never have predicted, and undoubtedly, a group of people also became attached to Moses and his religion following the magicians.

Therefore, Pharaoh saw no choice but to gather the remnants of the dignity he did not possess through shouting and severe, heavy threats. He turned to the magicians and "said: 'Did you believe in him before I gave you permission?!'" (قالَ آمَنْتُمْ لَهُ قَبْلَ أَنْ آذَنَ لَكُمْ‌).

This arrogant tyrant not only claimed to rule over the bodies and souls of the people but wanted to say that your hearts are also at my disposal and belong to me, and must decide with my permission. This is the same thing that all Pharaohs in every age and time advocate for.

Some, like the Pharaoh of Egypt, utter it clumsily in a state of panic, while others, mysteriously and by using means of propaganda, mass communication, and various types of censorship, practically claim this right for themselves and believe that people should not be allowed independent thought; rather, sometimes even in the name of freedom of thought, this deprivation of freedom must be imposed upon the people.

In any case, Pharaoh did not stop at this; he immediately attached a label to the magicians and accused them, saying: "He is your chief, he is the one who taught you sorcery," and all of this is a conspiracy with a prior plan!! (إِنَّهُ لَكَبِيرُكُمُ الَّذِي عَلَّمَكُمُ السِّحْرَ).

Undoubtedly, Pharaoh knew and was certain that this statement was a lie. Fundamentally, such a conspiracy that would encompass all of Egypt while his secret agents and spies remained unaware of it was impossible. Principally, Pharaoh had raised Moses in his own lap, and his absence from Egypt was certain to him; if he were the chief of the magicians of Egypt, he would have been famous everywhere by this title, and it was not something that could be hidden.

But we know that bullies and tyrants, when they see their illegitimate position in danger, fear no lie or slander.

Furthermore, he did not stop at this either and threatened the magicians with death in the severest tone, saying: "I swear that I will cut off your hands and feet on opposite sides, and I will crucify you upon the high trunks of palm trees, so that you may know whose punishment is more painful and more lasting—mine or the punishment of the God of Moses and Aaron" (فَلَأُقَطِّعَنَّ أَيْدِيَكُمْ وَ أَرْجُلَكُمْ مِنْ خِلافٍ وَ لَأُصَلِّبَنَّكُمْ فِي جُذُوعِ النَّخْلِ وَ لَتَعْلَمُنَّ أَيُّنا أَشَدُّ عَذاباً وَ أَبْقى‌). [1] In reality, the phrase "which of us is more severe in punishment" refers to the threat that Moses had previously made, specifically warning the magicians before this event that if they forged a lie against Allah, He would uproot them with His punishment.

The expression "on opposite sides" (cutting off hands and feet crosswise) refers to the right hand with the left foot or vice versa. Perhaps the choice of this type of torture for the magicians was because, in this state, a person dies more slowly—

[1]: It is well known that "fi" (in) in the phrase "in the trunks of palm trees" means "ala" (upon), meaning "I will crucify you upon the palm trees." However, Fakhr Razi believes that "fi" here retains its own meaning, because "fi" is for "vessel-hood" (containment), and the containment of everything is appropriate to it; we know that the gallows is like a vessel for the one who is hanged (but this justification does not seem very correct).

—meaning bleeding occurs more slowly and they will experience more torture. Additionally, it is as if he wants to say: I will mutilate both sides of your body.

As for the threat of crucifying them on palm trees, it was perhaps because these trees are among the tallest trees, and everyone from far and near can see someone hanging from them.

It is also noteworthy that in the custom of that time, hanging was not as it is common in our custom; they did not put the noose around the neck of the person they wanted to hang, but rather tied it to the hands or shoulders so that he would suffer a lingering death.

But let us see what the reaction of the magicians was to these severe threats from Pharaoh. They were not only not intimidated, did not flinch, and did not leave the field, but they established their presence on the scene more decisively and "said: 'By the God who created us, we will never prefer you over these clear proofs that have come to us'" (قالُوا لَنْ نُؤْثِرَكَ عَلى‌ ما جاءَنا مِنَ الْبَيِّناتِ وَ الَّذِي فَطَرَنا). "So decree whatever you desire to decree" (فَاقْضِ ما أَنْتَ قاضٍ‌).

But know that you can only judge in the life of this world (whereas in the Hereafter we are the victors and you are caught and afflicted with the severest punishments) (إِنَّما تَقْضِي هذِهِ الْحَياةَ الدُّنْيا).

In this way, they expressed three crushing sentences against Pharaoh: first, be certain that we will not exchange the guidance we have found for anything; second, we have absolutely no fear of your threats; and third, the realm of your rule and activity is only these few days of the world.

Then they added: "If you see that we have believed in our Lord, it is so that He may forgive us our sins" (we have committed many sins through sorcery and magic) (إِنَّا آمَنَّا بِرَبِّنا لِيَغْفِرَ لَنا خَطايانا).

And also "that He may encompass us in His mercy for this great sin that you forced upon us (sorcery against the Prophet of God), and Allah is better and more lasting" (وَ ما أَكْرَهْتَنا عَلَيْهِ مِنَ السِّحْرِ وَ اللَّهُ خَيْرٌ وَ أَبْقى‌).

In short, our goal is to be purified from past sins, including fighting against the true Prophet of God; through this path, we want to reach eternal felicity, while you threaten us with the death of this world. We accept this small loss in exchange for that great good!

Here a question arises: apparently, the magicians stepped into this field of their own will, even though Pharaoh had given them many promises; how is the term "compulsion" (ikrah) used in the above verse?

In response, we say: there is no evidence that the magicians were not forced to accept this invitation from the beginning. Rather, the appearance of the phrase "bring to you every learned magician" (the officials must go and bring every knowledgeable magician) (Al-A'raf: 112) is that the knowledgeable magicians were obliged to accept. Of course, under the conditions of Pharaoh's autocratic and tyrannical government, it seems quite natural that he would move individuals by force toward his intentions. As for setting a prize and the like to encourage them, it does not contradict this meaning, for we have often seen that oppressive, bullying governments use material incentives alongside the use of force.

The possibility has also been raised that in the first encounter of the magicians with Moses (عليه السلام), it became clear to them through certain signs that Moses (عليه السلام) was in the right, or at least they fell into doubt and hesitation, and for this reason, "dispute" arose among them, as we read in verse 62 of this same Surah: "So they disputed their affair among themselves."

Pharaoh and his apparatus became aware of this and forced them to continue the struggle.

The magicians then continued: If we have believed, the reason is clear, "for whoever comes to the presence of the Lord on the Day of Resurrection as a faithless sinner, for him is the burning fire of Hell" (إِنَّهُ مَنْ يَأْتِ رَبَّهُ مُجْرِماً فَإِنَّ لَهُ جَهَنَّمَ‌). And his great misery in Hell is that "he neither dies nor lives" (لا يَمُوتُ فِيها وَ لا يَحْيى‌).

Rather, he constantly struggles between death and life—a life that is more bitter and more arduous than death.

"And whoever enters that great presence with faith and righteous deeds, high ranks await him" (وَ مَنْ يَأْتِهِ مُؤْمِناً قَدْ عَمِلَ الصَّالِحاتِ فَأُولئِكَ لَهُمُ الدَّرَجاتُ الْعُلى‌).

"Gardens of eternity beneath which rivers flow, and they will abide therein forever" (جَنَّاتُ عَدْنٍ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ خالِدِينَ فِيها). "And that is the reward of him who purifies himself through faith and obedience to his Lord" (وَ ذلِكَ جَزاءُ مَنْ تَزَكَّى‌).

There is a discussion among commentators as to whether these last three verses are a continuation of the magicians' speech before Pharaoh or independent sentences from Allah mentioned here as a completion of their words.

A group considers them a continuation of the magicians' speech, and perhaps the beginning with "Innahu" (Indeed he), which is actually to state a cause, supports this view.

However, the elaboration provided in these three verses regarding the fate of the righteous believers and the criminal disbelievers, ending with the phrase "And that is the reward of him who purifies himself," as well as the descriptions of Paradise and Hell therein, supports the second view—that these are from the words of Allah. This is because the magicians would have had to acquire a vast portion of divine knowledge and awareness in this short time to be able to judge so decisively and knowingly about Paradise, Hell, and the fate of believers and criminals.

Unless we say that Allah caused these profound words to flow from their tongues because of their faith—though from the perspective of divine upbringing and the result for us, it makes no difference whether Allah said it or believers taught by Allah, especially since the Quran narrates it all with an approving tone.

Key Points

1- Knowledge is the Source of Faith and Revolution

The most important issue seen in the above verses is the deep and rapid transformation of the magicians in the face of Moses. When they stood before Moses, they were his staunch enemies, but upon witnessing Moses' first miracle, they were so shaken, awakened, and changed course that everyone was plunged into astonishment.

This rapid and immediate change of course from disbelief to faith, from deviation to correctness and steadfastness, from crookedness to straightness, and from darkness to light, caught everyone so off guard that perhaps even for Pharaoh, it was unbelievable. Therefore, he tried to attribute it to a previously calculated conspiracy, while he himself knew this attribution was a lie.

What factor caused this deep and rapid transformation? What factor caused the light of faith to shine so powerfully in their hearts that they were even ready to stake their entire existence and being on this matter—and according to historical accounts, they did, for Pharaoh carried out his threat and martyred them in a brutal manner.

Do we know of any factor here other than knowledge and awareness? Because they were familiar with the techniques and secrets of sorcery, and clearly realized that Moses' program was not sorcery but a divine miracle, they changed course so bravely and decisively. From this, we well understand that to transform deviant individuals or societies and bring about a rapid and true revolution, one must first and foremost give them awareness. [1]

2- We Will Not Prefer You Over the "Clear Proofs"

Interestingly, they chose the most logical expression against the illogical Pharaoh. First, they said: We have found clear, manifest proofs for the truthfulness of Moses and his divine call, and we will prefer nothing over these clear proofs. Then, with the phrase "By Him who created us," they emphasized this point, which, considering the word "fatara-na" (created us), seemingly refers to their monotheistic nature (fitrah). That is, we see the light of monotheism from within our souls as well as from the proof of reason; with these manifest proofs, how can we abandon this straight path and step into your crooked ways?!

[1]: We have discussed this in Volume 6 under verses 123-126 of Surah Al-A'raf (page 305).

It is also necessary to note that a group of commentators have not taken the phrase "By Him who created us" as an oath, but rather consider it conjoined to "the clear proofs that have come to us." Accordingly, the meaning of the whole sentence becomes: "We will never prefer you over these clear proofs and over the God who created us."

However, the first interpretation seems more correct, as the conjunction of these two is not very appropriate. (Reflect on this).

3- Who is the "Criminal"?

Given the above verses which say: "Whoever enters the scene of gathering as a criminal, for him is the fire of Hell," the appearance of which suggests eternal punishment, the question arises: does every criminal have such a fate?

But considering that in the next verse, which states the opposite, the word "believer" is used, it becomes clear that "criminal" here means "disbeliever." Additionally, the use of this word to mean "disbeliever" is seen in many verses of the Quran.

For example, regarding the people of Lot who never believed in their Prophet, we read: "And We rained upon them a rain [of stones]. Then see how was the end of the criminals" (Al-A'raf: 84).

And in Surah Al-Furqan, verse 31, we read: "And thus have We made for every prophet an enemy from among the criminals (disbelievers)."

4- Environmental Determinism is a Myth

The story of the magicians in the above verses showed that the issue of environmental determinism is nothing but a lie. Man is an agent with free will and possesses freedom of choice; whenever he decides, he can change his path from falsehood toward truth, even if all the people of his environment are drowned in sin and caught in deviation. The magicians, who for many years in that polytheistic environment committed the most polytheistic acts themselves, when they decided to accept the truth and stand passionately in its way, feared no threat and attained their goal. As the great commentator, the late Tabarsi, said: "They were disbelieving magicians at the start of the day, and righteous martyrs at the end of the day!" [1] Also, from here it becomes clearly evident how weak and baseless the myths of materialists, and especially Marxists, are regarding the origin of religion. They consider the factor of every movement to be economic issues, while here it was completely the opposite; for the magicians initially stepped into the field of struggle against the truth due to the pressure of Pharaoh's apparatus on one hand and his economic incentives on the other, but faith in Allah destroyed all of these. They sacrificed both the wealth and status Pharaoh had promised them for their faith, and laid down their precious lives for this love!

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 4, page 464 (under verse 126 of Surah Al-A'raf).

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ أَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَىٰ مُوسَىٰٓ أَنْ أَسْرِ بِعِبَادِى فَٱضْرِبْ لَهُمْ طَرِيقًا فِى ٱلْبَحْرِ يَبَسًا لَّا تَخَـٰفُ دَرَكًا وَلَا تَخْشَىٰ (٧٧)
We revealed to Moses, [saying], ‘Set out with My servants at night, and strike out for them a dry path through the sea. Do not be afraid of being overtaken, and have no fear [of getting drowned]. (77)
فَأَتْبَعَهُمْ فِرْعَوْنُ بِجُنُودِهِۦ فَغَشِيَهُم مِّنَ ٱلْيَمِّ مَا غَشِيَهُمْ (٧٨)
Then Pharaoh pursued them with his troops, whereat they were engulfed by what engulfed them of the sea. (78)
وَأَضَلَّ فِرْعَوْنُ قَوْمَهُۥ وَمَا هَدَىٰ (٧٩)
Pharaoh led his people astray and did not guide them. (79)

Commentary

The Salvation of the Children of Israel and the Drowning of the Pharaoh’s People

Following the event of Musa’s struggle against the sorcerers, his decisive and striking victory over them, and the subsequent faith of that vast assembly, Musa and his religion officially entered the thoughts of the Egyptian people. Although the majority of the "Copts" did not accept it, it remained a constant issue for them. Under the leadership of Musa, the Children of Israel, along with a minority of Egyptians, were in a continuous struggle against the Pharaoh’s people.

Years passed in this manner, and various bitter and sweet events occurred, parts of which Allah has recounted in Surah Al-A'raf from verse 127 onwards.

The verses under discussion refer to the final chapter of these events—the plan for the departure of the Children of Israel from Egypt—stating: "We revealed to Musa: 'Take My servants away by night'" (وَ لَقَدْ أَوْحَيْنا إِلى‌ مُوسى‌ أَنْ أَسْرِ بِعِبادِي‌).

The Children of Israel prepared to move toward the Promised Land (Palestine). However, when they reached the banks of the Nile, the Pharaoh’s people became aware, and Pharaoh pursued them with a massive army. They found themselves besieged by the sea and the enemy; on one side was the great Nile River, and on the other, a powerful, bloodthirsty, and enraged enemy! But Allah, who willed to rescue this oppressed, deprived, and faithful group from the clutches of the tyrants and to send the oppressors to the realm of annihilation, commanded Musa:

"Strike for them a dry path in the sea!" (فَاضْرِبْ لَهُمْ طَرِيقاً فِي الْبَحْرِ يَبَساً).

A path such that whenever you step upon it, "you will neither fear being overtaken [by the Pharaoh’s people] nor dread [drowning in the sea]" (لا تَخافُ دَرَكاً وَ لا تَخْشى‌).

Interestingly, not only was a path opened, but by Allah’s command, this path was dry (يَبَساً), despite the fact that usually, if the water of a river or sea recedes, its depths remain impassable for a long time.

Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: "Darak" (rhyming with marg) means the lowest depth of the sea. A rope that is attached to another rope to reach the water is called "darak" (rhyming with mahak). Similarly, the losses that afflict a person are called darak. The "darakat of the Fire" (levels of Hell), as opposed to the "darajat of Paradise," refer to the lower stages of Hell.

However, considering that (according to verse 61 of Surah Ash-Shu'ara) when the Children of Israel became aware of the Pharaoh’s army approaching, they said to Musa: إِنَّا لَمُدْرَكُونَ‌ ("Indeed, we are overtaken"), it seems that the meaning of darak in the verse under discussion is that you will not face such a capture. The meaning of "لا تَخْشى‌" (nor dread) is that no danger from the sea will threaten you either.

Thus, Musa and the Children of Israel entered the paths that appeared within the sea as the waters receded. At this moment, Pharaoh reached the seaside with his troops and was confronted with this unexpected and astonishing scene: "And Pharaoh pursued them with his legions, and he himself entered those same paths" (فَأَتْبَعَهُمْ فِرْعَوْنُ بِجُنُودِهِ‌). [^1] Certainly, Pharaoh's army was initially reluctant to step into such a dangerous and unknown place to pursue the Children of Israel; at the very least, witnessing such a magnificent miracle should have been enough to deter them from continuing this path.

But Pharaoh, whose mind was filled with the wind of pride and arrogance and who was submerged in obstinacy and headstrongness, ignored such a great miracle and encouraged his army to enter these unknown maritime paths! From one side, the last member of Pharaoh's army entered the sea, and from the other side, the last member of the Children of Israel exited.

At this moment, the waves of water were commanded to return to their original state.

The waves collapsed all at once, like a dilapidated building whose foundation is pulled out, "and the sea covered them with its surging waves, covering them completely" (فَغَشِيَهُمْ مِنَ الْيَمِّ ما غَشِيَهُمْ‌). [^2] In this way, a tyrannical and oppressive power, along with its mighty and overwhelming army, was submerged in the waves of water, becoming ready bait for the fish of the sea!

Indeed, "Pharaoh led his people astray and never guided them" (وَ أَضَلَّ فِرْعَوْنُ قَوْمَهُ وَ ما هَدى‌). It is true that the phrase "led astray" (adalla) and the phrase "did not guide" (ma hada) convey nearly the same concept, and perhaps for this reason, some commentators have considered it an emphasis. However, it appears that there is a difference between the two: "adalla" refers to the act of leading astray, while "ma hada" refers to the failure to guide even after the deviation became clear.

To explain: a leader sometimes makes a mistake and leads his followers onto a deviant path, but as soon as he realizes it, he immediately returns them to the correct route. Pharaoh, however, was so obstinate that even after witnessing the deviation, he still did not explain the truth to his people and continued to lead them into the wilderness until he and they were destroyed.

In any case, this sentence actually refutes Pharaoh's claim mentioned in Surah Ghafir, verse 29: وَ ما أَهْدِيكُمْ إِلَّا سَبِيلَ الرَّشادِ ("I do not guide you except to the path of right guidance"). Events showed that this statement was a great lie, just like his other lies.

[^1]: In the commentary of the above sentence, another possibility has been suggested: that the "ba" in "bijunudihi" means "with" (ma'a), making the meaning: "Pharaoh, along with his army, pursued the Children of Israel," though there is not much difference between these two interpretations.

[^2]: "Yamm" means the sea and also a great river. Some researchers believe this is an ancient Egyptian word rather than Arabic—for more explanation, refer to the footnote on page 327 of Volume 6.

Ayahs

يَـٰبَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ قَدْ أَنجَيْنَـٰكُم مِّنْ عَدُوِّكُمْ وَوَٰعَدْنَـٰكُمْ جَانِبَ ٱلطُّورِ ٱلْأَيْمَنَ وَنَزَّلْنَا عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلْمَنَّ وَٱلسَّلْوَىٰ (٨٠)
O Children of Israel! We delivered you from your enemy, and We appointed with you a tryst on the right side of the Mount and We sent down to you manna and quails: (80)
كُلُوا۟ مِن طَيِّبَـٰتِ مَا رَزَقْنَـٰكُمْ وَلَا تَطْغَوْا۟ فِيهِ فَيَحِلَّ عَلَيْكُمْ غَضَبِى ۖ وَمَن يَحْلِلْ عَلَيْهِ غَضَبِى فَقَدْ هَوَىٰ (٨١)
‘Eat of the good things We have provided you, but do not overstep the bounds therein, lest My wrath should descend on you, and he on whom My wrath descends certainly perishes. (81)
وَإِنِّى لَغَفَّارٌ لِّمَن تَابَ وَءَامَنَ وَعَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا ثُمَّ ٱهْتَدَىٰ (٨٢)
Indeed I forgive those who repent, become faithful, act righteously, and follow guidance.’ (82)

Commentary

The Only Way to Salvation

Following the previous discussion which described the salvation of the Children of Israel from the clutches of the Pharaohs as a great miracle, the three aforementioned verses address the Children of Israel in general—across every age and time—reminding them of the great blessings Allah has bestowed upon them and showing them the path to salvation.

First, He says: "O Children of Israel, We delivered you from your enemy" (يَا بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ قَدْ أَنْجَيْنَاكُمْ مِنْ عَدُوِّكُمْ).

It is evident that the foundation of any positive activity is salvation and liberation from the grip of dominant powers and the attainment of independence and freedom; for this reason, it is mentioned before anything else.

Then, referring to one of the important spiritual blessings, He says: "And We made an appointment with you on the holy side of the Mount [Tur], on the right side, that center of Divine revelation" (وَوَاعَدْنَاكُمْ جَانِبَ الطُّورِ الْأَيْمَنَ).

This refers to the event of Moses عليه السلام going, accompanied by a group of the Children of Israel, to the appointed place at Mount Tur. It was at this meeting place that Allah revealed the Tablets of the Torah to Moses, spoke with him, and everyone witnessed a special manifestation of the Lord. [^1] Finally, referring to an important material blessing originating from the special favors of Allah toward the Children of Israel, He says: "And We sent down 'Manna' and 'Salwa' upon you" (وَنَزَّلْنَا عَلَيْكُمُ الْمَنَّ وَالسَّلْوَى).

In that desert where you were wandering and had no suitable food, the grace of Allah rushed to your aid and provided you with delicious and tasty food in the amount you needed, and you utilized it.

Regarding what is meant by "Manna" and "Salwa," commentators have many discussions which we explained in the first volume of this Tafsir (under verse 57 of Surah Al-Baqarah). After mentioning the views of other commentators, we stated it is not unlikely that "Manna" was a type of natural honey found in the mountains adjacent to that desert, or special nourishing plant saps that appeared on the trees growing in corners of that wilderness, and "Salwa" was a type of lawful-meat bird similar to a pigeon.

[^1]: For a detailed explanation of this event, please read Volume 6, Surah Al-A'raf, under verses 155 and 156 (page 388). For further clarification, refer to Volume 1 under the aforementioned verse.

In the next verse, following the mention of these three highly valuable blessings, He addresses them thus: "Eat of the good things We have provided for you, but do not transgress therein" (كُلُوا مِنْ طَيِّبَاتِ مَا رَزَقْنَاكُمْ وَلَا تَطْغَوْا فِيهِ).

Transgression regarding blessings occurs when a human, instead of using them in the path of obedience to Allah and the way of his own prosperity, turns them into a means for sin, ingratitude, disbelief, rebellion, and becoming a captive of [evil] thoughts—just as the Children of Israel did; they received all these Divine blessings and then pursued the path of disbelief, transgression, and sin.

Following that, He warns them: "For if you transgress, My wrath will descend upon you" (فَيَحِلَّ عَلَيْكُمْ غَضَبِي).

"And whoever My wrath descends upon has indeed fallen" (وَمَنْ يَحْلِلْ عَلَيْهِ غَضَبِي فَقَدْ هَوَى).

"Hawa" (هوى) originally means falling from a height, which usually results in destruction. Additionally, here it refers to a fall in status, distance from proximity to the Lord, and being driven away from His presence.

Since warnings and threats must always be accompanied by encouragement and glad tidings—to equally stimulate the forces of fear and hope, which are the primary factors of perfection, and to open the doors of return for the repentant—the next verse says: "And indeed, I am Most Forgiving to whoever repents, believes, performs righteous deeds, and then is guided" (وَإِنِّي لَغَفَّارٌ لِمَنْ تَابَ وَآمَنَ وَعَمِلَ صَالِحًا ثُمَّ اهْتَدَى).

Considering that "Ghaffar" (غَفَّار) is an intensive form (Sighat al-Mubalaghah), it indicates that Allah grants His forgiveness to such individuals not just once, but repeatedly.

It is noteworthy that the first condition of repentance is turning back from sin. After the page of the human soul is washed clean of this pollution, the second condition is that the light of faith in Allah and Monotheism (Tawhid) must settle upon it.

In the third stage, the blossoms of faith and Monotheism—which are righteous deeds and worthy actions—must appear on the branches of the human being's existence.

However, here, unlike other verses of the Quran that speak only of repentance, faith, and righteous deeds, a fourth condition has been added under the title "then is guided" (ثُمَّ اهْتَدَى).

Regarding the meaning of this phrase, commentators have many discussions, among which two interpretations seem most interesting:

First, it refers to continuing the path of faith, piety, and righteous deeds. That is, repentance washes away the past and causes salvation, provided that the repentant person does not fall into the same valley of polytheism and sin again, and constantly remains vigilant so that the temptations of Satan and the self do not return him to the former path.

Second, this phrase refers to the necessity of accepting the Wilayah (guardianship) and the leadership of Divine leaders. That is, repentance, faith, and righteous deeds lead to salvation only when they are placed under the umbrella of the guidance of Divine leaders—at one time Moses عليه السلام, at another time the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, on one day the Commander of the Faithful Ali عليه السلام, and today Hazrat Mahdi (may Allah hasten his reappearance).

This is because one of the pillars of religion is accepting the call of the Prophet and his leadership, and subsequently accepting the leadership of his successors.

The late Tabarsi, under this verse, narrates from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام that he said:

"The meaning of the phrase 'then is guided' is guidance toward the Wilayah of us, the Ahl al-Bayt." Then he added:

"By Allah, if a man were to worship Allah his entire life between the Rukn and the Maqam (near the Kaaba), and then die without having brought [accepted] our Wilayah, Allah would cast him face-first into the Fire."

This narration has also been cited by the famous Sunni traditionist, Hakim Abu al-Qasim al-Haskani. [^1] Numerous other narrations in this regard have been narrated from Imam Zayn al-Abidin عليه السلام, Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, and from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله himself.

To understand how lethal abandoning this principle is, it is sufficient to examine the subsequent verses, showing how the Children of Israel, due to abandoning the Wilayah and leaving the line of following Moses and his successor Aaron, became entangled in calf-worship, polytheism, and disbelief.

From here, it becomes clear that what Alusi stated in the commentary Ruh al-Ma'ani—after mentioning some of these narrations—that "the obligation of loving the Ahl al-Bayt is beyond doubt for us, but this has no connection to the Children of Israel and the era of Moses," is a baseless statement.

This is because, firstly, the discussion is not about "love" but about accepting "leadership." Secondly, the intention is not to restrict leadership to the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام [exclusively for all times]; rather, in the era of Moses, he and his brother Aaron were the leaders and accepting their Wilayah was necessary; in the era of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, his Wilayah; and in the era of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt, their Wilayah.

It is also clear that although the addressees of this verse are the Children of Israel, it is not exclusive to them; any individual or group that traverses these four stages will be included in Allah's forgiveness and pardon.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse being discussed.

Ayahs

۞ وَمَآ أَعْجَلَكَ عَن قَوْمِكَ يَـٰمُوسَىٰ (٨٣)
[God said,] ‘O Moses, what has prompted you to hasten ahead of your people?’ (83)
قَالَ هُمْ أُو۟لَآءِ عَلَىٰٓ أَثَرِى وَعَجِلْتُ إِلَيْكَ رَبِّ لِتَرْضَىٰ (٨٤)
He said, ‘They are close upon my heels, and I hurried on to You, my Lord, that You may be pleased.’ (84)
قَالَ فَإِنَّا قَدْ فَتَنَّا قَوْمَكَ مِنۢ بَعْدِكَ وَأَضَلَّهُمُ ٱلسَّامِرِىُّ (٨٥)
He said, ‘Indeed We tried your people in your absence, and the Samiri has led them astray.’ (85)
فَرَجَعَ مُوسَىٰٓ إِلَىٰ قَوْمِهِۦ غَضْبَـٰنَ أَسِفًا ۚ قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ أَلَمْ يَعِدْكُمْ رَبُّكُمْ وَعْدًا حَسَنًا ۚ أَفَطَالَ عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلْعَهْدُ أَمْ أَرَدتُّمْ أَن يَحِلَّ عَلَيْكُمْ غَضَبٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ فَأَخْلَفْتُم مَّوْعِدِى (٨٦)
Thereupon Moses returned to his people, indignant and grieved. He said, ‘O my people! Did your Lord not give you a true promise? Did the period [of my absence] seem too long to you? Or did you desire that your Lord’s wrath should descend on you and so you failed your tryst with me?’ (86)
قَالُوا۟ مَآ أَخْلَفْنَا مَوْعِدَكَ بِمَلْكِنَا وَلَـٰكِنَّا حُمِّلْنَآ أَوْزَارًا مِّن زِينَةِ ٱلْقَوْمِ فَقَذَفْنَـٰهَا فَكَذَٰلِكَ أَلْقَى ٱلسَّامِرِىُّ (٨٧)
They said, ‘We did not fail our tryst with you of our own accord, but we were laden with the weight of those people’s ornaments, and we cast them [into the fire] and so did the Samiri.’ (87)
فَأَخْرَجَ لَهُمْ عِجْلًا جَسَدًا لَّهُۥ خُوَارٌ فَقَالُوا۟ هَـٰذَآ إِلَـٰهُكُمْ وَإِلَـٰهُ مُوسَىٰ فَنَسِىَ (٨٨)
Then he produced for them a calf—a [lifeless] body with a low—and they said, This is your god and the god of Moses, so he forgot! (88)
أَفَلَا يَرَوْنَ أَلَّا يَرْجِعُ إِلَيْهِمْ قَوْلًا وَلَا يَمْلِكُ لَهُمْ ضَرًّا وَلَا نَفْعًا (٨٩)
Did they not see that it did not answer them, nor could it bring them any benefit or harm? (89)
وَلَقَدْ قَالَ لَهُمْ هَـٰرُونُ مِن قَبْلُ يَـٰقَوْمِ إِنَّمَا فُتِنتُم بِهِۦ ۖ وَإِنَّ رَبَّكُمُ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ فَٱتَّبِعُونِى وَأَطِيعُوٓا۟ أَمْرِى (٩٠)
Aaron had certainly told them earlier, ‘O my people! You are only being tested by it. Indeed your Lord is the All-beneficent. So follow me and obey my command!’ (90)
قَالُوا۟ لَن نَّبْرَحَ عَلَيْهِ عَـٰكِفِينَ حَتَّىٰ يَرْجِعَ إِلَيْنَا مُوسَىٰ (٩١)
They had said, ‘We will keep on clinging to it until Moses returns to us.’ (91)

Commentary

The Uproar of Samiri!

In these verses, another significant chapter of the life of Musa (peace be upon him) and the Children of Israel is presented, concerning Musa’s (peace be upon him) departure with the representatives of the Children of Israel to the appointed tryst at Mount Tur, and the subsequent calf-worship of the Children of Israel during their absence.

The plan was for Musa (peace be upon him) to go to Mount Tur to receive the laws of the Torah, accompanied by a group from the Children of Israel, so that new truths regarding the gnosis of God and revelation would be manifested to them during this journey.

However, since the longing for intimate discourse (munajat) with the Lord and hearing the melody of revelation was blazing in Musa’s heart to such an extent that he was restless—forgetting everything, even eating, drinking, and resting along the way according to narrations—he traversed the path with speed and reached the Lord’s appointed place alone, ahead of the others.

At this point, revelation descended upon him: "O Musa, what made you hasten away from your people to come here?" (وَ ما أَعْجَلَكَ عَنْ قَوْمِكَ يا مُوسى‌).

Musa immediately submitted: "O Lord! They are following in my footsteps, and I hastened to the place of meeting and the presence of Your revelation so that You might be pleased with me" (قالَ هُمْ أُولاءِ عَلى‌ أَثَرِي وَ عَجِلْتُ إِلَيْكَ رَبِّ لِتَرْضى‌). Not only did the love of Your intimate discourse and hearing Your word make me restless, but I was also eager to receive Your laws and decrees as soon as possible to deliver them to Your servants, thereby better seeking Your pleasure. Yes, I am a lover of Your pleasure and eager to hear Your command.

Ultimately, during this meeting, the spiritual manifestations of the Lord were extended from thirty nights to forty nights. The various underlying factors for deviation that previously existed among the Children of Israel took their toll. Samiri, that cunning and deviant man, took center stage. Using means we will mention later, he fashioned a calf and invited the masses to worship it. Undoubtedly, factors such as having witnessed the calf-worship of the Egyptians, or seeing scenes of idol-worship (cow-worship) after crossing the Nile and requesting an idol like them, as well as the extension of Musa’s appointment and the spread of rumors of his death by the hypocrites, and finally the ignorance of this population, all played a role in the occurrence of this incident and the great deviation from monotheism to disbelief. Social events usually do not occur without a prelude; however, sometimes these preludes are overt and sometimes mysterious and hidden.

In any case, polytheism in its worst form gripped the Children of Israel, especially since the elders of the tribe were with Musa at the tryst, and the only leader left with the people was Harun, without having effective assistants. Finally, it was there that Allah said to Musa (peace be upon him) at the appointed place: "We have tested your people after you, but they did not pass the trial well, and Samiri has led them astray" (قالَ فَإِنَّا قَدْ فَتَنَّا قَوْمَكَ مِنْ بَعْدِكَ وَ أَضَلَّهُمُ السَّامِرِيُ‌).

Upon hearing this, Musa became so agitated that his entire being seemed to catch fire. Perhaps he said to himself: "For long years I suffered, toiled, and faced every danger to acquaint this people with monotheism, but alas, a hundred times alas, with my absence of a few days, my efforts have gone to the wind!" Therefore, immediately "Musa returned to his people, angry and sorrowful" (فَرَجَعَ مُوسى‌ إِلى‌ قَوْمِهِ غَضْبانَ أَسِفاً).

When his eyes fell upon that repulsive scene of calf-worship, he cried out: "O my people! Did your Lord not make you a fair promise?" (أَ لَمْ يَعِدْكُمْ رَبُّكُمْ وَعْداً حَسَناً). This fair promise was either the promise regarding the revelation of the Torah and the explanation of heavenly laws therein, or the promise of salvation and victory over the Pharaohs and inheriting the rule of the land, or the promise of forgiveness for those who repent and have faith and righteous deeds—or all of these matters.

Then he added: "Has the time of my separation from you been too long?" (أَ فَطالَ عَلَيْكُمُ الْعَهْدُ). Implying: Suppose the duration of my promised return was extended from thirty days to forty; this is not a long time. Should you not have preserved yourselves during this short period? Even if I were away from you for years, the religion of God that I taught you and the miracles you witnessed with your own eyes should have governed you.

"Or did you desire that wrath from your Lord should descend upon you, so you broke your promise to me?" (أَمْ أَرَدْتُمْ أَنْ يَحِلَّ عَلَيْكُمْ غَضَبٌ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ فَأَخْلَفْتُمْ مَوْعِدِي‌) [1]

[1]: Naturally, no one decides to purchase the wrath of the Lord for themselves; the meaning of this expression is that your action is such as if you had made such a decision regarding yourselves.

I had covenanted with you to stand on the line of monotheism and the path of sincere obedience to the Lord and not to deviate from it in the slightest, but it seems you forgot all my words in my absence and also disobeyed the command of my brother Harun.

The Children of Israel, finding themselves faced with Musa’s (peace be upon him) severe protest and realizing they had indeed committed a very evil act, began making excuses and said: "We did not break our promise to you of our own will and volition" (قالُوا ما أَخْلَفْنا مَوْعِدَكَ بِمَلْكِنا). [1] In fact, it was not we who inclined toward calf-worship by our own will, "but we were made to carry weights of the ornaments of the people (Egyptians), so we cast them away, and so did Samiri throw" (وَ لكِنَّا حُمِّلْنا أَوْزاراً مِنْ زِينَةِ الْقَوْمِ فَقَذَفْناها فَكَذلِكَ أَلْقَى السَّامِرِيُ‌). Regarding what the Children of Israel did and what Samiri did, and exactly what the phrases of the above verses mean, commentators have discussions that, overall, do not differ much in terms of the conclusion.

Some have said "we cast them" (قذفناها) means we threw the ornaments we had taken from the Egyptians before moving from Egypt into the fire, and Samiri also threw what he had into the fire until it melted and he fashioned the calf from it.

[1]: "Mulk" and "Malk" both mean possessing something, and the intention of the Children of Israel was that we were not in control or masters of this act, but rather we were so influenced that our hearts and faith were snatched from us. Some commentators have attributed this sentence to a minority of the Children of Israel who did not worship the calf (they say six hundred thousand of them became calf-worshippers and only twelve thousand remained on monotheism), but the interpretation we mentioned above seems more correct.

Some have said the meaning of the sentence is that we cast the ornaments away from ourselves and Samiri took them and threw them into the fire to make the calf from them. It is also possible that the phrase "and so did Samiri throw" (فَكَذلِكَ أَلْقَى السَّامِرِيُ‌) refers to the entire plan and scheme that Samiri executed.

In any case, it is common that when a leader reproaches his subordinates for a sin they have committed, they try to deflect the guilt from themselves and place it on another. The calf-worshippers of the Children of Israel, who had inclined from monotheism to polytheism by their own will, wanted to place all the guilt on Samiri.

In any case, from the ornaments of the Egyptians—which had come into the hands of the Egyptians through oppression and sin and had no value other than being spent on such a forbidden act—yes, from the sum of these ornaments, "he produced for them a statue of a calf, a lifeless body that had a lowing sound" (فأخرج لهم عجلا جسدا له خوار). [1] When the Children of Israel saw this scene, they suddenly consigned all of Musa’s monotheistic teachings to oblivion and said to one another: "This is your god and the god of Musa" (فَقالُوا هذا إِلهُكُمْ وَ إِلهُ مُوسى‌). It is also possible that the speaker of these words was Samiri and his assistants and his first believers.

"And in this way, Samiri forgot his covenant with Musa, or rather with the God of Musa," and led the people into misguidance (فَنَسِيَ‌). The fact that some commentators have interpreted "forgetfulness" (نسيان) here to mean misguidance and going astray, or have considered the subject of "forgot" to be Musa and said this sentence is the speech of Samiri—meaning to say: "Musa has forgotten that this calf is your god"—all of these are contrary to the apparent meaning of the verse. The apparent meaning is what we stated above: that Samiri forgot his covenant with Musa and the God of Musa and took the path of idol-worship.

[1]: "Khuwar" means the sound of a cow or calf, and sometimes it is also applied to the sound of a camel.

Here, Allah says by way of rebuke and reproach to these idol-worshippers: "Do they not see that it does not return to them a word, and possesses for them neither harm nor benefit?" (أَ فَلا يَرَوْنَ أَلَّا يرجع إِلَيْهِمْ قَوْلًا وَ لا يَمْلِكُ لَهُمْ ضَرًّا وَ لا نَفْعاً). A true deity must at least be able to answer the questions of His servants. Can the mere hearing of a calf's sound from this golden statue—a sound in which no will or choice was felt—be a reason for worship?

And even if it were to answer their words, it would still be a being like a helpless human who does not possess the power of benefit or harm for another, or even for himself. In such a state, can it be a deity?! What intellect allows a person to worship a lifeless statue from which an unintelligible sound occasionally arises and to bow down before it?

Undoubtedly, in this clamor and uproar, Harun, the successor of Musa (peace be upon him) and the great Prophet of God, did not abandon his mission and performed the duty of struggling against deviation and corruption to the extent of his ability, as the Quran says: "Harun had said to them before Musa’s return from the tryst: 'O my people! You are only being tested by this'"—do not be deceived and do not deviate from the path of monotheism (وَ لَقَدْ قالَ لَهُمْ هارُونُ مِنْ قَبْلُ يا قَوْمِ إِنَّما فُتِنْتُمْ بِهِ‌).

Then he added: "Your Lord is surely the Most Merciful, who has bestowed all these blessings upon you" (وَ إِنَّ رَبَّكُمُ الرَّحْمنُ‌). You were slaves, He made you free; you were captives, He gave you liberation; you were lost, He guided you; you were scattered, and under the leadership of a heavenly man, He gathered and united you; you were ignorant and lost, He cast the light of knowledge upon you and guided you to the straight path of monotheism.

"Now that it is so, follow me and obey my command" (فَاتَّبِعُونِي وَ أَطِيعُوا أَمْرِي‌). Have you forgotten that my brother Musa made me his successor and made obedience to me obligatory upon you? Why do you break the covenant? Why do you plunge yourselves into the abyss of destruction?

But the Children of Israel were so stubbornly attached to this calf that the powerful logic and clear proofs of this man of God and compassionate leader did not affect them. They explicitly declared their opposition to Harun and said: "We will never cease to be devoted to its worship until Musa returns to us" (قالُوا لَنْ نَبْرَحَ عَلَيْهِ عاكِفِينَ حَتَّى يَرْجِعَ إِلَيْنا مُوسى‌). [1] In short, they dug their heels in and said: "This is the matter and nothing else; the program of calf-worship must continue until Musa returns, and we seek judgment from him: perhaps he himself will prostrate before the calf along with us!! Therefore, do not exhaust yourself too much and leave us alone!" In this way, they trampled upon both the clear command of intellect and the command of their leader’s successor.

However, as commentators have written—and as logic dictates—when Harun fulfilled his mission in this struggle and the majority did not accept, he, along with the minority who followed him, separated from them and kept his distance, lest their mixing with one another be seen as an endorsement of their deviant programs.

Strangely, some commentators have narrated that these deviant changes in the Children of Israel occurred in just a few short days. When 35 days had passed since Musa’s departure to the tryst, Samiri got to work and asked the Children of Israel to gather all the jewelry they had borrowed from the Egyptians and kept after the story of their drowning. On the thirty-sixth, thirty-seventh, and thirty-eighth days, he put them all in the crucible, melted them, and fashioned the statue of the calf. On the thirty-ninth day, he invited them to worship it, and a vast group (according to some narrations, six hundred thousand people!) accepted it. One day later, with the completion of the forty days, Musa returned. [1] In any case, Harun, with a minority of about twelve thousand steadfast believers, separated from the population while the ignorant and stubborn majority were close to killing him.

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse being discussed.

Key Points

1- The Longing for the Meeting!

For those who are unaware of the attraction of the love of God, Musa’s words in response to the Lord’s question regarding his haste in rushing to the Lord’s tryst might seem strange, where he says: "وَ عَجِلْتُ إِلَيْكَ رَبِّ لِتَرْضى‌" (O Lord, I hastened to You to seek Your pleasure).

But they have understood this truth with their entire being: When the promise of union draws near, The fire of love burns more fiercely. They know well what mysterious force was pulling Musa toward the tryst of "Allah," and he went with such speed that he even left behind the people who were with him.

Musa had tasted the sweetness of union with the Friend and intimate discourse with the Lord many times before; he knew that the entire world is not equal to one moment of this discourse. Yes, such is the way and custom of those who have passed beyond metaphorical love and stepped into the stage of true love—the love of the Eternal Deity. The love of a God whose Holy Essence is never touched by annihilation, who is Absolute Perfection and infinite goodness; and whatever beauty all the beautiful ones possess, He alone possesses. Rather, the beauty of all the beautiful ones is but a small reflection of His eternal beauty. O Great Lord! Grant us a taste of a particle of this sacred love.

Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him)—according to a narration—says: "The one who is restless with longing desires no food, finds no pleasure in drink, finds no comfort in sleep, finds no intimacy with a close friend, and takes no shelter in a house... and he worships Allah night and day, hoping to reach what he longs for... just as Allah informed about Musa ibn 'Imran at the tryst of his Lord with His saying: 'وَ عَجِلْتُ إِلَيْكَ رَبِّ لِتَرْضى‌'" [1]

2- Counter-Revolutionary Movements Against the Revolution of Prophets

Usually, against every revolution, there exists a counter-revolutionary movement that tries to undo the achievements of the revolution and return society to the pre-revolutionary era. The reason for this is not very complex, because with the realization of a revolution, all the corrupt elements of the past are not destroyed at once; usually, dregs of it remain that rise to struggle to preserve their existence. Depending on the conditions, quantity, and quality, they engage in overt or covert counter-revolutionary acts.

In the revolutionary movement of "Musa ibn 'Imran" toward monotheism, independence, and the freedom of the Children of Israel, Samiri was the leader of this reactionary movement. He—like all leaders of reactionary movements—was well acquainted with the weaknesses of his people and knew that by using these weaknesses, he could start a commotion. He tried to fashion a calf out of ornaments and gold—which are the deities of the worldly and attractive to the common masses—and placed it in a specific way in the path of the wind (or using any other means) so that a sound would arise from it. Then, using a suitable opportunity (Musa’s absence of a few days) and considering that the Children of Israel, after being saved from the sea and passing by an idol-worshipping people, had requested an idol from Musa—in short, by using all psychological weaknesses and suitable temporal and spatial opportunities—he began his anti-monotheistic program. He arranged its components so skillfully that in a short time, he diverted the overwhelming majority of the ignorant Children of Israel from the path of monotheism and led them to polytheism.

Although this conspiracy was neutralized as soon as Musa returned, by the power of his faith and logic in the light of revelation, let us think: what would have happened if Musa had not returned? Certainly, they would have either killed his brother Harun or isolated him so much that his voice would reach no one! Yes, every revolution is this fragile at the beginning, and one must be completely alert, monitor the smallest reactionary polytheistic movements, and nip the enemy's conspiracies in the bud.

Furthermore, one must pay attention to the fact that many true revolutions, for various reasons, are initially dependent on a specific individual or individuals; if they are removed, the danger of reversal threatens the revolution. For this reason, efforts must be made to implement revolutionary standards deep within society as soon as possible, and the people must be built in such a way that counter-revolutionary storms do not shake them in any way, and they stand like a mountain against every reactionary movement. Or in other words, it is one of the duties of true leaders to transfer the standards from themselves to the society, and undoubtedly this important matter also requires the passage of time, but one must strive to make this time as short as possible.

Regarding who Samiri was and what his fate was, we will speak in the following verses, God willing.

3- Stages of Leadership

Undoubtedly, Harun did not show the slightest weakness in fulfilling his mission during Musa’s absence, but the ignorance of the people on one hand, and the residues of the era of servitude, slavery, and idol-worship in Egypt on the other, neutralized his efforts. According to the above verses, he implemented his duty in four stages:

First, he announced to them that this event was a line of deviation and a dangerous field of testing for all of them, so that sleeping brains might wake up and people might begin to think, and this was the most important thing (يا قَوْمِ إِنَّما فُتِنْتُمْ بِهِ‌).

The second stage was to remind them of the various blessings of Allah that had included the Children of Israel from the beginning of Musa’s uprising until the time of salvation from the clutches of the Pharaohs, and specifically described God with His attribute of general mercy, to leave a deeper impact and also to make them hopeful of the forgiveness of this great error (وَ إِنَّ رَبَّكُمُ الرَّحْمنُ‌).

The third stage was to draw their attention to his own position of prophethood and his succession to his brother Musa (فَاتَّبِعُونِي‌).

And finally, the fourth stage was to acquaint them with their divine duties (وَ أَطِيعُوا أَمْرِي‌).

4- Response to an Objection

The famous commentator Fakhr al-Razi raised an objection here and remained stuck in its answer. He says that the Shi'a have argued for the guardianship (wilayah) of 'Ali (peace be upon him) based on the famous saying of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) to 'Ali (peace be upon him): "You are to me as Harun was to Musa." While Harun, in the face of the vast multitude of idol-worshippers, never allowed himself to practice dissimulation (taqiyyah) and explicitly invited the people to follow him and abandon following others. If indeed the Ummah of Muhammad (peace be upon him and his family) followed the path of error after his passing, it was obligatory upon 'Ali (peace be upon him) to act upon the same program as Harun, to go upon the pulpit and say without any fear or dissimulation: "Follow me and obey my command." Since he did not do such a thing, we understand that the way and custom of the Ummah at that time was right and correct.

But it seems Fakhr al-Razi neglected two fundamental points in this regard:

1- His claim that 'Ali (peace be upon him) said nothing regarding his immediate succession is a mistake, because we have numerous documents in hand showing that the Imam stated this subject on various occasions, sometimes explicitly and openly and sometimes in a veiled manner. In the book Nahj al-Balagha, various passages are seen, such as the Sermon of Shiqshiqiyyah (Sermon 3), and Sermons 87, 97, 94, 154, and 147, all of which speak in this regard. In the fifth volume of Tafsir Nemouneh, under verse 67 of Surah Al-Ma'idah, after explaining the story of Ghadir, we have narrated multiple traditions that 'Ali (peace be upon him) himself repeatedly cited the Hadith of Ghadir to prove his position and immediate succession (for more explanation, refer to Volume 5, page 19 onwards).

After the passing of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), there were special conditions; the hypocrites who were counting the days in anticipation of the Prophet’s (peace be upon him and his family) death had prepared themselves for the final blow to the fledgling Islam. Therefore, we see that the Ashab al-Riddah (the anti-Islamic revolutionary group) immediately rose during the caliphate of Abu Bakr, and if it were not for the unity, cohesion, and alertness of the Muslims, they might have dealt irreparable blows to Islam. 'Ali (peace be upon him) stepped back for this very reason, so that the enemy would not take advantage.

Coincidentally, Harun—even though Musa was alive—in response to his brother’s reproach as to why he fell short, explicitly submitted: "I feared that you would say: 'You have caused division among the Children of Israel'" (Ta-Ha: 94). This shows that he too stepped back to some extent for fear of division.

Ayahs

قَالَ يَـٰهَـٰرُونُ مَا مَنَعَكَ إِذْ رَأَيْتَهُمْ ضَلُّوٓا۟ (٩٢)
He said, ‘O Aaron! What kept you, when you saw them going astray, (92)
أَلَّا تَتَّبِعَنِ ۖ أَفَعَصَيْتَ أَمْرِى (٩٣)
from following me? Did you disobey my command?’ (93)
قَالَ يَبْنَؤُمَّ لَا تَأْخُذْ بِلِحْيَتِى وَلَا بِرَأْسِىٓ ۖ إِنِّى خَشِيتُ أَن تَقُولَ فَرَّقْتَ بَيْنَ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ وَلَمْ تَرْقُبْ قَوْلِى (٩٤)
He said, ‘O son of my mother! Do not grab my beard or my head! I feared lest you should say, ‘‘You have caused a rift among the Children of Israel and did not heed my word [of advice].’’’ (94)
قَالَ فَمَا خَطْبُكَ يَـٰسَـٰمِرِىُّ (٩٥)
He said, ‘What is your business, O Samiri?’ (95)
قَالَ بَصُرْتُ بِمَا لَمْ يَبْصُرُوا۟ بِهِۦ فَقَبَضْتُ قَبْضَةً مِّنْ أَثَرِ ٱلرَّسُولِ فَنَبَذْتُهَا وَكَذَٰلِكَ سَوَّلَتْ لِى نَفْسِى (٩٦)
He said, ‘I saw what they did not see. I took a handful [of dust] from the messenger’s trail and threw it. That is how my soul prompted me.’ (96)
قَالَ فَٱذْهَبْ فَإِنَّ لَكَ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ أَن تَقُولَ لَا مِسَاسَ ۖ وَإِنَّ لَكَ مَوْعِدًا لَّن تُخْلَفَهُۥ ۖ وَٱنظُرْ إِلَىٰٓ إِلَـٰهِكَ ٱلَّذِى ظَلْتَ عَلَيْهِ عَاكِفًا ۖ لَّنُحَرِّقَنَّهُۥ ثُمَّ لَنَنسِفَنَّهُۥ فِى ٱلْيَمِّ نَسْفًا (٩٧)
He said, ‘Begone! It shall be your [lot] throughout life to say, ‘‘Do not touch me!’’ Indeed, there is a tryst for you which you will not fail to keep! Now look at your god to whom you went on clinging. We will burn it down and then scatter it[s ashes] into the sea. (97)
إِنَّمَآ إِلَـٰهُكُمُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۚ وَسِعَ كُلَّ شَىْءٍ عِلْمًا (٩٨)
Indeed your God is Allah. There is no god except Him. He embraces all things in [His] knowledge.’ (98)

Commentary

The Painful Fate of Samiri!

Following the discussion Moses (peace be upon him) had with the Children of Israel in severe rebuke of calf-worship, which we read in the previous verses, the verses under discussion first reflect the dialogue of Moses (peace be upon him) with his brother Aaron (peace be upon him) and then with Samiri.

First, turning to his brother Aaron, "He said: 'O Aaron! What prevented you, when you saw them going astray, from following me?'" (قَالَ يَا هَارُونُ مَا مَنَعَكَ إِذْ رَأَيْتَهُمْ ضَلُّوا أَلَّا تَتَّبِعَنِ).

Did I not say to you when I wanted to go to the appointed place: "Be my successor among my people, and act righteously, and do not follow the way of the mischief-makers"? [^1]

[^1]: Surah Al-A'raf, Verse 142 (اخْلُفْنِي فِي قَوْمِي وَ أَصْلِحْ وَ لا تَتَّبِعْ سَبِيلَ الْمُفْسِدِينَ).

Why did you not rise up to struggle against these idol-worshippers?

Therefore, the meaning of the phrase "أَلَّا تَتَّبِعَنِ" (that you should not follow me) is: Why did you not follow my method and Sunnah in being firm and severe against idolatry?

As for what some have suggested—that this phrase means "Why did you not follow me to Mount Sinai along with the minority who remained steadfast in monotheism?"—it seems very unlikely and does not align well with the response Aaron gives in the following verses.

Then Moses added: "Have you then disobeyed my command?" (أَفَعَصَيْتَ أَمْرِي).

Moses spoke these words to his brother with the utmost intensity and anger, shouting at him while grasping his beard and head and pulling him. When Aaron saw his brother's severe distress, in order to move him to kindness, reduce his agitation, and simultaneously state his valid excuse in this matter, he said: "O son of my mother! Do not seize me by my beard nor by my head; indeed, I feared that if I rose to fight and a conflict occurred, you would say upon your return: 'You have caused a division among the Children of Israel and did not observe my word.'" (قَالَ يَا بْنَ أُمَّ لَا تَأْخُذْ بِلِحْيَتِي وَلَا بِرَأْسِي إِنِّي خَشِيتُ أَنْ تَقُولَ فَرَّقْتَ بَيْنَ بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ وَلَمْ تَرْقُبْ قَوْلِي).

In reality, Aaron's view refers to the very words Moses had spoken to him when departing for the appointed place, the content of which was a call to reform (Surah Al-A'raf, Verse 142).

He meant to say: "If I had initiated a conflict, it would have been contrary to your command [to maintain order and reform], and you would have had the right to call me to account."

In this way, Aaron proved his innocence, especially considering another sentence mentioned in Surah Al-A'raf, Verse 150: إِنَّ الْقَوْمَ اسْتَضْعَفُونِي وَ كادُوا يَقْتُلُونَنِي—"Indeed, the people oppressed me and were about to kill me." I am innocent, truly innocent.

Here, a question arises: Moses (peace be upon him) and Aaron (peace be upon him) were undoubtedly both Prophets and infallible (Ma'sum). How can this argument, severe rebuke from Moses, and the defense Aaron makes for himself be justified?

In response, it can be said: Moses was certain that his brother was innocent, but through this action, He wanted to prove two points: First, to make the Children of Israel understand that they had committed a truly enormous sin—a sin so grave that it even brought the brother of Moses, who was himself a high-ranking Prophet, to the "courtroom" for questioning, and with such severity. This was to show that the matter was not as simple as some of the Children of Israel imagined; deviating from monotheism and returning to polytheism after all those teachings and witnessing so many miracles and signs of God's greatness was unbelievable and had to be stood against with the utmost decisiveness.

Sometimes, when a great tragedy occurs, a person may tear his own collar or strike his own head, let alone rebuke his brother. Undoubtedly, for the sake of preserving the objective and leaving a psychological impact on the deviant individuals—and showing them the magnitude of the sin—these actions are effective. Certainly, Aaron was also fully consenting to this process.

Secondly, Aaron's innocence would be proven to everyone through the explanations he provided, so that he would not later be accused of negligence in fulfilling his mission.

After finishing the dialogue with his brother Aaron and exonerating him, he proceeded to the trial of Samiri and "said: 'What was your business, O Samiri?'" (قَالَ فَمَا خَطْبُكَ يَا سَامِرِيُّ).

In response, he "said: 'I perceived what they did not perceive'" (قَالَ بَصُرْتُ بِمَا لَمْ يَبْصُرُوا بِهِ). "I took a handful [of dust] from the tracks of the messenger, then I threw it [into the calf], and thus did my soul embellish the matter for me!" (فَقَبَضْتُ قَبْضَةً مِنْ أَثَرِ الرَّسُولِ فَنَبَذْتُهَا وَكَذَلِكَ سَوَّلَتْ لِي نَفْسِي).

Regarding what Samiri meant by this statement, there are two well-known interpretations among commentators:

First: He meant that when Pharaoh's army came to the Nile, I saw Gabriel on a mount, moving ahead of them to encourage the army to enter the dried paths of the sea. I took some of the dust from under his feet or his mount's feet and saved it for today, then threw it into the golden calf, and this sound [the lowing] is from its blessing!

The second interpretation: I initially believed in a part of the signs/teachings of this Messenger of the Lord (Moses), then I doubted it and cast it away, leaning toward the religion of idolatry, and this seemed more attractive and beautiful to me!

According to the first interpretation, "Messenger" refers to "Gabriel," while in the second interpretation, "Messenger" refers to Moses.

"Athar" (track/trace) in the first interpretation means "dust from under the feet," and in the second, it means "a portion of the teachings." "Nabaztuha" (I threw it away) in the first interpretation means throwing the dust into the calf, and in the second, it means abandoning the teachings of Moses (peace be upon him). Finally, "I perceived what they did not perceive" in the first interpretation refers to seeing Gabriel, who had appeared as a horseman (perhaps others saw him too but did not recognize him), while in the second, it refers to specific insights regarding the religion of Moses (peace be upon him).

In any case, each of these two interpretations has its supporters and contains both clear and ambiguous points. However, overall, the second interpretation seems better in several respects, especially since we read in a Hadith in the book Al-Ihtijaj by Tabarsi that when the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (peace be upon him), conquered Basra, people gathered around him. Among them was "Hasan al-Basri," who had brought tablets and was immediately writing down every word the Commander of the Faithful said. The Imam addressed him loudly in the crowd and said: "What are you doing?" He replied: "I am writing your traces and words to recount them for future generations." The Commander of the Faithful said:

"Know that every people has a Samiri, and this man (Hasan al-Basri) is the Samiri of this Ummah! The only difference between him and the Samiri of Moses' time is that whoever approached Samiri, he would say 'La Misas' (Touch me not), but this one says to the people 'La Qital' (No fighting—meaning one should not fight even against the deviants, referring to the propaganda Hasan al-Basri had against the Battle of the Camel)." [^1]

From this Hadith, it is inferred that Samiri was also a hypocritical man who, by using some seemingly rightful points, attempted to lead the people astray, and this meaning is more consistent with the second interpretation.

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 392.

It is clear that Samiri's response and excuse to Moses' question were by no means acceptable. Therefore, Moses issued the sentence of his condemnation in this court and gave three commands regarding him and his calf:

First, he said to him: "You must go away from among the people and have no contact with anyone, and your lot in the remainder of your life is that whoever approaches you, you will say: 'Touch me not!'" (قَالَ فَاذْهَبْ فَإِنَّ لَكَ فِي الْحَيَاةِ أَنْ تَقُولَ لَا مِسَاسَ). In this way, with a decisive command, he expelled Samiri from society and forced him into absolute isolation.

Some commentators have said that the phrase "La Misas" refers to one of the penal laws of the Sharia of Moses (peace be upon him) issued regarding certain individuals who committed a heavy sin. That person would become like a being who was filthy, impure, and unclean; no one would touch him, and he had no right to touch anyone. [^1] After this event, Samiri was forced to leave the Children of Israel, their city, and land, and wander in the deserts. This is the punishment for an ambitious man who, through his innovations, wanted to lead large groups astray and gather them around himself. He must fail, and not even one person should contact him. For such individuals, this absolute rejection and complete isolation is harder than death or execution, as they are driven away from everywhere as a filthy and contaminated being.

Some commentators have also said that after Samiri's crime and great error were proven, Moses prayed against him, and Allah afflicted him with a mysterious disease such that as long as he lived, no one could touch him, and if anyone did, they would become afflicted with the disease.

Or, Samiri became afflicted with a type of psychological illness in the form of severe obsession and fear of any human, such that whenever anyone approached him, he would cry out "La Misas!" (Touch me not!). [^1]

Samiri's second punishment was that Moses (peace be upon him) warned him of his penalty in the Hereafter, saying: "And indeed, you have an appointment—the promise of painful divine punishment—which will never be failed" (وَإِنَّ لَكَ مَوْعِدًا لَنْ تُخْلَفَهُ). [^2]

The third program was that Moses said to Samiri: "Look at your god to which you remained devoted; we will surely burn it and then scatter its particles into the sea" (so that it is obliterated forever) (وَانْظُرْ إِلَى إِلَهِكَ الَّذِي ظَلْتَ عَلَيْهِ عَاكِفًا لَنُحَرِّقَنَّهُ ثُمَّ لَنَنْسِفَنَّهُ فِي الْيَمِّ نَسْفًا).

Here, two questions arise:

First: The phrase "We will surely burn it" (لنحرقنه) is evidence that the calf was a combustible body, which supports the view of those who say the calf was not gold but had turned into a living being due to the dust from Gabriel's feet.

In response, we say: The apparent meaning of the phrase "a body that had a lowing sound" (جَسَدًا لَهُ خُوارٌ) is that the calf was a lifeless statue from which a sound similar to a calf's lowing (by the methods we previously mentioned) emanated. As for the matter of burning, it could be for one of two reasons: one is that this statue was not made solely of gold but likely had wood used in it, with gold as a covering.

The other is that even assuming it was entirely made of gold, burning it was for the purpose of humiliation, insult, and destroying its shape and appearance, just as this action was repeated in the case of the metal statues of the tyrannical kings of our era! Therefore, after burning it, they crushed it with tools and then threw its particles into the sea.

The second question: Was throwing all this gold into the sea permissible? And was it not considered wastefulness (israf)?

The answer: Sometimes, for a high and more important goal, such as crushing the ideology of idolatry, it becomes necessary to deal with an idol in this manner lest the material of corruption remains among the people and continues to be a source of temptation for some.

To put it more clearly, if Moses (peace be upon him) had left the gold used in making the calf or, for example, distributed it among the people, it was still possible that one day ignorant individuals would look at it with a sense of sanctity, and the memory of calf-worship would be revived in them. Here, this precious material had to be sacrificed to preserve the people's faith, and there was no other way. In this way, Moses, with the exceptionally decisive method he took toward both Samiri and his calf, was able to dismantle the crisis of calf-worship and sweep its psychological effects from their minds. Later, we will also see that through the decisive encounter he had with the calf-worshippers, he so influenced the minds of the Children of Israel that they would never follow such deviant paths in the future. [^1]

[^1]: Tafsir Fi Zilal, Vol. 5, p. 494. [^1]: Tafsir Qurtubi, Vol. 6, p. 4281. [^2]: "لَنْ تُخْلَفَهُ" is a passive verb where the pro-agent (na'ib fa'il) is Samiri, and the pronoun is the second object, while the original agent is Allah. The overall meaning is: "For you is an appointment which Allah will not fail to keep regarding you."
