And in Moses' final sentence, with great emphasis on the issue of monotheism, he defined the sovereignty of the path of Allah and said: "Your god is only Allah, the One besides whom there is no deity; He has encompassed all things in knowledge" (إِنَّمَا إِلَهُكُمُ اللَّهُ الَّذِي لَا إِلَهَ إِلَّا هُوَ وَسِعَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ عِلْمًا). Not like fabricated idols that neither hear a word, nor give an answer, nor solve a problem, nor repel any harm.

In fact, the phrase "He has encompassed all things in knowledge" stands in contrast to the description given a few verses earlier regarding the calf and its ignorance and helplessness.

Key Points

1. One Must Stand Firm Against Severe Events

The method of Moses (peace be upon him) against the deviation of calf-worship by the Children of Israel is a method that can be emulated for any time and place in the field of combating severe and complex deviations.

If Moses (peace be upon him) had wanted to stand against hundreds of thousands of calf-worshippers with only advice, preaching, and some reasoning, he certainly would not have achieved anything. He had to stand decisively against three currents: against his brother, against Samiri, and against the calf-worshippers. First, he started with his "brother," seizing his beard and pulling him and shouting at him, effectively forming a court for him (even though his innocence was eventually proven to the people) so that others would take heed.

Then he went after the main agent of the conspiracy, "Samiri," and condemned him to a punishment worse than death: expulsion from society, isolating him, and turning him into a filthy and contaminated being from whom everyone must keep their distance, and threatening him with the painful punishment of the Lord.

Then he came to the calf-worshippers of the Children of Israel and made them realize that this sin of theirs was so great that there was no way to repent for it except to put the sword among themselves, and for a group to be killed by each other's hands, so that this "impure blood" would be drained from the body of this society. In this way, a group of sinners was executed by their own hands so that this dangerous deviant thought would be removed from their minds forever. We have explained the details of this event in Volume 1 under verses 51 to 54 of Surah Al-Baqarah under the title "An Unprecedented Repentance."

Thus, he first went to the leader of the community to see if he had been negligent in his duty, and after his innocence was established, he went after the agent of corruption, and then after the supporters and followers of corruption.

2. Who is Samiri?

The origin of the word "Samiri" in the Hebrew language is "Shimri." Since it is common when Hebrew words are dressed in Arabic garb for the letter "Shin" to change to "Sin"—just as "Moshe" becomes "Musa" and "Yeshua" becomes "Yasua"—therefore, Samiri is attributed to "Shimron," and Shimron is the son of Issachar, the fourth generation of Jacob.

From this, it becomes clear that the criticism of some Christians against the Holy Quran—claiming that the Quran identifies the person who lived in the time of Moses and became the leader of calf-worship as a "Samiri" attributed to the city of "Samaria," whereas Samaria did not exist at that time—is baseless. For as we said, Samiri is attributed to Shimron, not Samaria. [^1] In any case, Samiri was a self-centered and deviant yet intelligent man who, with special courage and skill, utilized the weaknesses of the Children of Israel to create such a great fitna (trial) that caused the overwhelming majority to turn to idolatry. As we saw, he received the punishment for this selfishness and warmongering in this very world.

[^1]: A'lam al-Quran, p. 359.

Ayahs

كَذَٰلِكَ نَقُصُّ عَلَيْكَ مِنْ أَنۢبَآءِ مَا قَدْ سَبَقَ ۚ وَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَـٰكَ مِن لَّدُنَّا ذِكْرًا (٩٩)
Thus do We relate to you some accounts of what is past. Certainly, We have given you a Reminder from Ourselves. (99)
مَّنْ أَعْرَضَ عَنْهُ فَإِنَّهُۥ يَحْمِلُ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ وِزْرًا (١٠٠)
Whoever disregards it shall bear a burden [of punishment] on the Day of Resurrection, (100)
خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهِ ۖ وَسَآءَ لَهُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ حِمْلًا (١٠١)
remaining in it [forever]. Evil is their burden on the Day of Resurrection (101)
يَوْمَ يُنفَخُ فِى ٱلصُّورِ ۚ وَنَحْشُرُ ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ يَوْمَئِذٍ زُرْقًا (١٠٢)
—the day the Trumpet will be blown; on that day We shall muster the guilty with blind eyes. (102)
يَتَخَـٰفَتُونَ بَيْنَهُمْ إِن لَّبِثْتُمْ إِلَّا عَشْرًا (١٠٣)
They will whisper to one another: ‘You have stayed only for ten [days].’ (103)
نَّحْنُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا يَقُولُونَ إِذْ يَقُولُ أَمْثَلُهُمْ طَرِيقَةً إِن لَّبِثْتُمْ إِلَّا يَوْمًا (١٠٤)
We know best what they will say, when the smartest of them in approach will say, ‘You stayed only a day!’ (104)

Commentary

The Worst Burden They Carry!

Although various discussions were presented throughout the previous verses concerning the eventful history of Moses, the Children of Israel, the Pharaohs, and Samiri, the Quran provides a general conclusion after finishing these accounts, stating: "Thus do We relate to you some of the news of what has preceded" (كَذلِكَ نَقُصُّ عَلَيْكَ مِنْ أَنْباءِ ما قَدْ سَبَقَ).

It then adds: "And We have certainly given you from Us a Reminder [the Quran]" (وَ قَدْ آتَيْناكَ مِنْ لَدُنَّا ذِكْراً).

A Quran filled with lessons of admonition, rational proofs, instructive news of the predecessors, and awakening matters for those to come.

In principle, a significant portion of the Holy Quran is dedicated to relating the stories of the ancients. Mentioning all this history of the past in the Quran—which is a book of human development—is not without reason. Its purpose is to benefit from various aspects of their history: the factors behind victory and defeat, the causes of prosperity and misery, and to utilize the vast experiences hidden within the pages of their history.

Generally, among the most reliable sciences are the empirical sciences, which are tested in a laboratory and whose objective results are witnessed.

History is the great laboratory of human life. In this laboratory, the honor and defeat of nations, successes and failures, happiness and misfortunes have all been put to the test, and their objective results are before our eyes. We can learn some of our most reliable knowledge regarding life's issues from it.

In other words, the sum of human life—from one perspective—is nothing but experience. History, provided it is free from any distortion, is the product of thousands of years of human life, made available in one place for those who study it.

For this reason, the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, in his wise counsels to his son, Imam al-Mujtaba عليه السلام, specifically emphasized this point, saying:

"O my son! Even though I have not lived as long as those who came before me, I have looked into their deeds, reflected upon their reports, and traveled through their remains until I became like one of them. Rather, it is as if, through what has reached me of their affairs, I have lived with them from the first of them to the last of them. Thus, I recognized the pure from the turbid, and the beneficial from the harmful, and I have extracted for you the quintessence of every matter." [1]

Therefore, history is a mirror reflecting the past and a link connecting today with yesterday, expanding a person's lifespan by its own measure! History is a teacher that recounts the secrets of the glory and fall of nations; it warns the oppressors, visualizing the grim fate of previous tyrants who were more powerful than them; it gives glad tidings to the people of truth, inviting them to steadfastness and encouraging them on their path.

History is a lamp that illuminates the path of human life and opens and levels the roads for the movement of people today.

History is the educator of the people of today, and the people of today are the builders of the history of tomorrow.

In short, history is one of the means of Divine guidance.

However, let there be no mistake: just as the narration of a true history is constructive and educational, fabricated and distorted histories are extremely misleading. For this reason, those with diseased hearts have always tried to deceive people and turn them away from the path of Allah by distorting history. We must not forget that distortion in history is frequent. [2]

It is also necessary to mention that the word "Dhikr" (Reminder) here and in many other verses of the Quran refers to the Quran itself, because its verses cause people to remember, awaken, and be vigilant.

Consequently, the next verse speaks of those who forget the truths of the Quran and the lessons of history, saying: "Whoever turns away from it—then indeed, he will bear on the Day of Resurrection a heavy burden [of sin and responsibility]" (مَنْ أَعْرَضَ عَنْهُ فَإِنَّهُ يَحْمِلُ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ وِزْراً).

Yes, turning away from the Lord leads a person into such wildernesses that it places heavy burdens of various sins and intellectual and ideological deviations upon his shoulders.

(In principle, the word wizr itself means a heavy burden, and its mention in the indefinite form [nakirah] adds more emphasis in this regard).

It then adds: "Abiding eternally therein" (خالِدِينَ فِيهِ). "And evil for them on the Day of Resurrection is that burden" (وَ ساءَ لَهُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ حِمْلًا).

It is noteworthy that the pronoun "therein" (fihi) in this verse refers back to "burden" (wizr), meaning they will remain forever within that very burden, responsibility, and heavy load. (There is no reason to assume an omitted word here and say they remain eternally in punishment or in Hell). This itself is an allusion to the issue of the "embodiment of deeds" (tajassum al-a'mal)—that a person is rewarded or punished on the Day of Resurrection by the very deeds and actions they performed in this world.

Then, describing the Day of Resurrection and its beginning, it says: "The Day the Horn will be blown and We will gather the criminals that Day, zurqa" (يَوْمَ يُنْفَخُ فِي الصُّورِ وَ نَحْشُرُ الْمُجْرِمِينَ يَوْمَئِذٍ زُرْقاً).

As we have previously mentioned, it is understood from the Quranic verses that the end of this world and the beginning of the next will occur with two sudden, revolutionary movements, each referred to as the "Blowing of the Horn" (Nafkh al-Sur). By Allah's will, we will explain this under verse 68 of Surah Az-Zumar.

The word zurq (plural of azraq) usually means "blue-eyed," but it is sometimes applied to someone whose body has become dark and discolored due to the intensity of pain and suffering. This is because when the body endures pain and suffering, it becomes thin and weak, loses its freshness and moisture, and appears discolored.

Some have also interpreted this word to mean "blind," because sometimes blue-eyed individuals have extreme visual weakness, usually accompanied by blondness of all body hair. However, what we mentioned in the first interpretation is perhaps the best of all.

In this state, the criminals whisper among themselves regarding the duration of their stay in the world of Barzakh (the purgatory). Some say: "You stayed not but ten [nights or days]" (يَتَخافَتُونَ بَيْنَهُمْ إِنْ لَبِثْتُمْ إِلَّا عَشْراً). [3]

Undoubtedly, the duration of their stay in the world of Barzakh was long, but compared to the lifespan of the Hereafter, it seems like a very short period. Their whispering is either due to the intense terror and horror that grips them upon witnessing the scene of the Resurrection, or due to extreme weakness and frailty.

Some commentators have also suggested that this sentence might refer to their stay in the world, as the Hereafter and its terrifying events make it seem like only a few short days.

It then adds: "We are most knowing of what they say" (نَحْنُ أَعْلَمُ بِما يَقُولُونَ).

Whether they speak softly or loudly.

"When the best of them in manner says, 'You have remained only a day'" (إِذْ يَقُولُ أَمْثَلُهُمْ طَرِيقَةً إِنْ لَبِثْتُمْ إِلَّا يَوْماً).

Certainly, neither ten days is a long time, nor is one day; however, the difference is that "one day" refers to the smallest of the single digits, while "ten days" refers to the smallest of the tens. Therefore, the former indicates a shorter duration. For this reason, the Quran describes the speaker of these words as amthaluhum tariqatan (the one best in conduct and thought), because the brevity of the life of the world or Barzakh compared to the life of the Hereafter—as well as the insignificance of their quality compared to its quality—is more consistent with the smallest number (reflect on this).

[^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Letter 31 (from the section of letters). [^2]: We have discussed history and its importance at the beginning of Surah Yusuf and its end (Vol. 9, p. 292 and Vol. 10, p. 100), as well as under verse 120 of Surah Hud (Vol. 9, p. 283). [^3]: From the perspective of Arabic grammar, since 'ashr (ten) is used here in the masculine form, its elided noun (mudaf ilayh) must be layal (nights), which is feminine. If the elided noun were ayyam (days), it should have been said 'asharah. However, it is narrated from some Arabic linguists that when a number is mentioned alone and its specification (tamyiz) is omitted, the previous rule is no longer strictly applied. Therefore, 'ashr here refers to ten days.

They question you concerning the mountains. Say, ‘My Lord will scatter them [like dust].’ (105)
فَيَذَرُهَا قَاعًا صَفْصَفًا (١٠٦)
Then He will leave it a level plain. (106)
لَّا تَرَىٰ فِيهَا عِوَجًا وَلَآ أَمْتًا (١٠٧)

يَوْمَئِذٍ يَتَّبِعُونَ ٱلدَّاعِىَ لَا عِوَجَ لَهُۥ ۖ وَخَشَعَتِ ٱلْأَصْوَاتُ لِلرَّحْمَـٰنِ فَلَا تَسْمَعُ إِلَّا هَمْسًا (١٠٨)

يَوْمَئِذٍ لَّا تَنفَعُ ٱلشَّفَـٰعَةُ إِلَّا مَنْ أَذِنَ لَهُ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ وَرَضِىَ لَهُۥ قَوْلًا (١٠٩)

يَعْلَمُ مَا بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَمَا خَلْفَهُمْ وَلَا يُحِيطُونَ بِهِۦ عِلْمًا (١١٠)

۞ وَعَنَتِ ٱلْوُجُوهُ لِلْحَىِّ ٱلْقَيُّومِ ۖ وَقَدْ خَابَ مَنْ حَمَلَ ظُلْمًا (١١١)
