وَمَن يَعْمَلْ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَلَا يَخَافُ ظُلْمًا وَلَا هَضْمًا (١١٢)
But whoever does righteous deeds, being faithful, will neither fear any injustice or disparagement. (112)

Commentary

The Terrifying Scene of Resurrection

Since the previous verses discussed events related to the end of the world and the beginning of the Resurrection, the verses under discussion follow the same subject.

From the first verse, it appears that people had asked the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله about the fate of the mountains at the end of the world; perhaps because they could not believe that such magnificent entities, whose roots are sunk deep into the earth and whose peaks reach the sky, could be shaken. Furthermore, they might have wondered what wind or storm could possibly possess the power to uproot them if they were to be moved.

Thus, He says: "And they ask you about the mountains" (وَ يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْجِبالِ‌).

In response: "Say, 'My Lord will break them into pieces and turn them into pebbles, then scatter them like dust!'" (فَقُلْ يَنْسِفُها رَبِّي نَسْفاً) [^1]. From the collection of Quranic verses regarding the fate of the mountains, it is understood that they pass through several stages on the threshold of the Resurrection:

First, they are shaken (يَوْمَ تَرْجُفُ الْأَرْضُ وَ الْجِبالُ‌—Muzzammil: 14).

Then, they are set in motion (وَ تَسِيرُ الْجِبالُ سَيْراً—Tur: 10). In the third stage, they are broken apart and become a heap of sand (وَ كانَتِ الْجِبالُ كَثِيباً مَهِيلًا—Muzzammil: 14).

And in the final stage, such a storm and wind move them from their place and scatter them in the air that they appear like carded wool (وَ تَكُونُ الْجِبالُ كَالْعِهْنِ الْمَنْفُوشِ‌—Al-Qari'ah: 5).

The next verse says: With the disintegration of the mountains and the scattering of their particles, "Allah will turn the surface of the earth into a smooth, level plain, devoid of water and vegetation" (فَيَذَرُها قاعاً صَفْصَفاً) [^2].

"Such that you will observe therein no crookedness, nor any depression or elevation" (لا تَرى‌ فِيها عِوَجاً وَ لا أَمْتاً) [^1].

"At this time, the Divine caller invites the people to life and to gather at the Place of Assembly and for the Account, and everyone, without exception, responds to his call and follows him" (يَوْمَئِذٍ يَتَّبِعُونَ الدَّاعِيَ لا عِوَجَ لَهُ‌). Is this caller "Israfil" or another of Allah's great angels?

It is not precisely specified in the Quran, but whoever it may be, his command is so influential that no one has the power to disobey it.

The phrase "لا عِوَجَ لَهُ‌" (there is no deviation in it) could be a description of the call of this caller, or a description of the following by those who are called, or both. It is noteworthy that just as the surface of the earth becomes so smooth and level that there is not the slightest crookedness in it, the Divine command and His caller are also so straight and direct, and the following of him is so definite, that no deviation or crookedness finds its way into it.

"At this moment, voices shall be humbled before the majesty of the All-Merciful Lord, and you shall hear nothing but a faint sound" (وَ خَشَعَتِ الْأَصْواتُ لِلرَّحْمنِ فَلا تَسْمَعُ إِلَّا هَمْساً) [^2].

[^1]: The root "nasf" (نسف) literally means to pour food grains into a sieve and shake or winnow them so the husk is separated from the grain. Here, it refers to the mountains being shattered and crushed, and then scattered by the wind.

[^2]: "Qa’" (قاع) is smooth and level ground, and some have interpreted it as a place where water collects. As for "safsaf" (صفصف), it is sometimes interpreted as land devoid of any vegetation, and sometimes as level ground. From the combination of these two descriptions, it is understood that on 그날, the mountains and plants will all vanish from the face of the earth, leaving a smooth and simple land.

[^1]: "Iwaj" (عوج) means crookedness and hollows, and "amt" (أمت) means high ground and hills. Therefore, the verse collectively means that on that day, no depressions or elevations will be seen on the earth.

[^2]: "Hams" (همس), as Raghib says in Al-Mufradat, means a low and hidden sound. Some have interpreted it as the soft sound of footsteps (bare feet), and some as the movement of lips without any audible sound, which are not very different from each other.

This silencing of voices is either due to the dominance of Divine Majesty over the arena of Resurrection, before which everyone humbles themselves, or out of fear of the Account and the result of one's deeds, or both.

Since some might fall into the error of thinking they can be drowned in sin and yet be saved through the intercession of intercessors, He immediately adds: "On that day, no one's intercession shall avail except for those to whom the All-Merciful has given permission to intercede, and whose word He is pleased with in this regard" (يَوْمَئِذٍ لا تَنْفَعُ الشَّفاعَةُ إِلَّا مَنْ أَذِنَ لَهُ الرَّحْمنُ وَ رَضِيَ لَهُ قَوْلًا). This indicates that intercession there is not without criteria; rather, it follows a precise program regarding both the intercessor and the one being interceded for. Unless there is merit and worthiness in individuals to be interceded for, intercession has no meaning.

The truth is that a group has false notions of intercession, viewing it as not unlike the nepotism and favoritism of this world. However, from the perspective of Islamic logic, intercession is a high class of education; it is a lesson for those who traverse the path of truth with the feet of effort and striving, but who occasionally fall victim to shortcomings and slips. These slips might scatter the dust of despair and hopelessness upon their hearts. It is here that intercession comes to them as a motivating force, saying: "Do not despair; continue on the path of truth and do not give up striving in this way. If a slip has occurred from you, there are intercessors who, by the permission of the All-Merciful Allah—Whose general mercy encompasses everyone—will intercede for you."

Intercession is not an invitation to laziness, or an escape from responsibility, or a green light for committing sins. Intercession is an invitation to steadfastness on the path of truth and the reduction of sin to the greatest extent possible.

Although we have discussed the issue of intercession in detail in Volume 1 under verses 47 and 48 of Surah Al-Baqarah and Volume 2 under verse 255 of Surah Al-Baqarah, it is not bad to add an interesting story here: The late godly scholar Mr. Yaseri, one of the respected scholars of Tehran, used to narrate that a poet named "Hajib," who had fallen into common misconceptions regarding intercession, composed a poem to this effect: O Hajib! If the dealing of the Resurrection is with Ali, I guarantee that you may commit as many sins as you wish!! That night, he saw the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, in a dream. While he was upset and angry, he said: "You did not compose a good poem!" He asked: "What should I say?" He replied: "Correct your poem like this: O Hajib! If the dealing of the Resurrection is with Ali, Feel shame before the face of Ali and commit fewer sins!"

And since the presence of people in the arena of Resurrection for the Account and Recompense requires Allah's knowledge of their deeds and behavior, He adds in the next verse: "Allah knows what is before them and what they have left behind in the world; He is aware of all their past actions, words, and intentions, and the reward or punishment they face in the future. But they do not encompass Him in knowledge" (يَعْلَمُ ما بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَ ما خَلْفَهُمْ وَ لا يُحِيطُونَ بِهِ عِلْماً) [^1].

[^1]: Some commentators have suggested that the plural pronouns in the first phrase refer to the intercessors, and some have suggested that the pronoun in "bihi" (به) refers to the deeds of the criminals and their results. However, what we stated above seems more correct (reflect upon it).

In this way, Allah's scientific encompassment pertains both to their deeds and to their recompense. These two are, in fact, the two pillars of a perfect and just judgment: that the judge is fully aware of the events that occurred and of the ruling and its penalty.

"On that day, all people shall be completely humbled before the Ever-Living, the Self-Subsisting Lord" (وَ عَنَتِ الْوُجُوهُ لِلْحَيِّ الْقَيُّومِ‌). "Anat" (عنت) comes from the root "anwah" (عنوة), meaning humility and lowliness. Thus, a captive is called "ani" (عانى) because he is humble and lowly in the hands of the captor.

The reason we see humility attributed here to "wujuh" (faces) is that the effects of all psychological phenomena, including humility, first appear on the human face.

Some commentators have also suggested that "wujuh" here means "leaders," chiefs, and rulers, who on 그날 will all be lowly and humble before Allah (but the first interpretation seems more appropriate).

The choice of the attributes "Al-Hayy" (The Ever-Living) and "Al-Qayyum" (The Self-Subsisting) from among Allah's attributes here is due to the suitability these two attributes have with the issue of Resurrection, which is the day of life and the standing (qiyam) of all.

At the end of the verse, He adds: "Desperate and hopeless of Divine reward are those who carry the burden of oppression and injustice" (وَ قَدْ خابَ مَنْ حَمَلَ ظُلْماً). It is as if oppression and injustice are like a massive burden weighing down on a person's shoulders, preventing him from progressing toward the eternal Divine blessings. The oppressors and wrongdoers—whether those who oppressed themselves or others—will look on hopelessly as they see with their own eyes the light-burdened ones going toward Paradise, while they, under the heavy burden of oppression, are kneeling beside Hell, filled with regret!

Since the method of the Quran is often the comparative presentation of issues, after mentioning the fate of the oppressors and criminals on that day, it proceeds to describe the state of the believers, saying: "But whoever performs righteous deeds while being a believer, they shall fear neither injustice nor the withholding of their right" (وَ مَنْ يَعْمَلْ مِنَ الصَّالِحاتِ وَ هُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَلا يَخافُ ظُلْماً وَ لا هَضْماً) [^1]. The expression "مِنَ الصَّالِحاتِ‌" (of righteous deeds) indicates that if they cannot perform all righteous deeds, they at least perform a portion of them. For faith without righteous deeds is a tree without fruit, just as righteous deeds without faith are a tree without roots, which might stand for a few days but will eventually wither. For this reason, after mentioning righteous deeds in the above verse, the condition "while being a believer" is added.

Fundamentally, "righteous deeds" cannot exist without faith, and even if faithless individuals occasionally perform good deeds, they will undoubtedly be limited, weak, and exceptional. In other words, for righteous deeds to be performed in a continuous, rooted, and deep manner, they must be nourished by a pure creed and correct belief.

Key Points

1- The Difference Between "Zulm" and "Hadm"

In the final phrase of the verses under discussion, we read that the righteous believers on that day fear neither "zulm" (injustice) nor "hadm" (withholding/diminution). Some commentators have said that "zulm" refers to the fact that in that Court of Justice, they never fear that an injustice will be done to them or that they will be held accountable for a sin they did not commit. "Hadm" refers to the fact that they also have no fear of their reward being diminished, for they know their recompense will be given in full.

Others have suggested that the first refers to not fearing the destruction of all their good deeds, and the second refers to not fearing even the reduction of a small amount of them, because the Divine account is precise.

There is also the possibility that these righteous believers might have had some slips; they are certain that these slips will not be recorded against them as more than what they were, and that nothing will be subtracted from the reward of their righteous deeds.

These interpretations are not mutually exclusive, and the above phrase may refer to all these meanings.

2- The Stages of Resurrection

In the above verses, a series of events that occur on the threshold of Resurrection and after it are mentioned:

  1. The dead return to life (يَوْمَ يُنْفَخُ فِي الصُّورِ).
  2. The sinners are gathered and assembled (نَحْشُرُ الْمُجْرِمِينَ‌).
  3. The mountains of the earth are disintegrated and then scattered everywhere, and the surface of the earth becomes smooth and completely level (يَنْسِفُها رَبِّي نَسْفاً).
  4. Everyone listens to the command of the Divine caller, and all voices become silent and faint (يَوْمَئِذٍ يَتَّبِعُونَ الدَّاعِيَ ...).
  5. On that day, intercession is not effective without Allah's permission (يَوْمَئِذٍ لا تَنْفَعُ الشَّفاعَةُ ...).
  6. Allah, with His infinite knowledge, prepares everyone for the Account (يَعْلَمُ ما بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ ...).
  7. Everyone bows in submission before His judgment (وَ عَنَتِ الْوُجُوهُ لِلْحَيِّ الْقَيُّومِ‌).
  8. The oppressors become desperate (وَ قَدْ خابَ مَنْ حَمَلَ ظُلْماً).
  9. And the believers are hopeful of the Lord's grace (وَ مَنْ يَعْمَلْ مِنَ الصَّالِحاتِ وَ هُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ ...).

[^1]: "Hadm" (هضم) literally means deficiency or reduction. If the absorption of food into the body is called "hadm" (digestion), it is because the food apparently decreases and its waste remains.

Ayahs

وَكَذَٰلِكَ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ قُرْءَانًا عَرَبِيًّا وَصَرَّفْنَا فِيهِ مِنَ ٱلْوَعِيدِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَّقُونَ أَوْ يُحْدِثُ لَهُمْ ذِكْرًا (١١٣)
Thus We have sent it down as an Arabic Quran and We have paraphrased the warnings in it variously so that they may be Godwary, or it may prompt them to remembrance. (113)
فَتَعَـٰلَى ٱللَّهُ ٱلْمَلِكُ ٱلْحَقُّ ۗ وَلَا تَعْجَلْ بِٱلْقُرْءَانِ مِن قَبْلِ أَن يُقْضَىٰٓ إِلَيْكَ وَحْيُهُۥ ۖ وَقُل رَّبِّ زِدْنِى عِلْمًا (١١٤)
So exalted is Allah, the True Sovereign. Do not hasten with the Quran before its revelation is completed for you, and say, ‘My Lord! Increase me in knowledge.’ (114)

Commentary

Say: "O My Lord, Increase Me in Knowledge"

The above verses are, in fact, an allusion to the sum of what was mentioned in the preceding verses regarding the educational matters related to the Resurrection, the promises, and the warnings.

It says: "And thus We have sent it down as an Arabic Quran (eloquent and expressive) and We have explained therein various warnings in different forms and expressions, that perhaps they may become God-fearing, or at least it may serve as a reminder for them" (وَ كَذلِكَ أَنْزَلْناهُ قُرْآناً عَرَبِيًّا وَ صَرَّفْنا فِيهِ مِنَ الْوَعِيدِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَّقُونَ أَوْ يُحْدِثُ لَهُمْ ذِكْراً).

The expression "And thus" (كذلك) is actually a reference to the matters explained before this verse. It is exactly as if a person speaks awakening and cautionary words to another and then adds: "In this manner one should give counsel." (Therefore, there is no need for the other interpretations that some commentators have mentioned here, which are far from the meaning of the verse).

The word "Arabic" (عربي), although it means the Arabic language, refers here to the eloquence and rhetoric of the Quran and the clarity of its concepts from two perspectives:

First: Fundamentally, the Arabic language—according to the world's linguists—is one of the most expressive languages, and its literature is among the most powerful.

Second: Sometimes the phrase "We have explained" (صرفنا) refers to the various presentations the Quran provides for a single reality. For instance, it presents the issue of warnings and the punishment of criminals sometimes in the guise of the history of past nations, sometimes in the form of addressing those present, sometimes by depicting their state on the scene of the Resurrection, and sometimes in other forms.

The difference between the phrase "that perhaps they may become God-fearing" (لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَّقُونَ) and the phrase "or it may serve as a reminder for them" (يُحْدِثُ لَهُمْ ذِكْراً) might be from the perspective that in the first phrase, the goal is the attainment of complete piety (Taqwa), while in the second phrase, the goal is that if complete piety is not achieved, at least an awakening and awareness should occur that limits the person to some extent for now and becomes a source of movement and action in the future.

There is also the possibility that the first phrase refers to the realization of piety for the non-pious, and the second refers to the reminder and remembrance for the pious, as we read in verse 2 of Surah Al-Anfal: "When His verses are recited to them, it increases them in faith" (إِذا تُلِيَتْ عَلَيْهِمْ آياتُهُ زادَتْهُمْ إِيماناً).

In truth, the above verse points to two effective principles of education: first, the issue of clarity in expression and the eloquence of phrases and their being lucid and heart-warming; and second, presenting matters in various forms so that it does not lead to repetition and boredom, and becomes a cause for penetrating the hearts.

The next verse adds: "Exalted is Allah, the True Sovereign" (فَتَعالَى اللَّهُ الْمَلِكُ الْحَقُّ). The mention of the word "True" (الحق) after the word "Sovereign" (الملك) might be because people usually have a bad memory of the word "Sovereign" (King/Ruler), and oppression, tyranny, and despotism are associated with it in their minds; therefore, He immediately says: "Allah is the True Sovereign."

And since the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, due to his love for learning the Quran and memorizing it for the people, would sometimes hasten during the reception of revelation and would not give Jibril (Gabriel) full time to finish his speech, he is reminded in the continuation of this verse: "And do not hasten with the Quran before its revelation is completed to you" (وَ لا تَعْجَلْ بِالْقُرْآْنِ مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ يُقْضى‌ إِلَيْكَ وَحْيُهُ).

"And say: 'My Lord, increase me in knowledge'" (وَ قُلْ رَبِّ زِدْنِي عِلْماً).

It is also understood from some other verses of the Quran that the Prophet had a special fervor during the descent of revelation which caused him to hasten to receive it, such as: "Move not your tongue with it to hasten with it. Indeed, upon Us is its collection and its recitation. So when We have recited it, then follow its recitation" (لا تُحَرِّكْ بِهِ لِسانَكَ لِتَعْجَلَ بِهِ إِنَّ عَلَيْنا جَمْعَهُ وَ قُرْآنَهُ فَإِذا قَرَأْناهُ فَاتَّبِعْ قُرْآنَهُ). [1]

[1]: Surah Al-Qiyamah, verses 16 to 18.

Key Points

1- Do Not Hasten Even in Receiving Revelation

The final sentences contained instructive lessons, including the prohibition of hastening during the reception of revelation. It is often seen that when hearing a speaker, some people begin to repeat or complete the point before it is finished. This sometimes stems from a lack of patience, and sometimes from pride and self-assertion, but sometimes it is driven by a great love for receiving the subject and fulfilling a mission, in which case it has a sacred motive. However, the act itself—namely hastening—often creates problems. For this reason, the above verses prohibit this act, even if it is for a correct purpose. Fundamentally, actions performed hastily will not be free of flaws and defects. Certainly, the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله action was immune to error and mistake due to his status of infallibility (Ismah), but he must be a role model and example for the people in everything, so that people realize that if one should not show haste in receiving revelation, the status of other matters is clear.

Of course, haste (Ajalah) should not be confused with speed (Sur'ah). Speed is when a program is fully organized and all issues are calculated, then the program is implemented without wasting time. But haste is when the program is not yet fully matured and needs completion and review. For this reason, speed is desirable, while haste and precipitancy are undesirable.

Of course, other possibilities have been mentioned in the interpretation of this sentence, including that sometimes when the revelation was delayed, the Prophet would become restless; the verse teaches him: "Do not be restless, We will reveal to you what is necessary at its proper time." Some have said that since the verses of the Holy Quran were revealed once collectively to the heart of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله on the Night of Power (Laylat al-Qadr), and once gradually over a period of 23 years, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله would sometimes precede Jibril during the gradual revelation of the verses. The Quran commands: "Do not hasten in this and let the gradual revelation of each occur at its own time."

However, the first interpretation seems closer to the meaning.

2- Be Ambitious in Knowledge

Since the prohibition of hastening during the reception of revelation might create the illusion that seeking more knowledge is prohibited, this thought is immediately prevented by the sentence: "Say: 'My Lord, increase me in knowledge'" (قُلْ رَبِّ زِدْنِي عِلْماً). That is, precipitancy is not correct, but striving to increase knowledge is necessary.

Some commentators have also said that in the first sentence, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was commanded not to hasten in the comprehensive understanding of the verses before their clarification in other verses, and in the second sentence, he was commanded to ask Allah for more awareness regarding the various dimensions of the Quranic verses.

In any case, where the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, with that overflowing knowledge and a soul full of awareness, is commanded to seek an increase in knowledge from Allah until the end of his life, the duty of others is perfectly clear. In truth, from the perspective of Islam, knowledge knows no boundaries. Ambition is condemned in many matters, but it is praised in knowledge. Excess is bad, but "excess" in knowledge has no meaning.

Knowledge has no geographical boundary; one must run in its pursuit even to China or the Pleiades.

It has no temporal boundary; it continues from the cradle to the grave.

It knows no boundary regarding the teacher, for wisdom is the lost property of the believer; wherever he finds it, he takes it, and if a gem falls from an impure mouth, he picks it up.

It has no boundary in terms of the amount of effort and struggle; he dives into the depths of the seas to acquire knowledge and even gives his precious life in the way of acquiring it.

Thus, in the logic of Islam, the term "graduate" is a meaningless word. A true Muslim's education never ends; he is always a student and a seeker of knowledge, even if he becomes the greatest master.

Interestingly, we read in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام that he said to one of his companions: "We have a special joy and happiness every Friday night." He asked: "May Allah increase this joy, what is this joy?" He replied:

"When it is Friday night, the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله reaches the Throne (Arsh), and the Imams عليه السلام reach it, and we reach it with them. Our souls do not return to our bodies except with acquired knowledge, and were it not for that, our knowledge would be exhausted!" [1] This theme has been expressed in numerous narrations with various wordings, showing that the Prophet and the Imams increase in their knowledge until the end of the world.

In another narration from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, we read that he said:

"If a day comes upon me in which I do not increase in knowledge that brings me closer to Allah, then may the rising of the sun of that day not be blessed for me!" [2]

Again, in another Hadith from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read:

"The most knowledgeable of people is he who adds the knowledge of the people to his own knowledge, and the most valuable of people is the one most knowledgeable, and the least valuable of people is the one with the least knowledge." [3] And this is the value of knowledge from the perspective of Islamic teachings.

[1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 397. [2]: Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, Nur al-Thaqalayn, and Safi, under the verses discussed. [3]: Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 2, p. 219 (under the root 'ilm).

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ عَهِدْنَآ إِلَىٰٓ ءَادَمَ مِن قَبْلُ فَنَسِىَ وَلَمْ نَجِدْ لَهُۥ عَزْمًا (١١٥)
Certainly We had enjoined Adam earlier; but he forgot, and We did not find any resoluteness in him. (115)
وَإِذْ قُلْنَا لِلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ ٱسْجُدُوا۟ لِـَٔادَمَ فَسَجَدُوٓا۟ إِلَّآ إِبْلِيسَ أَبَىٰ (١١٦)
When We said to the angels, ‘Prostrate before Adam,’ they prostrated, but not Iblis: he refused. (116)
فَقُلْنَا يَـٰٓـَٔادَمُ إِنَّ هَـٰذَا عَدُوٌّ لَّكَ وَلِزَوْجِكَ فَلَا يُخْرِجَنَّكُمَا مِنَ ٱلْجَنَّةِ فَتَشْقَىٰٓ (١١٧)
We said, ‘O Adam! This is indeed an enemy of yours and your mate’s. So do not let him expel you from paradise, or you will be miserable. (117)
إِنَّ لَكَ أَلَّا تَجُوعَ فِيهَا وَلَا تَعْرَىٰ (١١٨)
You will neither be hungry in it nor naked. (118)
وَأَنَّكَ لَا تَظْمَؤُا۟ فِيهَا وَلَا تَضْحَىٰ (١١٩)
You will neither be thirsty in it, nor suffer from [the heat of] the sun.’ (119)
فَوَسْوَسَ إِلَيْهِ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ قَالَ يَـٰٓـَٔادَمُ هَلْ أَدُلُّكَ عَلَىٰ شَجَرَةِ ٱلْخُلْدِ وَمُلْكٍ لَّا يَبْلَىٰ (١٢٠)
Then Satan tempted him. He said, ‘O Adam! Shall I show you the tree of immortality, and an imperishable kingdom?’ (120)
فَأَكَلَا مِنْهَا فَبَدَتْ لَهُمَا سَوْءَٰتُهُمَا وَطَفِقَا يَخْصِفَانِ عَلَيْهِمَا مِن وَرَقِ ٱلْجَنَّةِ ۚ وَعَصَىٰٓ ءَادَمُ رَبَّهُۥ فَغَوَىٰ (١٢١)
So they both ate of it, and their nakedness became evident to them, and they began to stitch over themselves with the leaves of paradise. Adam disobeyed his Lord, and went amiss. (121)
ثُمَّ ٱجْتَبَـٰهُ رَبُّهُۥ فَتَابَ عَلَيْهِ وَهَدَىٰ (١٢٢)
Then his Lord chose him, and turned to him clemently, and guided him. (122)

Commentary

Adam and the Deception of Iblis

The major portion of this Surah dealt with the story of Musa عليه السلام and the Children of Israel, and their struggle against Pharaoh and his folk. However, in the verses under discussion and the subsequent ones, the discourse turns to the story of Adam and Eve and the struggle and enmity of Iblis toward them.

Perhaps this is an allusion to the point that the struggle between Truth and Falsehood is not confined to today or yesterday, or to Musa عليه السلام and Pharaoh; rather, it has existed since the beginning of Adam's creation and continues as such.

Although the story of Adam and Iblis has appeared many times in the Quran, in each instance it is blended with new points. Here, it first speaks of Adam's covenant with God, stating: "We had already taken a covenant from Adam before, but he forgot, and We found in him no firm resolve!" (وَلَقَدْ عَهِدْنَا إِلَىٰ آدَمَ مِن قَبْلُ فَنَسِيَ وَلَمْ نَجِدْ لَهُ عَزْمًا).

Regarding what is meant by this "covenant," some have said it refers to God's command not to approach the forbidden tree; several narrations also support this interpretation.

Meanwhile, some commentators have offered other possibilities which can be considered branches of this meaning, such as God's warning to Adam that Satan is his sworn enemy and that he must not follow him.

As for "forgetfulness" (nisyan) here, it certainly does not mean absolute forgetting, for there is no reproach or blame in absolute forgetting. Rather, it either means "abandoning," just as in everyday speech we say to someone who has not fulfilled his promise, "It is as if you forgot your promise," meaning your action is like that of a forgetful person; or it refers to the kind of forgetfulness that arises due to lack of attention and what is termed "failure to maintain vigilance."

And the meaning of "resolve" ('azm) here is the firm decision and will that protects a human being against the powerful whisperings of Satan.

In any case, there is no doubt that Adam did not commit a sin; rather, he only committed a "leaving of the better" (tark al-awla). In other words, the period of Adam's stay in Paradise was not a period of religious obligation (taklif), but rather an experimental period to prepare for life in the world and the acceptance of the responsibility of obligations. This is especially true since God's prohibition here was of an "advisory" (irshadi) nature, as He had told him that if he ate from the forbidden tree, he would certainly fall into great hardship. (A detailed explanation of all this, as well as the meaning of the forbidden tree and the like, has been provided in Volume 6, page 115 onwards, under verses 19 to 22 of Surah Al-A'raf).

Then, referring to another part of this story, it says: "And remember when We said to the angels, 'Prostrate before Adam,' and they all prostrated except Iblis, who refused" (وَإِذْ قُلْنَا لِلْمَلَائِكَةِ اسْجُدُوا لِآدَمَ فَسَجَدُوا إِلَّا إِبْلِيسَ أَبَىٰ).

From here, the exalted status of Adam becomes clearly evident—the human being before whom the angels prostrated and who was respected by these great creatures of the Lord. Simultaneously, the enmity of Iblis toward him becomes apparent from the very first step, as he never bowed his head in submission before Adam's greatness.

There is no doubt that prostration in the sense of "worship" is exclusive to Allah, and no one and nothing besides Allah can be a deity. Therefore, the angels' prostration was for Allah, but it was on account of the creation of this magnificent being who: Is worthy of the praise of that Creator Who brings such a charming form out of water and clay! Alternatively, prostration here means "submission and humility."

In any case, at this time We warned Adam and "said: O Adam! With this event, it has become established that Iblis is an enemy to you and your wife; so let him not drive you both out of Paradise, for then you will fall into hardship and suffering" (فَقُلْنَا يَا آدَمُ إِنَّ هَٰذَا عَدُوٌّ لَّكَ وَلِزَوْجِكَ فَلَا يُخْرِجَنَّكُمَا مِنَ الْجَنَّةِ فَتَشْقَىٰ).

It is clear that "Paradise" (Jannah) here does not mean the eternal Paradise of the Hereafter, which is a point of ultimate perfection where leaving and returning backward is impossible. This Paradise was a garden containing everything, one of the gardens of this world, in which, by the grace of the Lord, no discomfort existed. Therefore, Allah warns Adam that if he leaves this point of safety and security, he will fall into trouble ("tashqa" is from the root shaqawah, and one of the meanings of shaqawah is pain and suffering).

A question arises here: why did Allah first address both of them, namely Adam and Eve, saying: فَلَا يُخْرِجَنَّكُمَا مِنَ الْجَنَّةِ (let him not drive you two out of Paradise), but mentioned the result of leaving in the singular form regarding Adam, saying: "فَتَشْقَىٰ" (then you [singular] will fall into hardship)?

This difference in expression may be an allusion to the point that hardships and sufferings were primarily directed at Adam, and he even had the duty to bear the difficulties of his wife, Eve—and such has been the responsibility of men from the very beginning! Or, since the covenant was directed at Adam from the start, the end point was also directed at him.

Then Allah describes the comfort of Paradise and the pain and suffering of the environment outside it for Adam as follows: "Indeed, it is provided for you that you will not go hungry therein, nor will you be naked" (إِنَّ لَكَ أَلَّا تَجُوعَ فِيهَا وَلَا تَعْرَىٰ).

"And that you will not suffer from thirst therein, nor will the scorching sun afflict you" (وَأَنَّكَ لَا تَظْمَأُ فِيهَا وَلَا تَضْحَىٰ).

A question has been raised by commentators here: why are thirst and the sun's heat mentioned together, and hunger and nakedness mentioned together in these verses, while usually thirst is paired with hunger?

In response to this question, it has been said that there is an undeniable link between "thirst" and the "sun's radiance" ("tadha" is from the root duha, meaning the radiance of the sun without the veil of clouds and the like).

As for the combination of hunger and nakedness, it may be because hunger is also a kind of "nakedness of the interior" from food! (It is better to say that these two—nakedness and hunger—are two distinct signs of poverty that are usually mentioned together).

In any case, these two verses refer to the four primary and basic needs of man: the need for food, water, clothing, and shelter (protection against the sun). The fulfillment of these needs in Paradise was due to the abundance of blessings, and in fact, the mention of these matters is an explanation for what appeared in the phrase "فَتَشْقَىٰ" (you will fall into hardship).

However, despite all this, Satan had girded himself with enmity and hostility toward Adam. For this reason, he did not remain quiet "and began whispering to Adam, saying: O Adam! Shall I show you the tree of eternal life, so that whoever eats of its fruit will live forever? Do you want to know the way to reach an everlasting kingdom and sovereignty?" (فَوَسْوَسَ إِلَيْهِ الشَّيْطَانُ قَالَ يَا آدَمُ هَلْ أَدُلُّكَ عَلَىٰ شَجَرَةِ الْخُلْدِ وَمُلْكٍ لَّا يَبْلَىٰ).

"Whispering" (waswasah) originally means a very faint sound; subsequently, it was applied to the occurrence of evil matters and baseless thoughts in the mind, whether they spring from within the person himself or someone from the outside becomes the cause of it.

In reality, Satan calculated what Adam's inclination was and concluded that he had a desire for eternal life and reaching an undying power. Therefore, to draw him into disobeying the Lord's command, he utilized these two factors. In other words, just as Allah promised Adam that if he kept Satan away from himself, he would always be encompassed by the Lord's blessings in Paradise, Satan also placed his finger on this very point in his whisperings. Yes, Satans always begin their programs through the same paths that the leaders of the path of Truth have started, but it does not take long before they lead it into deviation, using the attraction of the path of Truth as a means to reach the wrong paths.

Finally, what should not have happened, happened, and both Adam and Eve ate from the forbidden tree, and following that, the heavenly garments fell from their bodies and their private parts became visible! (فَأَكَلَا مِنْهَا فَبَدَتْ لَهُمَا سَوْآتُهُمَا). [^1] When Adam and Eve saw this, they immediately "began to use the leaves of the trees of Paradise to cover their bodies" (وَطَفِقَا يَخْصِفَانِ عَلَيْهِمَا مِن وَرَقِ الْجَنَّةِ). [^2] Yes, eventually "Adam disobeyed his Lord and was deprived of His reward" (وَعَصَىٰ آدَمُ رَبَّهُ فَغَوَىٰ).

"Ghawa" is derived from the root ghayy, which means an ignorant act stemming from an incorrect belief. Since Adam here, because of the suspicion created in him by Satan's words, unknowingly ate from the forbidden tree, it is expressed as "ghawa."

Some commentators have taken "ghawa" to mean ignorance resulting from heedlessness, some to mean deprivation, and some to mean corruption in life.

In any case, "ghayy" is the opposite of "guidance" (rushd). Guidance is when a person follows a path and reaches the destination, but "ghayy" is to be prevented from reaching the objective.

However, since Adam was inherently pure and a believer, and walked in the path of God's pleasure, and this error that overtook him due to Satan's whispering was exceptional in nature, Allah did not distance him from His mercy forever. Rather, "after this event, his Lord chose him, accepted his repentance, and guided him" (ثُمَّ اجْتَبَاهُ رَبُّهُ فَتَابَ عَلَيْهِ وَهَدَىٰ).

Did Adam Commit a Sin?

Although "disobedience" ('isyan) in today's common usage usually means "sin," linguistically it means "exiting from obedience and command" (regardless of whether this command is an obligatory command or a recommended one). Therefore, the use of the word 'isyan does not necessarily mean abandoning an obligation or committing a forbidden act; rather, it can be the abandonment of a recommended matter or the commission of a disliked (makruh) act.

Furthermore, sometimes "command and prohibition" have an advisory (irshadi) aspect, like the command and prohibition of a doctor who instructs a patient to take a certain medicine and avoid a certain unsuitable food. There is no doubt that if the patient violates the doctor's instruction, he only harms himself because he has ignored the doctor's advice and guidance.

Allah had also told Adam: "Do not eat of the fruit of the forbidden tree, for if you eat, you will leave Paradise and be afflicted with much pain and suffering on earth." He violated this advisory command and saw its result.

This statement takes on a clearer meaning especially considering that Adam's stay in Paradise was a period of trial, not a period of religious obligation (taklif).

Beyond this, disobedience and sin sometimes have an absolute aspect—meaning it is a sin for everyone without exception, such as lying, committing oppression, and consuming forbidden wealth. And sometimes it has a relative aspect—meaning it is an act which, if committed by one person, is not only not a sin but is sometimes a desirable and worthy act for him; however, if it is committed by another, in comparison to his status, it is an inappropriate act.

For example, to build a hospital, help is requested from the people. A laborer gives one day's wage, which is sometimes no more than a few tomans; this act is sacrifice and a good deed for him and is completely desirable. But if a wealthy person gives this same amount, not only is this act not praiseworthy for him, but it is sometimes deserving of blame and reproach, even though fundamentally he has not only not done anything forbidden but has apparently given a small amount of help to a good cause.

This is what we mean by: "The good deeds of the righteous are the sins of the near ones" (Hasanat al-abrar sayyi'at al-muqarrabin).

And this is also what is known as "leaving the better" (tark al-awla), which we refer to as "relative sin," which is neither a sin nor contrary to the status of infallibility ('ismah).

In Islamic hadiths, the term "disobedience" ('isyan) has occasionally been applied to the violation of recommended acts (mustahabbat):

In a hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read regarding the daily supererogatory prayers: "These are all recommended and not obligatory... and whoever abandons them has committed 'isyan (disobedience), because it is recommended that when a person performs a good deed, his work should be continuous." [^1] Regarding this subject and other issues related to Adam and his exit from Paradise, we have discussed them in Volume 6 of Surah Al-A'raf under verse 19 onwards, and in Volume 1 under verses 30 to 38, and there is no need for repetition.

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 404. [^1]: "Saw'at" is the plural of "saw'ah" (on the pattern of 'awrah), which originally means anything that is unpleasant; hence it is sometimes applied to a "dead body" and sometimes to "private parts," and here the latter meaning is intended. [^2]: "Yakhsifan" is from the root "khasf," meaning here to sew clothing.

Ayahs

قَالَ ٱهْبِطَا مِنْهَا جَمِيعًۢا ۖ بَعْضُكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ عَدُوٌّ ۖ فَإِمَّا يَأْتِيَنَّكُم مِّنِّى هُدًى فَمَنِ ٱتَّبَعَ هُدَاىَ فَلَا يَضِلُّ وَلَا يَشْقَىٰ (١٢٣)
He said, ‘Get down both of you from it, all together, being enemies of one another! Yet, should any guidance come to you from Me, those who follow My guidance will not go astray, nor will they be miserable. (123)
وَمَنْ أَعْرَضَ عَن ذِكْرِى فَإِنَّ لَهُۥ مَعِيشَةً ضَنكًا وَنَحْشُرُهُۥ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ أَعْمَىٰ (١٢٤)
But whoever disregards My remembrance, his shall be a wretched life, and We shall raise him blind on the Day of Resurrection.’ (124)
قَالَ رَبِّ لِمَ حَشَرْتَنِىٓ أَعْمَىٰ وَقَدْ كُنتُ بَصِيرًا (١٢٥)
He will say, ‘My Lord! Why have You raised me blind, though I used to see?’ (125)
قَالَ كَذَٰلِكَ أَتَتْكَ ءَايَـٰتُنَا فَنَسِيتَهَا ۖ وَكَذَٰلِكَ ٱلْيَوْمَ تُنسَىٰ (١٢٦)
He will say: ‘So it is. Our signs came to you, but you forgot them, and so you will be forgotten today.’ (126)
وَكَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى مَنْ أَسْرَفَ وَلَمْ يُؤْمِنۢ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ رَبِّهِۦ ۚ وَلَعَذَابُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ أَشَدُّ وَأَبْقَىٰٓ (١٢٧)
Thus do We requite those who transgress and do not believe in the signs of their Lord, and the punishment of the Hereafter is severer and more lasting. (127)

Commentary

A Constricted Life!

Although Adam's repentance was accepted, he had done something that made returning to the original state impossible. Therefore, Allah commanded him and Eve—and also the Satan who was with them—to "descend from Paradise to the earth" (قَالَ اهْبِطَا مِنْهَا جَمِيعًا).

"While you will be enemies to one another" (بَعْضُكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ عَدُوٌّ).

However, I warn you that the path to happiness and salvation remains open to you: "Whenever guidance comes to you from Me, whoever follows My guidance will neither go astray nor be wretched" (فَإِمَّا يَأْتِيَنَّكُمْ مِنِّي هُدًى فَمَنِ اتَّبَعَ هُدَايَ فَلَا يَضِلُّ وَلَا يَشْقَىٰ).

And to clarify the fate of those who forget the command of the Truth, He adds: "And whoever turns away from My remembrance, indeed, he will have a depressed [narrow] life" (وَمَنْ أَعْرَضَ عَنْ ذِكْرِي فَإِنَّ لَهُ مَعِيشَةً ضَنْكًا). "And We will gather him on the Day of Resurrection blind" (وَنَحْشُرُهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ أَعْمَىٰ).

There, "He will say, 'My Lord, why have You raised me blind while I was [once] seeing?'" (قَالَ رَبِّ لِمَ حَشَرْتَنِي أَعْمَىٰ وَقَدْ كُنْتُ بَصِيرًا).

He immediately receives the answer: "It is because Our signs came to you, but you forgot them [ignored them]; and thus you will this Day be forgotten" (قَالَ كَذَٰلِكَ أَتَتْكَ آيَاتُنَا فَنَسِيتَهَا ۖ وَكَذَٰلِكَ الْيَوْمَ تُنْسَىٰ).

And your eyes will be blind to seeing the blessings of your Lord and the station of proximity to Him.

Finally, as a summary and conclusion in the last verse under discussion, He says: "And thus do We recompense he who transgressed and did not believe in the signs of his Lord" (وَكَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِي مَنْ أَسْرَفَ وَلَمْ يُؤْمِنْ بِآيَاتِ رَبِّهِ). "And the punishment of the Hereafter is more severe and more enduring" (وَلَعَذَابُ الْآخِرَةِ أَشَدُّ وَأَبْقَىٰ).

Key Points

1- Negligence of the Remembrance of the Truth and Its Consequences

Sometimes, the doors of life are completely closed to a person; whatever they attempt, they face closed doors. Conversely, sometimes wherever they turn, they find open doors; the prerequisites for every task are provided, and there are no deadlocks or knots before them. The latter state is referred to as "expansiveness of life," while the former is called "distress" or "constriction of livelihood." This is exactly what is meant by "Ma'ishatan Dhanka" (a constricted life) mentioned in the verses above. [^1] Sometimes, the constriction of life is not because one has a low income; perhaps one's wealth and income are enormous, but stinginess, greed, and avarice make life narrow for them. Not only do they have no desire for their door to be open for others to benefit from their life, but it is as if they do not want to open it for themselves either. As Imam Ali عليه السلام said: "He lives like the poor, but will be held accountable like the rich."

Truly, why does man fall into these straits? The Quran says: the primary factor is turning away from the remembrance of the Truth.

The remembrance of Allah is the source of peace for the soul, piety, and courage, while forgetting Him is the cause of anxiety, fear, and worry.

When a person forgets their responsibilities as a result of forgetting the remembrance of Allah, they become drowned in lusts, greed, and avarice. It is evident that their portion will be a constricted life—neither a contentment that fills the soul, nor an attention to spirituality that provides spiritual richness, nor a morality that restrains them against the rebellion of desires.

Fundamentally, the constriction of life is mostly due to spiritual deficiencies and the absence of inner richness; it is due to a lack of confidence in the future, fear of the destruction of existing resources, and excessive attachment to the material world. He who has faith in Allah and has tied his heart to His Pure Essence is safe from all these anxieties.

Of course, thus far the discussion has been about the individual. When we enter societies that have turned away from the remembrance of Allah, the issue becomes even more terrifying. Societies that, despite astonishing industrial progress and the availability of all means of living, exist in intense anxiety and worry; they are caught in a strange bottleneck and see themselves as imprisoned.

Everyone fears one another; no one trusts anyone else. Relationships and bonds are all centered around personal interests. The burden of heavy armaments, due to the fear of war, has swallowed most of their economic resources, and their backs are bent under this heavy load.

Prisons are full of criminals, and every hour and minute, according to their official statistics, horrific murders and crimes occur. Addiction to drugs and immorality has enslaved them. In their family environments, there is neither the light of love nor a refreshing emotional bond. Yes, this is their hard life and constricted livelihood! The former president of America (the country of the Great Satan), Nixon, in his first presidential speech, confessed to this reality and said: "We find ourselves rich in goods, but ragged in spirit; reaching with magnificent precision for the moon, but falling into raucous discord on earth... We are caught in war, wanting peace. We are torn by division, wanting unity. We see empty lives around us, wanting fulfillment, but never being satisfied!" Another of their famous men, whose role in society was supposedly to create happiness for everyone, says: "I see humanity running down a dark alley, at the end of which is nothing but absolute anxiety!" [^1]

Interestingly, we read in Islamic narrations that Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام was asked about the meaning of the verse: "And whoever turns away from My remembrance, indeed, he will have a depressed life." He replied: "It refers to turning away from the Wilayah (guardianship) of the Commander of the Faithful [Ali] عليه السلام." [^2] Yes, he who takes his life model from Ali عليه السلام—that Great Man in whose eyes the entire world was worth less than a leaf of a tree—and attaches his heart to Allah such that the world becomes small in his eyes, whoever he may be, will have an expansive and wide life. But those who forget these models are caught in a constricted life under any circumstances.

In several narrations, turning away from the remembrance of the Truth in the above verse is interpreted as "abandoning the Hajj" for those who are capable. This is because the soul-stirring ceremonies of Hajj create a renewed connection and bond between man and Allah, and this very connection is the path-opener of his life, whereas the opposite causes even greater attachment to material things, which is the source of a constricted life.

2- Inner and Outer Blindness

Two punishments are designated in the above verses for those who turn away from the remembrance of Allah: one is a constricted life in this world, which was mentioned in the previous point, and the other is blindness in the next world.

We have said many times that the world of the Hereafter is a vast and extensive embodiment of the world of this life, and all the realities of this world will be embodied there in a proportionate form. Those whose inner eyes are blind to seeing the truths in this world will also have their physical eyes blind there. Therefore, when they say, "We were seeing before, why were we gathered blind?" they are told it is because you consigned the Divine signs to oblivion (and this state is a reflection of that state).

Here, the question arises that the literal meaning of some Quranic verses is that all people are "seeing" on the Day of Resurrection and are told: "Read your record..." (Al-Isra: 14), or that the criminals see the fire of Hell with their own eyes (Al-Kahf: 53). How is this consistent with a group being blind?

Some great commentators have said that the state of that world is different from this world; perhaps individuals are seeing regarding some matters and blind regarding others! As quoted by the late Tabarsi from some commentators: "They are blind to the directions of goodness and are not guided to any of them," meaning they are blind to what is good, happiness, and blessing, but they are seeing toward what is punishment, evil, and the source of regret and misery, because the system of that world differs from the system of this world.

It is also possible that they are blind in some stages and stations and become seeing in others.

Furthermore, the meaning of the criminals being "forgotten" in the other world is not that Allah forgets them; rather, it is clear that it means treating them with the "treatment of forgetting," just as we have in our daily expressions that if someone ignores another, the latter says: "Why did you forget us?"

3- Transgression (Israf) in Sin

Interestingly, in the above verses, these painful punishments are mentioned for individuals who "transgress" (commit Israf) and do not believe in the signs of Allah.

The expression "Israf" here might refer to the fact that they used God-given blessings like eyes, ears, and intellect in wrong paths, and Israf is nothing other than a person wasting a blessing in vain.

Or it may point to the fact that sinners are of two types: one group has limited sins and a fear of Allah in their hearts—meaning they have not completely severed their connection with the Lord. If, for instance, they commit an injustice, they do not permit it against an orphan or the poor, and at the same time, they consider themselves guilty and ashamed before Allah. Undoubtedly, such a person is a sinner and deserves punishment, but they are very different from someone who sins without measure, recognizes no limits or conditions for sin, and sometimes takes pride in committing sin or considers sin to be trivial. This is because the first group may eventually repent and make amends, but those who transgress (commit Israf) in sin do not.

4- What is "Hubut" (Descent)?

"Hubut" literally means a forced coming down, like a stone falling from a height. When used regarding a human, it means being driven down as a punishment.

Given that Adam was created for life on earth and Paradise was a lush and blessed region of this very world, the descent (Hubut) of Adam here means a descent in status, not location. That is, Allah lowered his station because of his "leaving the better option" (Tark al-Awla), deprived him of all those Paradisiacal blessings, and subjected him to the hardships of this world.

It is noteworthy that the addressee here is mentioned in the dual form (اهْبِطَا - "descend both of you"), which might refer to Adam and Eve. If in some other verses of the Quran "ihbitu" is mentioned in the plural form, it is because Satan was also included in this command, as he too was driven out of Paradise.

It is also possible that the addressees are Adam and Satan, because in the following sentence it says: "بَعْضُكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ عَدُوٌّ" (some of you will be enemies to others).

Some commentators have also said that the phrase "بَعْضُكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ عَدُوٌّ," which is in the plural form, means that enmity was established between Adam and Eve on one side and Satan on the other, or between Adam and his children on one side and Satan and his progeny on the other.

However, in any case, the addressee in the sentence "فَإِمَّا يَأْتِيَنَّكُمْ مِنِّي هُدًى" (whenever guidance comes to you from Me) is certainly the children of Adam and Eve, because Divine guidance is specific to them. Satan and his progeny, who have separated themselves from the program of Divine guidance, are not included in this address.

[^1]: "Dhank" means hardship and constriction; this word is always used in the singular and does not have dual, plural, or feminine forms. [^1]: The Mystery of Existence, pages 50-51. [^2]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, page 405.

Ayahs

أَفَلَمْ يَهْدِ لَهُمْ كَمْ أَهْلَكْنَا قَبْلَهُم مِّنَ ٱلْقُرُونِ يَمْشُونَ فِى مَسَـٰكِنِهِمْ ۗ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّأُو۟لِى ٱلنُّهَىٰ (١٢٨)
Does it not dawn upon them how many generations We have destroyed before them, amid [the ruins of] whose dwellings they walk? There are indeed signs in this for those who have reason. (128)
وَلَوْلَا كَلِمَةٌ سَبَقَتْ مِن رَّبِّكَ لَكَانَ لِزَامًا وَأَجَلٌ مُّسَمًّى (١٢٩)
Were it not for a prior decree of your Lord and a specified time, [their doom] would have attended [their guilt]. (129)
فَٱصْبِرْ عَلَىٰ مَا يَقُولُونَ وَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّكَ قَبْلَ طُلُوعِ ٱلشَّمْسِ وَقَبْلَ غُرُوبِهَا ۖ وَمِنْ ءَانَآئِ ٱلَّيْلِ فَسَبِّحْ وَأَطْرَافَ ٱلنَّهَارِ لَعَلَّكَ تَرْضَىٰ (١٣٠)
So be patient with what they say, and celebrate the praise of your Lord before the rising of the sun and before the sunset, and glorify Him in watches of the night and at the day’s ends, that you may be pleased. (130)

Commentary

Take Lesson from the History of Predecessors

Since the preceding verses contained extensive discussions regarding the criminals, the first of the verses under discussion points to one of the best and most effective ways of awakening—which is the study of the history of those who came before—stating: "Is it not a guidance for them [to know] how many generations We destroyed before them" (أَ فَلَمْ يَهْدِ لَهُمْ كَمْ أَهْلَكْنا قَبْلَهُمْ مِنَ الْقُرُونِ‌) [^1]; those very people who were seized by the painful divine punishment, and "they [the current generation] walk among their [ruined] dwellings" (يَمْشُونَ فِي مَساكِنِهِمْ‌).

In their travels, they pass by the houses of the people of 'Ad (during journeys to Yemen), the ruined dwellings of the people of Thamud (during journeys to Sham/Syria), and the overturned homes of the people of Lot (during journeys to Palestine). They see their remains but do not take a lesson—ruins that, with a silent tongue, recount the painful stories of the past and warn the people of today and the future, crying out and explaining the ultimate end of injustice, disbelief, and corruption.

Indeed, "In this are clear signs and many proofs for those possessors of intellect and awakened thought" (إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآياتٍ لِأُولِي النُّهى‌) [^2]. The subject of taking lessons from the history of predecessors is among the matters upon which the Quran and Islamic Hadiths have placed great emphasis. Truly, it is an awakening teacher; how many individuals there are who are not admonished by any sermon, yet seeing scenes of the exemplary remains of the past shakes them and often transforms the course of their lives.

[^1]: As we have mentioned previously, "Qurun" (قرون) is the plural of "Qarn" (قرن), meaning a people living in one era, and sometimes the time itself is called a century/Qarn (derived from the root muqaranah - coexistence/conjunction).

[^2]: "Nuha" (نهى) from the root "Nahy" (forbidding) here means "intellect," because the intellect forbids a human from ugliness and evils.

In a Hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, we read:

"The most heedless of people is he who does not take a lesson from the changing of the world from one state to another" [^1] and does not reflect upon the turning of the pages of night and day.

The next verse is, in fact, an answer to a question that arises here: why does Allah not arrange for this group the same program He arranged for the previous criminals? The Quran says: "And had it not been for a word that preceded from your Lord and a term determined, [the punishment] would have been a necessity" (وَ لَوْ لا كَلِمَةٌ سَبَقَتْ مِنْ رَبِّكَ لَكانَ لِزاماً وَ أَجَلٌ مُسَمًّى‌).

This divine Sunnah (tradition), referred to in the Quran in numerous instances as a "Word" (Kalimah), is an allusion to the decree of creation regarding the freedom of human beings. For if every criminal were punished immediately and without any respite, faith and righteous deeds would take on an almost compulsory and forced nature, driven mostly by fear and terror of immediate punishment. Consequently, they would not serve as a means of perfection, which is the primary goal.

Furthermore, if it were decreed that all criminals be punished immediately, no one would remain alive on the face of the earth: "And if Allah were to punish men for their wrongdoing, He would not leave on it [the earth] a single creature" (An-Nahl: 61).

Therefore, there must be a respite so that the sinners may come to their senses and take the path of reform, and so that an opportunity for self-building is given to all seekers of the path of Truth.

The expression "Ajalun Musamma" (أَجَلٌ مُسَمًّى‌ - a determined term), as understood from the collection of Quranic verses, refers to the certain time of the end of a human's life. [^1] In any case, the faithless oppressors and audacious criminals must not be deceived by the delay of divine punishment, nor should they ignore the reality that this grace of God, this divine Sunnah, and this law of perfection is what has opened the field for them.

Then, addressing the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it says: "Now that it is not intended for these evildoers to be punished immediately, be patient and steadfast against what they say" (فَاصْبِرْ عَلى‌ ما يَقُولُونَ‌).

And to strengthen the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله morale and to comfort his heart, He commands him to engage in intimate supplication with God, prayer, and glorification, saying: "And glorify with the praise of your Lord before the rising of the sun and before its setting, and during the hours of the night, and at the ends of the day, that you may be satisfied" and your heart may not be distressed by their painful words (وَ سَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّكَ قَبْلَ طُلُوعِ الشَّمْسِ وَ قَبْلَ غُرُوبِها وَ مِنْ آناءِ اللَّيْلِ فَسَبِّحْ وَ أَطْرافَ النَّهارِ لَعَلَّكَ تَرْضى‌).

Undoubtedly, this praise and glorification is a struggle against polytheism and idol-worship, while simultaneously being an act of patience and endurance against the slanders and unseemly words of the polytheists.

However, there is a discussion among commentators as to whether absolute praise and glorification are intended, or specifically the five daily prayers. A group believes that the literal expression should be left in its broad meaning to imply absolute glorification and praise. Meanwhile, another group considers it an allusion to the five prayers, in this order:

"Before the rising of the sun" refers to the Fajr (Dawn) prayer.

"And before its setting" refers to the 'Asr (Afternoon) prayer (or an allusion to the Zuhr and 'Asr prayers whose time continues until sunset).

"During the hours of the night" refers to the Maghrib and 'Isha prayers, as well as the Night Prayer (Salat al-Layl).

As for the expression "ends of the day" (أَطْرافَ النَّهارِ), it is either an allusion to the Zuhr (Noon) prayer—since "Atraf" is the plural of "Taraf" meaning side, and if we divide the day into two halves, the Zuhr prayer is located on one side of the second half.

It is also understood from some narrations that "ends of the day" refers to the recommended (Mustahabb) prayers that a person can perform at various times of the day, because "ends of the day" here stands in contrast to "hours of the night" and encompasses all hours of the day. (Especially considering that "Atraf" is plural while the day has only two ends, it becomes clear that "Atraf" has a broad meaning covering various hours of the day).

There is a third possibility in the interpretation of the verse: that it refers to specific Dhikr (remembrances) mentioned in Islamic narrations for these specific hours. For example, in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام regarding the interpretation of the above verse, we read that the Imam عليه السلام said: "It is necessary for every Muslim to say this Dhikr ten times before the rising of the sun and before its setting:

'There is no god but Allah, alone, without partner; to Him belongs the kingdom and to Him belongs the praise; He gives life and He causes death, and He is the Ever-Living who does not die; in His hand is all good, and He is powerful over all things.'"

In any case, these interpretations are not mutually exclusive. It is possible that the verse refers to both the glorifications and the obligatory and recommended prayers during the day and night. Thus, there will be no contradiction among the narrations received in this regard, as some narrations interpret it as specific Dhikr and others as prayer.

It is also necessary to mention that the phrase "that you may be satisfied" (لَعَلَّكَ تَرْضى‌) is actually the result of praising and glorifying the Lord and being patient against their words. This is because this praise, glorification, and the day and night prayers strengthen the human's connection with God to such an extent that he thinks of nothing but Him, does not fear difficult events, and with such a firm support, does not dread enemies. In this way, peace and tranquility of the soul and spirit fill him.

The use of "La'alla" (perhaps/so that) may refer to the same point we have mentioned previously in the interpretation of this word: that "La'alla" usually points to the conditions necessary to achieve a result. For instance, prayer and the remembrance of God bring such peace on the condition that they are performed with presence of heart and complete etiquette.

Furthermore, although the addressee in this verse is the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, evidence indicates that this ruling is general in nature.

[^1]: Safinat al-Bihar (under the root 'abar), Vol. 2, p. 146.

[^1]: For further explanation, please refer to the detailed discussion we provided in Volume 5 under verses 1 and 2 of Surah Al-An'am (page 148). Additionally, from a grammatical perspective, "Ajalun Musamma" is conjoined (atf) to "Kalimah."

Ayahs

وَلَا تَمُدَّنَّ عَيْنَيْكَ إِلَىٰ مَا مَتَّعْنَا بِهِۦٓ أَزْوَٰجًا مِّنْهُمْ زَهْرَةَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا لِنَفْتِنَهُمْ فِيهِ ۚ وَرِزْقُ رَبِّكَ خَيْرٌ وَأَبْقَىٰ (١٣١)
Do not extend your glance toward what We have provided certain groups of them as a glitter of the life of this world, so that We may test them thereby. The provision of your Lord is better and more lasting. (131)
وَأْمُرْ أَهْلَكَ بِٱلصَّلَوٰةِ وَٱصْطَبِرْ عَلَيْهَا ۖ لَا نَسْـَٔلُكَ رِزْقًا ۖ نَّحْنُ نَرْزُقُكَ ۗ وَٱلْعَـٰقِبَةُ لِلتَّقْوَىٰ (١٣٢)
And bid your family to prayer and be steadfast in maintaining it. We do not ask any provision of you: it is We who provide for you, and to Godwariness belongs the ultimate outcome [in the Hereafter]. (132)
وَقَالُوا۟ لَوْلَا يَأْتِينَا بِـَٔايَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّهِۦٓ ۚ أَوَلَمْ تَأْتِهِم بَيِّنَةُ مَا فِى ٱلصُّحُفِ ٱلْأُولَىٰ (١٣٣)
They say, ‘Why does he not bring us a sign from his Lord?’ Has there not come to them a clear proof in that which is in the former scriptures? (133)
وَلَوْ أَنَّآ أَهْلَكْنَـٰهُم بِعَذَابٍ مِّن قَبْلِهِۦ لَقَالُوا۟ رَبَّنَا لَوْلَآ أَرْسَلْتَ إِلَيْنَا رَسُولًا فَنَتَّبِعَ ءَايَـٰتِكَ مِن قَبْلِ أَن نَّذِلَّ وَنَخْزَىٰ (١٣٤)
Had We destroyed them with a punishment before it, they would have surely said, ‘Our Lord! Why did You not send us an apostle so that we might have followed Your signs before we were abased and disgraced?’ (134)
قُلْ كُلٌّ مُّتَرَبِّصٌ فَتَرَبَّصُوا۟ ۖ فَسَتَعْلَمُونَ مَنْ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلصِّرَٰطِ ٱلسَّوِىِّ وَمَنِ ٱهْتَدَىٰ (١٣٥)
Say, ‘Everyone [of us] is waiting. So wait! Soon you will know who are the people of the right path, and who is [rightly] guided.’ (135)

Commentary

In these verses, instructions are given to the Prophet ﷺ which, in reality, are intended for all Muslims, serving as a completion to the discussion regarding "patience" that we read in the preceding verses.

"First, He says: never cast your eyes toward the material blessings we have bestowed upon groups of them (the disbelievers and opponents)" (وَ لا تَمُدَّنَّ عَيْنَيْكَ إِلى‌ ما مَتَّعْنا بِهِ أَزْواجاً مِنْهُمْ‌).

Indeed, "these unstable blessings are but the blossoms of the life of this world" (زَهْرَةَ الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا).

Blossoms that bloom quickly, then wither, petal away, and fall to the ground, lasting no more than a few short days.

At the same time, "all of this is so that We may test them therewith" (لِنَفْتِنَهُمْ فِيهِ‌).

In any case, "that which your Lord has provided for you is better and more enduring" (وَ رِزْقُ رَبِّكَ خَيْرٌ وَ أَبْقى‌).

Allah has granted you various types of favors and blessings: faith and Islam, the Quran and divine verses, pure and lawful sustenance, and ultimately the eternal blessings of the Hereafter; these provisions are lasting and everlasting.

In the next verse, to soothe the spirit of the Prophet ﷺ and strengthen his heart, He says: "Enjoin prayer upon your family and be patient and steadfast in its performance" (وَ أْمُرْ أَهْلَكَ بِالصَّلاةِ وَ اصْطَبِرْ عَلَيْها).

For this prayer is a source of purity and clarity of heart, spiritual strength, and the permanence of Allah's remembrance for you and your family.

Undoubtedly, the literal meaning of "family" (أهل) here refers to the Prophet's ﷺ household in general. However, since this Surah was revealed in Mecca, at that time the primary examples of his family were Khadijah and Ali عليه السلام, and it may have included some other close relatives of the Prophet ﷺ. But as time passed, the scope of the Prophet's family expanded.

Then He adds that if the command for prayer has been given to you and your family, its benefits and blessings are directed only toward yourselves: "We do not ask you for provision; rather, We provide for you" (لا نَسْئَلُكَ رِزْقاً نَحْنُ نَرْزُقُكَ‌).

This prayer adds nothing to the greatness of the Lord; rather, it is a great asset for the perfection of you human beings and a high-level class of self-edification.

Or, in other words, Allah is not like kings and rulers who took tribute from their nations to manage their own lives and those of their associates. Allah is independent of all, and all are in need of Him.

In fact, this expression is similar to what appears in Surah Adh-Dhariyat, verses 56-58: "وَمَا خَلَقْتُ الْجِنَّ وَالْإِنْسَ إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُونِ مَا أُرِيدُ مِنْهُمْ مِنْ رِزْقٍ وَمَا أُرِيدُ أَنْ يُطْعِمُونِ إِنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ الرَّزَّاقُ ذُو الْقُوَّةِ الْمَتِينُ": "I did not create the jinn and mankind except to worship Me. I do not seek from them any provision, nor do I ask that they should feed Me. Indeed, Allah is the Provider, the Possessor of Power, the Firm."

Thus, the result of worship returns directly to the worshippers themselves.

And at the end of the verse, He adds: "The good end and outcome belongs to Taqwa (piety)" (وَ الْعاقِبَةُ لِلتَّقْوى‌).

That which remains and whose end is beneficial, constructive, and life-giving is Taqwa and righteousness. The pious are ultimately victorious, and the impious are doomed to failure.

There is also a possibility in the interpretation of this final phrase that its goal is to emphasize the spirit of Taqwa and sincerity in worship, for that is the foundation of worship. In verse 37 of Surah Al-Hajj, we read: "لَنْ يَنالَ اللَّهَ لُحُومُها وَ لا دِماؤُها وَ لكِنْ يَنالُهُ التَّقْوى‌ مِنْكُمْ‌": "Neither their meat nor their blood reaches Allah, but it is the Taqwa from you that reaches Him." What reaches the station of His proximity from your deeds is not the shell and appearance, but the core, the essence, and the sincerity within it that finds its way to His proximity.

The next verse refers to one of the excuses made by the disbelievers, saying: "They said, 'Why does the Prophet not bring us a miracle from his Lord—as we desire?'" (وَ قالُوا لَوْ لا يَأْتِينا بِآيَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّهِ‌). He immediately answers them: "Has there not come to them the clear evidence of what was in the former scriptures?" (أَ وَ لَمْ تَأْتِهِمْ بَيِّنَةُ ما فِي الصُّحُفِ الْأُولى‌). (Meaning: those who repeatedly sought excuses for miracles and, after witnessing them, continued in disbelief and denial, until the severe divine punishment overtook them. Do they not know that if they follow the same path, the same fate awaits them?)

There is also a possibility in the interpretation of the verse that "clear evidence" (بينة) refers to the Quran itself, which explains the truths of previous scriptures at a higher level. The above verse says: Why do they seek miracles and make excuses? Is not this Quran, with these great distinctions and containing the truths of previous scriptures, sufficient for them?

Another interpretation has also been mentioned for this verse: that the Prophet of Islam ﷺ, despite being unschooled, brought such a clear and manifest book that is consistent with what was in the texts of previous scriptures, and this itself is a sign of its miraculous nature. Furthermore, the attributes of the Prophet ﷺ and his Book perfectly match the signs mentioned in previous scriptures, and this is proof of his truthfulness. [1]

In any case, these excuse-seekers are not people seeking the truth; rather, they are constantly thinking of a new pretext. Even "if We had destroyed them with a punishment before this (the revelation of the Quran and the coming of the Prophet of Islam), they would have said on the Day of Resurrection: 'Our Lord, why did You not send us a messenger so we could follow Your verses before we were humbled and disgraced?'"

[1]: The first interpretation is in Majma' al-Bayan, the second in Fi Zilal, and the third was brought by Fakhr Razi in his Tafsir al-Kabir. These interpretations, though different, are not contradictory, especially the second and third.

(وَ لَوْ أَنَّا أَهْلَكْناهُمْ بِعَذابٍ مِنْ قَبْلِهِ لَقالُوا رَبَّنا لَوْ لا أَرْسَلْتَ إِلَيْنا رَسُولًا فَنَتَّبِعَ آياتِكَ مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ نَذِلَّ وَ نَخْزى‌).

But now that this great Prophet ﷺ has come to them with this magnificent Book, they say something new every day and devise an excuse to flee from the truth.

Warn them "and say: 'Each of us is waiting'" (قُلْ كُلٌّ مُتَرَبِّصٌ‌). We are waiting for the divine promises regarding you, and you are waiting for difficulties and calamities to overtake us.

"Now that it is so, then wait!" (فَتَرَبَّصُوا).

"But soon you will know who are the people of the straight path and the true religion, and who are those guided to the station of truth and eternal divine blessing" (فَسَتَعْلَمُونَ مَنْ أَصْحابُ الصِّراطِ السَّوِيِّ وَ مَنِ اهْتَدى‌).

And with this decisive and meaningful sentence, He ends the dialogue with these stubborn and excuse-seeking deniers here.

In summary, since this Surah was revealed in "Mecca," and at that time the Prophet ﷺ and the Muslims were under severe pressure from the enemies, Allah comforts them at the end of this Surah. Sometimes He says: do not let their wealth and riches—which are the fleeting assets of this world and for the purpose of testing—attract your attention.

And sometimes He commands prayer and steadfastness to strengthen their spiritual power against the multitude of enemies.

Finally, He gives glad tidings to the Muslims that if this group does not believe, they have a dark and ominous fate which they must await.

O Lord! Place us among the guided ones and the companions of the Straight Path. O Allah! Grant us that strength and courage so that we neither fear the multitude of enemies nor fall into dread from hard events and difficulties.

Take away from us the spirit of stubbornness and excuse-seeking, and grant us the success of accepting the truth.

End of Surah Ta-Ha. Thursday, 20th of Jumada al-Thani (the blessed birthday of the Lady of Islam, Fatima al-Zahra عليه السلام), the year 1402 AH.
